deck.title: CCNP ENCOR 350-401 Memory Tables B
deck.description:
Exam version: ccnp-encor-350-401-v1.2
Memory Tables B

front.text: Define the following EIGRP terms: Successor, Feasible Distance (FD), Reported Distance (RD), Feasibility Condition, and Feasible Successor.

rows: The route with the lowest path metric to reach a destination (the best/active route installed in the routing table).
The metric value for the lowest-metric path to reach a destination, calculated locally by the router.
The distance reported by a neighbor to reach a prefix. The RD value is the FD of the advertising router.
A loop-prevention rule: a route is loop-free if the neighbor's RD is less than the local FD.
A backup route that satisfies the feasibility condition (RD < FD), guaranteeing it is loop free.

back.text: Example: If R1's FD to 10.4.4.0/24 via R3→R4 is 3328, and R4 advertises the prefix to R1 with an RD of 2816, the R1→R4 path is a feasible successor because 2816 < 3328.

front.text: List the five EIGRP packet types by opcode value and describe the function of each.

rows: Used to transmit routing and reachability information with other EIGRP neighbors.
Used to request specific routing information from a neighbor.
Sent during the DUAL process when a route is lost and no feasible successor exists, asking neighbors for alternate paths.
Sent in response to a Query, indicating whether the neighbor has an alternate path.
Used for discovery of EIGRP neighbors and for detecting when a neighbor is no longer available.

front.text: Name the five OSPF packet types (by Type number) and describe the purpose of each.

rows: Discover and maintain neighbors; sent periodically on OSPF interfaces.
Summarize LSDB contents during initial adjacency formation.
Request specific LSAs the local router believes are stale or missing.
Carry one or more LSAs as a database update, often in response to an LSR.
Acknowledge received LSAs to provide reliable flooding.

front.text: Compare the OSPF network types: Broadcast, Non-Broadcast, Point-to-Point, Point-to-Multipoint, and Loopback. Include DR/BDR usage and timers.

rows: Frame Relay main interface or multipoint subinterfaces
Not enabled by default; advertises /32 host route, used for hub-and-spoke

front.text: What are the IANA-assigned multicast address ranges for the Local Network Control block, Internetwork Control block, SSM block, and Administratively Scoped block?
Match these well-known multicast addresses to their purpose: 224.0.0.13, 224.0.0.22, 224.0.1.39, 224.0.1.40.
What are the IGMPv2 message types for type values 0x16 and 0x11 (general query)?

items: 0x16 — Version 2 Membership Report (commonly called an IGMP join). Used by receivers to join a multicast group or respond to a router's membership query.
0x11 — General Membership Query. Sent periodically to 224.0.0.1 to discover receivers in the attached subnet; group address field set to 0.0.0.0.

front.text: Besides the Type field, what are the other three fields in an IGMP message and what does each carry?

items: Max Response Time — Set only in general/group-specific query messages (0x11). Specifies the maximum allowed time before a responding report, in units of 1/10 second. Set to 0x00 in other messages.
Checksum — 16-bit 1s complement of the 1s complement sum of the IGMP message; standard TCP/IP checksum.
Group Address — 0.0.0.0 in general queries; set to the group address in group-specific queries, membership reports, and leave messages.

front.text: Define the following PIM terms: RPF interface, RPF neighbor, Upstream, and Downstream.

items: RPF interface — The interface used to reach the source of the multicast tree, determined by performing a unicast routing lookup against the source (or RP) IP. Multicast traffic is only accepted on this interface.
RPF neighbor — The next-hop router on the RPF interface (toward the source or RP); PIM joins are sent to this neighbor.
Upstream — Direction toward the source of the tree (actual source for source trees, or RP for shared trees). PIM joins travel upstream.
Downstream — Direction away from the source and toward the receivers.

front.text: What are a First-Hop Router (FHR) and a Last-Hop Router (LHR) in a PIM multicast network?

items: First-Hop Router (FHR) — The router directly connected to the multicast source. It encapsulates source traffic in PIM Register messages to the RP in PIM-SM.
Last-Hop Router (LHR) — The router directly connected to the receivers. It is responsible for sending PIM joins toward the RP or source on behalf of receivers.

front.text: What is the difference between the MRIB, the MFIB, and multicast state?

items: MRIB (Multicast Routing Information Base) — Topology/control-plane table (the mroute table) derived from the unicast RIB and PIM. Contains (S, G), IIF, OIF list, RPF neighbor, and related info.
MFIB (Multicast Forwarding Information Base) — Forwarding/data-plane table programmed from MRIB, used for fast hardware multicast forwarding.
Multicast state — The actual forwarding state used to forward multicast traffic, made up of mroute entries (S, G, IIF, OIF, etc.).

front.text: List the five PIM operating modes.

items: PIM Dense Mode (PIM-DM) — Flood and prune; assumes receivers everywhere.
PIM Sparse Mode (PIM-SM) — Explicit join model using a Rendezvous Point (RP).
PIM Sparse-Dense Mode — Per-group choice; sparse if RP is known, dense otherwise.
Bidirectional PIM (Bidir-PIM) — Single shared bidirectional tree; no (S, G) state.
Source-Specific Multicast (SSM) — Receivers specify both source and group; no RP required (uses 232.0.0.0/8).

front.text: Identify the PIM control message types for type values 0, 3, and 8, and the destination they are sent to.
What are the three QoS implementation models and how do they differ?

items: Best Effort — QoS is not enabled. Used for traffic that doesn't require special treatment.
IntServ (Integrated Services) — Applications signal the network (e.g., RSVP) to reserve bandwidth and request specific QoS treatment.
DiffServ (Differentiated Services) — The network identifies traffic classes that require special QoS treatment using markings such as DSCP.

front.text: What traffic descriptors are used at Layer 2, Layer 3, Layer 4, and Layer 7 for QoS classification?

items: Layer 2 — MAC address and 802.1Q/p Class of Service (CoS) bits.
Layer 3 — IP Precedence (IPP), DSCP, and source/destination IP address.
Layer 4 — TCP or UDP ports.
Layer 7 — Application signatures (e.g., NBAR/NBAR2).

front.text: Which marking fields are used at Layer 2 and Layer 3 for QoS?

items: Layer 2 — 802.1Q/p Class of Service (CoS) bits (3 bits in the TCI field).
Layer 3 — Differentiated Services Code Points (DSCP) and IP Precedence (IPP) in the IP header ToS byte.

front.text: The 802.1Q Tag Control Information (TCI) field is 16 bits. What are its three sub-fields and their sizes?

items: Priority Code Point (PCP) — 3 bits; carries the CoS marking.
Drop Eligible Indicator (DEI) — 1 bit; indicates frames that may be dropped first under congestion.
VLAN Identifier (VLAN ID) — 12 bits; identifies the VLAN.

front.text: Name the four DiffServ PHBs and describe each.

items: Class Selector (CS) — Uses the first 3 DSCP bits as CS bits, providing backward compatibility with IP Precedence.
Default Forwarding (DF) — Used for best-effort service (DSCP 0).
Assured Forwarding (AF) — Used for guaranteed bandwidth service with drop precedence (e.g., AF11–AF43).
Expedited Forwarding (EF) — Used for low-latency, low-jitter, low-loss real-time traffic such as voice (DSCP 46).

front.text: Define CIR, Tc, Bc, and Token in the context of policing and shaping.

items: CIR (Committed Information Rate) — The rate, in bits per second, that the policer or shaper is configured to enforce as the long-term average.
Tc (Time Committed Interval) — The interval (ms) over which the committed burst (Bc) is sent. Tc = (Bc / CIR) × 1000.
Bc (Committed Burst) — Maximum size of the CIR token bucket in bytes; the maximum amount of traffic allowed in one Tc. Bc = CIR × (Tc / 1000).
Token — A virtual unit (representing bits) that must be available in the bucket to forward a packet; one byte = 8 bits/tokens.

front.text: What are the three common policing algorithms used by Cisco IOS?

items: Single-rate, two-color policer — Single token bucket with conform/exceed actions.
Single-rate, three-color policer (srTCM, RFC 2697) — Adds a Be (excess burst) bucket; conform/exceed/violate actions.
Two-rate, three-color policer (trTCM, RFC 2698) — Two rates (CIR and PIR) and two buckets; conform/exceed/violate actions.

front.text: Briefly describe FIFO, Round Robin, WRR, CQ, PQ, and WFQ legacy queuing algorithms.

rows: Single queue; first packet in is first packet out. All traffic is one class.
Multiple queues serviced one packet at a time in sequence. No starvation, but no prioritization.
Round robin with weights so each queue gets a configurable share of bandwidth.
Cisco WRR implementation with 16 queues, FIFO inside each. Configurable bandwidth per queue.
Four strict-priority queues (high/medium/normal/low). Lower queues only serviced when higher ones are empty — risk of starvation.
Auto-divides bandwidth among flows weighted by IP Precedence; fair, but no fixed-bandwidth guarantee.

front.text: Compare CBWFQ and LLQ. Which is suitable for real-time voice and why?

items: CBWFQ (Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing) — Up to 256 user-defined classes, each with a guaranteed minimum bandwidth during congestion. Provides bandwidth guarantees but no latency guarantee, so it is not suitable alone for real-time voice/video.

LLQ (Low Latency Queuing) — CBWFQ + a strict-priority queue (PQ). The priority queue is serviced first up to its configured bandwidth, providing both latency and bandwidth guarantees for real-time traffic. A built-in policer prevents the priority queue from starving other classes.
Voice traffic should use LLQ's priority queue because it requires both bounded delay/jitter and guaranteed bandwidth.

front.text: What are the four security services provided by IPsec, and what method or algorithm provides each?

rows: Verifies the identity of the VPN peer.
Protects data from eavesdropping; converts plaintext to ciphertext.
Unique sequence number per packet, tracked by the peer

front.text: Define DES, 3DES, AES, MD5, SHA, DH, RSA, and PSK in the context of IPsec.

items: DES — 56-bit symmetric encryption; very weak, avoid.
3DES — Runs DES three times with three 56-bit keys; deprecated, use AES.
AES — Symmetric encryption with 128/192/256-bit keys, based on Rijndael; preferred.
MD5 — One-way 128-bit hash for data authentication (HMAC-MD5); deprecated.
SHA — One-way 160-bit hash (SHA-1 HMAC); used for data authentication.
DH (Diffie-Hellman) — Asymmetric key-exchange protocol that establishes shared symmetric keys; avoid groups 1/2/5, use 14+.
RSA — Public-key (digital certificate) cryptosystem used to mutually authenticate peers.
PSK (Pre-Shared Key) — A locally configured key used as a credential for mutual peer authentication.

front.text: Compare IKEv1 and IKEv2 in terms of exchange modes, minimum messages to establish IPsec SAs, and authentication.

rows: Main Mode, Aggressive Mode, Quick Mode
Min messages to establish IPsec SAs
PSK, RSA-SIG, Public key — both peers must use the same method
PSK, RSA-SIG; supports asymmetric authentication (each peer can use a different method)
MitM, eavesdropping, plus DoS protection (cookie challenge)

front.text: What are the two methods to encrypt traffic over a GRE tunnel?

items: GRE over IPsec — A GRE tunnel is built first and then protected by an IPsec crypto map or IPsec profile (typically transport mode).
IPsec over GRE — IPsec protects the inner traffic before it is encapsulated into GRE (less common; used in specific designs).

front.text: Define the following LISP components: EID, RLOC, ITR, ETR, xTR, MS, MR, and MS/MR.

items: EID (Endpoint Identifier) — IP address of an endpoint inside a LISP site (IPv4 or IPv6).
RLOC (Routing Locator) — IPv4/IPv6 address of an ETR's Internet- or core-facing interface.
ITR (Ingress Tunnel Router) — LISP router that LISP-encapsulates packets from EIDs going to remote LISP sites.
ETR (Egress Tunnel Router) — LISP router that de-encapsulates LISP packets destined to local EIDs.
xTR — Router performing both ITR and ETR functions (most LISP routers).
MS (Map Server) — Learns EID-to-RLOC mappings from ETRs and stores them in the mapping database.
MR (Map Resolver) — Receives map requests from ITRs and consults the MS to find the appropriate ETR.
MS/MR — A single device performing both Map Server and Map Resolver functions.

front.text: What are PITRs, PETRs, and PxTRs in LISP?

items: PITR (Proxy ITR) — Acts like an ITR but for non-LISP sites that send traffic to LISP EID destinations.
PETR (Proxy ETR) — Acts like an ETR but for LISP EIDs sending traffic to destinations at non-LISP sites.
PxTR — A router that performs both PITR and PETR functions.

front.text: A VTEP has two interfaces. What are they and what does each do?

items: Local LAN interfaces — Connect to local hosts and provide bridging between them on the local LAN segment.
IP (core-facing) interface — Used to reach the underlay Layer 3 network. Its IP address identifies the VTEP and is used for VXLAN encapsulation/de-encapsulation.

front.text: Name the four VXLAN control and data planes supported by Cisco devices.

items: VXLAN with multicast underlay (flood-and-learn).
VXLAN with static unicast (flood-and-learn) underlay.
VXLAN with MP-BGP EVPN control plane.
VXLAN with LISP control plane (used by Cisco SD-Access).

front.text: Compare the 802.11 amendments: 11b, 11a, 11g, 11n, 11ac, 11ax — band(s), max data rate, and channel widths.

rows: Up to 150 Mbps per spatial stream, up to 4 streams
Up to 866 Mbps per spatial stream, up to 4 streams
Up to ~1.2 Gbps per spatial stream, up to 8 streams

front.text: What are the roles of the access, distribution, and core layers in a hierarchical LAN design?

items: Access layer — Provides endpoint connectivity (wired/wireless), PoE, port security, and first-hop services for users and devices.
Distribution layer — Aggregates access-layer switches, performs Layer 3 routing, applies policy (ACLs, QoS), and provides redundancy and fast convergence.
Core layer (also called the backbone) — High-speed, low-latency Layer 3 transport between distribution blocks; configuration is kept simple to maximize forwarding performance and stability.

front.text: Name three core capabilities SD-Access brings to a campus network.

items: Automation — Single point of automation, orchestration, and management via Cisco DNA Center; replaces manual device configurations.
Assurance/Analytics — Telemetry-based proactive monitoring and prediction of network and security risks, including encrypted traffic.
Mobility — Seamless host mobility for both wired and wireless clients.
Identity-based Policy — ISE-driven user/device identity for SGACL-based group policies instead of IP/ACL-based rules.
Segmentation/Virtual Networks — One physical infrastructure supports multiple VRFs (virtual networks) for guest, IoT, corporate, etc.

front.text: What are the three basic planes of operation in the SD-Access fabric, and what protocol provides each?

items: Control plane — Built on LISP; maintains EID-to-RLOC mappings for endpoints.
Data plane — Built on VXLAN; encapsulates traffic between fabric edges over the underlay.
Policy plane — Built on Cisco TrustSec (SGTs/SGACLs); carries identity and group-based policy.

front.text: List the five basic device roles in the SD-Access fabric overlay.

items: Control plane node — Holds the LISP map server/resolver and EID-to-RLOC mappings.
Border node — Connects external Layer 3 networks (firewalls, WAN, data center) to the fabric.
Edge node — Connects wired endpoints to the fabric (typically access/distribution switches).
Wireless controller / Fabric WLC — Connects APs and wireless endpoints to the fabric.
Intermediate node — Underlay-only routers/switches with no fabric overlay role.

front.text: What are the three types of SD-Access border nodes?

items: Internal border — Connects only to known areas of the organization (e.g., WLC, firewall, data center).
Default border — Connects only to unknown areas outside the organization; configured with a default route for the Internet/public cloud.
Anywhere/Internal+Default border — Combines both functions; connects to known internal networks and to external/transit networks.

front.text: List the four main Cisco SD-WAN components plus the optional analytics service, and describe each.

items: WAN Edge (vEdge / cEdge) — Physical or virtual devices forwarding traffic across WAN transports between sites (data plane).
vManage — SD-WAN management plane; single pane of glass GUI for configuration, monitoring, and orchestration.
vSmart — SD-WAN control plane; advertises routes (OMP) and data policies to edge devices.
vBond — SD-WAN orchestration; authenticates devices and orchestrates initial connectivity between edges, vManage, and vSmart.
vAnalytics (optional) — Cloud-based analytics and assurance service.

front.text: Per Gartner, what capabilities must a Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) include beyond traditional firewall functions?

items: Standard stateful firewall capabilities (e.g., stateful inspection, NAT, VPN).
Application awareness and control (Layer 7 visibility and policy).
Integrated Intrusion Prevention System (IPS).
Threat intelligence feeds and advanced malware protection (AMP) to block advanced/zero-day threats.

front.text: What are the three core components of Cisco Secure Network Analytics (formerly Stealthwatch) and what does each do?

items: Network Analytics Manager (Management Console) — Central console that aggregates and analyzes data from up to 25 Flow Collectors plus ISE; provides reports, dashboards, and security/network intelligence.
Flow Collector — Collects and analyzes telemetry (NetFlow, IPFIX, proxy data) from network devices and endpoints; integrates with ETA to detect threats in encrypted traffic without decryption.
Flow Rate License — Required to collect, manage, and analyze flow telemetry; defines the volume of flows that can be collected.

front.text: What are the two deployment models for Cisco Secure Cloud Analytics?

items: Public cloud monitoring — Monitors public cloud environments (AWS, Azure, GCP) using cloud-native telemetry (e.g., VPC flow logs).
Private network monitoring — Monitors on-premises networks using flow data forwarded to the cloud-based analytics service.

front.text: What are the four protocol/framework components used by 802.1X?

items: EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol, RFC 4187) — Provides an encapsulated transport for authentication parameters.
EAP methods — Specific authentication methods used inside EAP (e.g., EAP-TLS, PEAP, EAP-FAST).
EAPoL (EAP over LAN) — Layer 2 encapsulation defined by 802.1X to transport EAP messages over IEEE 802 wired/wireless networks.
RADIUS — AAA protocol used between the authenticator and the authentication server to carry EAP.

front.text: What are the three roles in an 802.1X network and what does each do?

items: Supplicant — Software on the endpoint (e.g., Windows/macOS native, Cisco AnyConnect) that provides credentials to the authenticator using EAPoL.
Authenticator — Network access device (switch or WLC) controlling network access; relays EAP between the supplicant (EAPoL) and the authentication server (RADIUS).
Authentication Server — RADIUS server (e.g., Cisco ISE) that validates the endpoint identity and returns an authorization result.

front.text: Compare inline (native) SGT tagging and SXP for propagating SGTs across the network.

items: Inline (native) tagging — Switch inserts a 16-bit SGT directly into the Layer 2 frame (CMD header). Independent of L3 protocol; preserved hop-by-hop. Requires Cisco devices with TrustSec ASIC support; non-supporting devices drop the tagged frame.

SXP (SGT Exchange Protocol) — TCP-based control-plane protocol that communicates IP-to-SGT mappings between devices that lack inline-tagging hardware. Sender is the speaker, receiver is the listener; SXP can be single-hop or multi-hop. The receiving switch enforces policy locally using its IP-to-SGT mapping database.

front.text: What are the numeric ranges for numbered standard and numbered extended ACLs, and what are named, PACL, and VACL?

items: Numbered standard ACLs — Match on source network only. Numbered ranges 1–99 and 1300–1999.
Numbered extended ACLs — Match on source, destination, protocol, port, etc. Numbered ranges 100–199 and 2000–2699.
Named ACLs — Standard or extended ACLs identified by a name instead of a number; preferred for readability and flexibility.
PACLs (Port ACLs) — ACLs (standard, extended, named, or MAC) applied to Layer 2 switch ports.
VACLs (VLAN ACLs) — ACLs applied to traffic within a VLAN.

front.text: What are the three default Cisco IOS privilege levels and what is each capable of?

rows: Allows only disable, enable, exit, help, and logout.
Default after login (R1>); read-only commands; configure terminal NOT allowed.
Highest level (R1#); full access to all CLI commands, including configuration.

front.text: What are the three independent security functions in the AAA framework?

items: Authentication — Verifies user/device identity prior to granting access to a device or service.
Authorization — Defines the access privileges and restrictions enforced for an authenticated user (what they're allowed to do).
Accounting — Tracks/logs user activity (login times, executed commands, etc.) for audit and security reporting.

front.text: What is the difference between a Type 1 and a Type 2 hypervisor? Give examples.

items: Type 1 (Bare-metal) — Runs directly on the physical hardware with no host OS underneath. Used in production data centers. Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Cisco NX-OS Hypervisor.
Type 2 (Hosted) — Runs as an application on top of a general-purpose host OS. Used mostly for desktops/labs. Examples: VMware Workstation/Fusion, Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels Desktop.

front.text: What are the four main components of Cisco Enterprise NFV (ENFV)?

items: Management and Orchestration (MANO) — Cisco DNA Center provides VNF management and NFV orchestration.
Virtual Network Functions (VNFs) — Software providing virtual networking functions (e.g., virtual router, virtual firewall, virtual WLC).
NFV Infrastructure Software (NFVIS) — Operating system that virtualizes the underlying hardware and hosts the VNFs.
Hardware (x86 platform) — x86-based compute resources (CPU, memory, storage) such as Cisco ENCS appliances on which NFVIS and VNFs run.

front.text: Match the HTTP methods GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE to their actions.

rows: Submits data to a specific destination
Submitting login credentials / creating a resource
Replaces data in a specific destination
Updating an NTP server entry (full replace)
Appends/modifies data in a specific destination
Removes data from a specific destination

front.text: What does each CRUD function do, and which HTTP method maps to it?

rows: Inserts data in a database or application
Retrieves data from a database or application
Modifies or replaces data in a database or application
Removes data from a database or application

front.text: Match these REST API HTTP status codes to their meaning: 200, 201, 400, 401, 403, 404.

rows: Successful GET/POST exchange of data with an API
A new resource was successfully created via the API
Request failed due to a client-side error (malformed request)
Client is not authenticated to access the site or API call
Authenticated, but access not granted by the supplied credentials
Resource at the URL does not exist or is hidden

front.text: In an IPsec transform set, what is the difference between an AH transform and an ESP transform, and what does esp-aes 256 with esp-sha-hmac provide?

items: AH (Authentication Header) transforms — Provide data integrity and origin authentication only; no confidentiality. Examples: ah-md5-hmac (deprecated), ah-sha-hmac, ah-sha256-hmac.
ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) transforms — Provide confidentiality (encryption) and optionally authentication. Examples: esp-aes, esp-aes 256, esp-3des (deprecated), esp-sha-hmac.

esp-aes 256 + esp-sha-hmac — ESP encryption with 256-bit AES plus integrity/authentication using SHA-1 HMAC. Provides confidentiality, integrity, anti-replay, and origin authentication of the payload.

back.text:
crypto ipsec transform-set TS_AES256 esp-aes 256 esp-sha-hmac
 mode tunnel

front.text: You configure OSPF on an Ethernet link between two routers and the adjacency stays at 2-WAY between two non-DR routers in a different lab. Why does this happen, and how do you fix it on a point-to-point Ethernet link?

back.text: On Ethernet, OSPF defaults to the Broadcast network type, which elects a DR/BDR. Non-DR/BDR routers (DROther) form a FULL adjacency only with the DR and BDR — with each other they remain at 2-WAY. That is normal behavior for Broadcast networks.

On a point-to-point Ethernet link with only two routers, you can change the OSPF network type to point-to-point so that no DR/BDR is elected, hello/dead timers become 10/40, and the routers form a single FULL adjacency.

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip ospf network point-to-point
Both ends of the link must agree on the OSPF network type and timers, or the adjacency will not come up.

front.text: R1 has two paths to 10.4.4.0/24: via R3 with FD = 3328, and via R4 with RD = 3500 (and total path metric 3700). Is the path via R4 a feasible successor? Why or why not?

back.text: No. The EIGRP feasibility condition requires the neighbor's reported distance (RD) to be strictly less than the local feasible distance (FD) of the current successor. Here RD via R4 = 3500, but FD = 3328, so RD is NOT less than FD.

Because the FC fails, the path via R4 is not a feasible successor and is not loop-free from R1's perspective. If the successor via R3 is lost, R1 must go ACTIVE for the route and send Queries to neighbors instead of using R4 immediately.
Rule: Feasible Successor exists only when neighbor's RD < local FD.

front.text: What K-values does EIGRP use by default in its composite metric, and which two are actually used?

items: Default K-values: K1=1, K2=0, K3=1, K4=0, K5=0.
K1 (Bandwidth) and K3 (Delay) are used by default.
K2 (Load), K4 and K5 (Reliability) are not used unless explicitly enabled.
Both neighbors must agree on K-values or the EIGRP adjacency will not form.

front.text: What are the EIGRP DUAL Passive and Active states, and what triggers a transition to Active?

items: Passive (P) — Normal/stable state. The route has a successor and DUAL is not actively recomputing it.
Active (A) — DUAL is actively computing a new path because the successor failed and no feasible successor exists. Router sends Queries to neighbors.
Trigger to Active: loss of the successor with no feasible successor in the topology table.
If queries are not answered, the route can become Stuck-In-Active (SIA), causing neighbor reset.

front.text: Identify OSPFv2 LSA types 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 7.

rows: Generated by every router; describes its links within an area.
Generated by the DR on multi-access networks; lists routers attached to the segment.
Generated by ABRs; advertises inter-area prefixes between areas.
Generated by ABRs; advertises the location of an ASBR to other areas.
Generated by ASBRs; advertises external routes redistributed into OSPF; flooded throughout the domain (except stub/NSSA).
Generated by ASBRs inside an NSSA; converted to Type 5 by the NSSA ABR when leaving the area.

front.text: Compare OSPF Stub, Totally Stubby, NSSA, and Totally NSSA areas. Which LSA types are blocked in each?

rows: Types 1, 2; only default route from ABR
Types 1, 2, 3; Type 7 (translated to Type 5 at ABR)

front.text: How is OSPF cost calculated, and what is the default reference bandwidth?

items: Formula: Cost = Reference Bandwidth / Interface Bandwidth.
Default reference bandwidth = 100 Mbps (10^8). This means anything 100 Mbps or faster has cost 1.
To differentiate Gig and 10-Gig links, change the reference bandwidth: auto-cost reference-bandwidth <Mbps>.
Reference bandwidth must match on all routers in the OSPF domain for consistent path selection.

back.text:
router ospf 1
 auto-cost reference-bandwidth 100000

front.text: What are the six BGP neighbor states in order, from initial state to fully established?

items: Idle — Initial state; BGP refuses incoming connections and is searching for a route to the peer.
Connect — Waiting for the TCP three-way handshake (port 179) to complete.
Active — TCP connection failed; retrying. (Active is bad — peer is not yet established.)
OpenSent — TCP up and an Open message has been sent; waiting for peer's Open.
OpenConfirm — Open received and validated; waiting for Keepalive or Notification.
Established — Peering complete; Update messages can be exchanged.

front.text: List the first eight steps of the BGP best-path selection algorithm in order.

items: Prefer the path with the highest WEIGHT (Cisco-only, local to router).
Prefer the path with the highest LOCAL_PREF.
Prefer the path that was locally originated (network or aggregate, redistributed).
Prefer the path with the shortest AS_PATH.
Prefer the path with the lowest ORIGIN type (IGP < EGP < Incomplete).
Prefer the path with the lowest MED (only when AS_PATH first AS is the same).
Prefer eBGP over iBGP paths.
Prefer the path with the lowest IGP metric to the BGP next hop.

back.text: Mnemonic: 'We Love Oranges As Oranges Mean Pure Refreshment' — Weight, Local Pref, Originated, AS-Path, Origin, MED, eBGP/iBGP, IGP.

front.text: Show a basic eBGP configuration between two directly connected routers (R1 in AS 65001, R2 in AS 65002), and the verification command.

back.text:
! On R1 (AS 65001)
router bgp 65001
 neighbor 10.1.1.2 remote-as 65002
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.1.1.2 activate
  network 192.168.1.0 mask 255.255.255.0

! On R2 (AS 65002)
router bgp 65002
 neighbor 10.1.1.1 remote-as 65001
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.1.1.1 activate

show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors 10.1.1.2

front.text: Compare HSRP, VRRP, and GLBP on standard, virtual MAC, multicast group, and load balancing.
What are the six HSRP states a router transitions through, and which two are stable in production?

items: Initial — Starting state, before HSRP is enabled or interface comes up.
Learn — Router doesn't know the virtual IP and is waiting to learn it.
Listen — Knows the virtual IP, listens for hellos, but is neither active nor standby.
Speak — Sends hellos and participates in election.
Standby — Backup router; will take over if Active fails.
Active — Forwards packets sent to the virtual IP/MAC.

back.text: Stable production states are Active (one router) and Standby (one router).

front.text: Define the four NAT address terms: Inside Local, Inside Global, Outside Local, and Outside Global.

rows: Private/internal IP of an inside host as seen inside the network (e.g., 10.1.1.10).
Translated public IP of an inside host as seen on the Internet (e.g., 203.0.113.5).
Real public IP of an external host as seen on the Internet (e.g., 198.51.100.1).
How the outside host's IP appears to inside hosts (often the same as Outside Global unless using outside NAT).

front.text: What is an NTP stratum, what is the valid range, and what does stratum 16 mean?

items: Stratum indicates the distance (in NTP hops) from an authoritative time source.
Stratum 0 — Reference clock (atomic, GPS) — not directly accessible over the network.
Stratum 1 — Server directly attached to a stratum-0 source.
Stratum 2–15 — Servers/clients increasingly distant from stratum 1.
Stratum 16 — Means the device is unsynchronized (invalid for time use).

front.text: How does PTP (Precision Time Protocol, IEEE 1588) differ from NTP, and where is PTP used?

items: PTP uses hardware timestamps in the network path to achieve sub-microsecond accuracy, while NTP typically achieves only millisecond accuracy.
PTP uses a master/slave/transparent/boundary clock hierarchy; NTP uses stratum-based hierarchy.
PTP works on Ethernet (Layer 2 multicast 01-1B-19-00-00-00) or UDP (Layer 3).
PTP is used in environments needing tight time sync: financial trading, industrial automation, mobile backhaul, and broadcast/media.

front.text: Compare the EtherChannel protocols PAgP and LACP, and list each protocol's modes.
What are the three RSTP port states and how do they map to legacy 802.1D states?

rows: Port does not learn MACs or forward frames.
Port learns MAC addresses but does not forward data frames.
Port forwards frames and learns MAC addresses normally.

front.text: Define the four RSTP port roles: Root, Designated, Alternate, and Backup.

items: Root port — The single port on a non-root switch with the lowest cost path to the root bridge.
Designated port — The port on each segment that forwards traffic toward and away from the root for that segment.
Alternate port — Provides an alternate (backup) path to the root bridge from another switch; goes Forwarding instantly if the root port fails.
Backup port — A backup for a Designated port on the same switch (only on shared/hub segments).

front.text: Compare BPDU Guard, BPDU Filter, Root Guard, and Loop Guard.

rows: Disables (err-disables) a PortFast-enabled access port if it receives any BPDU. Used on user-facing ports.
Suppresses sending and/or receiving BPDUs on a port. Use carefully — can break STP if misapplied.
If a superior BPDU is received on the port, the port is moved to root-inconsistent (blocking). Used on ports facing other switches that should never become root.
If BPDUs stop being received on a non-designated port, port goes loop-inconsistent (blocking) instead of becoming designated forwarding. Prevents unidirectional link loops.

front.text: What are the DTP modes, and which combinations form a trunk?

rows: Always trunk; sends DTP unless nonegotiate.
Actively tries to form a trunk via DTP.
Will become a trunk only if the other side is Trunk or Desirable.

items: Trunk forms with: Trunk-Trunk, Trunk-Desirable, Trunk-Auto, Desirable-Desirable, Desirable-Auto.
Does NOT form trunk: Auto-Auto, Access-anything.
Best practice: hard-code mode (access or trunk) and use switchport nonegotiate to disable DTP.

front.text: What problem does MST (Multiple Spanning Tree, 802.1s) solve, and what three values must match for switches to be in the same MST region?

items: Problem: PVST+ runs one STP instance per VLAN, which doesn't scale at high VLAN counts. MST maps many VLANs into a few instances to reduce CPU/memory load.
Switches must agree on these three values to be in the same MST region:
1. MST configuration name
2. MST revision number
3. VLAN-to-instance mapping table

back.text:
spanning-tree mode mst
spanning-tree mst configuration
 name CAMPUS
 revision 1
 instance 1 vlan 10,20
 instance 2 vlan 30,40

front.text: What is a VRF, and how does VRF-Lite differ from full MPLS VPN VRFs?

items: VRF (Virtual Routing and Forwarding) — A logical instance of a routing table on a router. Allows multiple isolated routing domains on the same physical device with overlapping IP addresses.
VRF-Lite — VRFs without MPLS; segmentation is done hop by hop on each device with no MP-BGP/label switching.
MPLS VPN VRFs — VRFs combined with MP-BGP and MPLS labels (RD/RT) to carry multiple customers' VRF traffic across a provider core.

back.text:
vrf definition CUSTOMER_A
 address-family ipv4
 exit-address-family
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 vrf forwarding CUSTOMER_A
 ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0

front.text: Show a basic GRE tunnel configuration between two routers, and explain what GRE provides.

back.text:
! On R1
interface Tunnel0
 ip address 172.16.0.1 255.255.255.252
 tunnel source 203.0.113.1
 tunnel destination 198.51.100.1
 tunnel mode gre ip

! On R2
interface Tunnel0
 ip address 172.16.0.2 255.255.255.252
 tunnel source 198.51.100.1
 tunnel destination 203.0.113.1
 tunnel mode gre ip

items: GRE encapsulates arbitrary L3 protocols inside an IP packet (IP protocol 47).
Provides a virtual point-to-point link, supports multicast (so it can carry IGPs and multicast).
Does NOT provide encryption or authentication — combine with IPsec for security.

front.text: What transport does VXLAN use, what UDP port, and how many bits is the VNI?

items: Transport: VXLAN encapsulates Ethernet frames in MAC-in-UDP/IP.
Standard UDP destination port: 4789 (IANA-assigned).
VNI (VXLAN Network Identifier) is 24 bits, allowing up to ~16 million segments (vs. only 4096 traditional VLANs).
VTEPs perform the encapsulation/decapsulation; the underlay only needs to route between VTEP IPs.

front.text: List the steps of a LISP packet flow when a host in Site A sends traffic to a host in Site B.

items: Source host sends packet to its default gateway (the ITR), destined for the remote EID.
ITR checks its EID-to-RLOC map cache. If no entry exists, ITR sends a Map-Request to the Map Resolver (MR).
MR forwards the request to the Map Server (MS), which finds the registered ETR for the destination EID.
ETR responds with a Map-Reply containing the EID-to-RLOC mapping back to the ITR.
ITR caches the mapping, then LISP-encapsulates the packet (IP-in-UDP, port 4341) with the ETR's RLOC as the outer destination.
Underlay routes the encapsulated packet to the ETR.
ETR de-encapsulates and forwards the original packet to the destination EID.

front.text: What is a QoS trust boundary, and where should it typically be placed?

items: A trust boundary is the demarcation between where QoS markings (CoS/DSCP) coming from a device can be trusted vs. where they should be rewritten.
Beyond the trust boundary (toward the network), markings are honored without modification.
At/before the trust boundary (toward the user), markings are typically classified, remarked, or zeroed.
Best practice: trust boundary at the access layer, ideally on the access switch port for trusted devices (Cisco IP phones), or as deep into the network as you can place it.

front.text: Compare traffic shaping and traffic policing.

rows: Buffers (delays) traffic to smooth output to CIR
Drops or remarks traffic above CIR
Can cause TCP retransmits and bursts
Sub-rate WAN circuits to match SP-enforced rate
Enforce contract rate at ingress, drop abusive flows

front.text: How many bits are in CoS, IP Precedence, and DSCP, and where in the frame/packet are they located?

rows: 802.1Q TCI field in Ethernet frame (only on tagged trunks)
First 3 bits of the ToS byte in the IPv4 header
First 6 bits of the DS field (replaces ToS) in IPv4 or Traffic Class in IPv6

front.text: Compare the three main lightweight AP operating modes: Local, FlexConnect, and Bridge/Mesh.

items: Local mode — Default. AP tunnels all client traffic via CAPWAP back to the WLC; centralized data plane.
FlexConnect (formerly H-REAP) — AP can locally switch client traffic to the wired LAN even if the WAN to the WLC fails. Used for branch offices.
Bridge / Mesh mode — AP acts as a wireless bridge; used for outdoor mesh networks, with a Root AP connecting back to the wired network and Mesh APs forming wireless backhaul.

front.text: What UDP ports does CAPWAP use, and what does each tunnel carry?

rows: CAPWAP data plane (client traffic to/from WLC)

front.text: List the order in which a lightweight AP attempts to discover a WLC.

items: Layer 3 broadcast on the local subnet (limited use).
Locally stored (primed) WLC IPs from prior controller (primary, secondary, tertiary).
DHCP Option 43 — provides WLC management IP(s) via DHCP.
DNS lookup — resolves CISCO-CAPWAP-CONTROLLER.localdomain.
After candidate WLCs are found, the AP joins the WLC with capacity (Master, primary configured, or least-loaded).

front.text: What are the four key configuration components of Flexible NetFlow?

items: Flow Record — Defines the key (match) and non-key (collect) fields used to identify a flow.
Flow Exporter — Defines the destination collector IP, transport protocol/port, and source interface.
Flow Monitor — Combines a record + exporter(s) and a cache for flow data.
Apply to interface — flow monitor MON input/output applies the monitor to traffic.

back.text:
flow record REC1
 match ipv4 source address
 match ipv4 destination address
 collect counter bytes
 collect counter packets
!
flow exporter EXP1
 destination 10.1.1.10
 transport udp 9996
!
flow monitor MON1
 record REC1
 exporter EXP1
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip flow monitor MON1 input

Remote capture anywhere reachable via IP

front.text: List the eight Syslog severity levels (0–7) by name.

items: 0 — Emergency: System is unusable.
1 — Alert: Immediate action required.
2 — Critical: Critical condition.
3 — Error: Error condition.
4 — Warning: Warning condition.
5 — Notification: Normal but significant condition.
6 — Informational: Informational message (default on most IOS devices).
7 — Debugging: Debug-level messages.

back.text: Use SNMPv3 with authPriv (auth + encryption) in production environments.

rows: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE (HTTP verbs)

front.text: What is YANG, and what is the difference between native, OpenConfig, and IETF YANG models?

items: YANG (Yet Another Next Generation, RFC 7950) — A data modeling language used by NETCONF, RESTCONF, and gRPC/gNMI to define configuration and operational state in a structured, hierarchical way.
Native models — Vendor/OS-specific (e.g., Cisco IOS-XE native models). Cover all features but are not portable.
OpenConfig models — Vendor-neutral models defined by network operators; portable across vendors at the cost of feature coverage.
IETF models — Standards-based YANG models from the IETF (e.g., ietf-interfaces). Vendor-neutral and standardized.

front.text: What are the data types supported by JSON, and what are the structural rules?

items: Data types: string (in double quotes), number, boolean (true/false), null, object {}, array [].
Objects use key-value pairs separated by commas, keys must be strings in double quotes.
Arrays are ordered lists of values, separated by commas.
JSON does not support comments, single quotes, trailing commas, or undefined.

front.text: What's the difference between a Python list, tuple, and dictionary?
Write an EEM applet that, every time a user enters 'write erase', logs a syslog message and sends a copy of the running config to a TFTP server.

back.text:
event manager applet PROTECT_CONFIG
 event cli pattern "write erase" sync no skip no
 action 1.0 syslog priority warnings msg "User $_cli_username executed write erase"
 action 2.0 cli command "enable"
 action 3.0 cli command "copy running-config tftp://10.1.1.10/$_event_pub_time-running.cfg"

items: event cli — Triggers on a CLI pattern.
action 1.0/2.0/3.0 — Numbered actions execute in order.
$_cli_username, $_event_pub_time — Built-in EEM variables.

front.text: Compare the orchestration tools Ansible, Puppet, and Chef. Which are agent-based vs. agentless?
What are the main mechanisms used to secure REST APIs?

items: HTTPS/TLS — Encrypts API traffic and authenticates the server.
Authentication tokens — Bearer tokens (e.g., JWT), API keys, or session tokens passed in HTTP headers (often X-Auth-Token or Authorization: Bearer).
OAuth 2.0 — Delegated authorization framework for third-party access.
Basic auth — Username/password Base64-encoded; should only be used with HTTPS, and only for simple/internal APIs.
Rate limiting — Prevents brute force and DoS by capping requests per client.
Input validation — Prevents injection attacks (SQLi, command injection) on API parameters.

front.text: What is Control Plane Policing (CoPP), and why is it important?

items: CoPP applies QoS-style policies (class-map / policy-map) to traffic destined to the route processor's control plane.
It protects the CPU from being overwhelmed by management traffic, routing protocol storms, or DoS attacks.
Common classes: routing protocols (OSPF, BGP, EIGRP), management (SSH, SNMP, NTP), undesirable (ICMP floods).
Applied with: control-plane / service-policy input <POLICY>.
Without CoPP, an attacker can overwhelm the CPU with crafted packets and crash adjacencies.

back.text:
control-plane
 service-policy input COPP_POLICY

front.text: Show the basic configuration steps to secure VTY lines with SSH-only access and local authentication.

back.text:
hostname R1
ip domain name example.com
crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048
ip ssh version 2
!
username admin privilege 15 secret StrongP@ssw0rd
!
line vty 0 4
 login local
 transport input ssh
 exec-timeout 5 0
 logging synchronous
!
ip access-list standard MGMT_ACL
 permit 10.10.10.0 0.0.0.255
 deny any log
!
line vty 0 4
 access-class MGMT_ACL in

front.text: How does Cisco Encrypted Traffic Analytics (ETA) detect threats in encrypted traffic without decrypting it?

items: ETA exports enhanced telemetry from Cisco switches/routers (Catalyst, ISR/ASR) for encrypted flows.
Three key data elements collected: Initial Data Packet (IDP) of the flow, Sequence of Packet Lengths and Times (SPLT), and TLS-specific metadata (cipher suites, key exchange).
Cisco Secure Network Analytics + Secure Cloud Analytics use machine learning on these features to identify malware patterns in encrypted traffic.
Result: detect threats and verify TLS compliance without breaking end-to-end encryption.

front.text: What is MACsec, what layer does it operate at, and what does it protect?

items: MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE) is a Layer 2 hop-by-hop encryption standard that encrypts Ethernet frames between two adjacent devices.
Provides confidentiality, integrity, and origin authentication for L2 traffic.
Uses GCM-AES-128 or GCM-AES-256 with a 128-bit Integrity Check Value.
Keys are managed by the MACsec Key Agreement (MKA) protocol; can be PSK or 802.1X-derived.
Use cases: switch-to-switch links, host-to-switch links, data center top-of-rack security.

front.text: What are the four primary workflow areas in Cisco Catalyst Center (formerly DNA Center)?

items: Design — Build network hierarchies, set global settings (DNS, NTP, AAA), and define IP address pools.
Policy — Define group-based access policies (using ISE/TrustSec) and create virtual networks.
Provision — Push configurations and onboard devices using PnP, including SD-Access fabric provisioning.
Assurance — Telemetry-driven monitoring, AI-powered insights, and proactive issue detection for clients, network, and applications.

front.text: Two OSPF routers are stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state and the adjacency never reaches FULL. What is the most common cause and fix?

items: Most common cause: MTU mismatch between the two interfaces. OSPF requires matching MTU to negotiate database descriptor (DBD) packets.
When MTUs differ, the side with the larger MTU sends DBDs the other side can't accept, and the FSM stalls at EXSTART/EXCHANGE.
Fix 1 (preferred): Match the MTU on both interfaces (e.g., both 1500 or both 9000).
Fix 2: Disable MTU check on the OSPF interface — but this is a workaround, not a real fix:

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip ospf mtu-ignore
Verify with: show ip ospf neighbor, show ip ospf interface, and show interfaces (look at MTU).

front.text: An eBGP session between two directly connected routers is stuck in Active state. List the most common causes.

items: TCP port 179 blocked — ACL or firewall preventing the TCP three-way handshake.
Wrong neighbor IP or remote-as configured on either side.
Source IP mismatch — eBGP peer expects a different source. Use 'neighbor X.X.X.X update-source <int>' if peering loopback-to-loopback (and adjust ebgp-multihop).
Default eBGP TTL = 1; if peering between loopbacks (not directly connected), need 'ebgp-multihop' or 'disable-connected-check'.
No IGP route or static route to reach the neighbor IP.
Authentication password mismatch (MD5).
Neighbor not yet activated under the address-family.

back.text:
show bgp ipv4 unicast summary
show ip route 10.1.1.2
debug ip tcp transactions
debug bgp ipv4 unicast events

front.text: You have a hub-and-spoke topology over a multipoint Frame Relay subinterface. Spokes can ping the hub but not each other through OSPF. Which OSPF network type would you choose, and why?

items: Use OSPF point-to-multipoint network type.

Why: P2MP advertises each spoke as a /32 host route with the next hop set to the spoke's own interface. This avoids the partial-mesh next-hop problem you hit with broadcast/non-broadcast on a hub-and-spoke NBMA, where the hub doesn't change the next hop on advertised routes.
P2MP also does not require DR/BDR election (since not all spokes have direct PVCs to each other).
Timers under P2MP: Hello 30 / Dead 120.

back.text:
interface Serial0/0.1 multipoint
 ip ospf network point-to-multipoint

front.text: You want to make Distribution-Switch-1 the root for VLANs 10–20 and Distribution-Switch-2 the root for VLANs 30–40, with each acting as backup for the other. Show the configuration.

back.text:
! On Distribution-Switch-1
spanning-tree vlan 10-20 root primary
spanning-tree vlan 30-40 root secondary
!
! On Distribution-Switch-2
spanning-tree vlan 30-40 root primary
spanning-tree vlan 10-20 root secondary

items: spanning-tree vlan X root primary sets the bridge priority to 24576 (or 4096 lower than current root).
spanning-tree vlan X root secondary sets the bridge priority to 28672.
This pattern is the classic per-VLAN load-balancing design with PVST+/Rapid-PVST+.
Verify with: show spanning-tree vlan 10.

front.text: What are the default administrative distances for connected, static, eBGP, EIGRP, OSPF, RIP, and iBGP, and why does AD matter during redistribution?

back.text: During mutual redistribution between protocols, AD differences can cause routing loops or suboptimal paths. Use route maps with tags or filtering to prevent feedback loops.

front.text: What is Policy-Based Routing (PBR), and what makes it different from normal destination-based routing?

items: PBR uses a route-map applied to an interface (or locally) to make forwarding decisions based on attributes other than just destination IP — e.g., source IP, source/destination port, packet size, or DSCP.
Normal routing only looks at destination IP via the routing table.
Use cases: send specific traffic out a backup link, route VoIP via a low-latency path, force certain users through a security device.
Applied with: ip policy route-map <NAME> on the ingress interface, or ip local policy route-map <NAME> for router-originated traffic.

back.text:
access-list 100 permit ip 10.10.10.0 0.0.0.255 any
route-map PBR_VOICE permit 10
 match ip address 100
 set ip next-hop 192.0.2.1
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip policy route-map PBR_VOICE

front.text: How does OSPFv3 differ from OSPFv2, and what address family does it support?

items: OSPFv3 was originally designed for IPv6 but supports both IPv4 and IPv6 via address families (RFC 5838).
Runs per-link (not per-subnet) — neighbors don't need to share a subnet.
Uses link-local addresses (FE80::/10) for neighbor adjacencies and updates.
Authentication uses IPsec (no built-in MD5/SHA like OSPFv2).
Multicast addresses: FF02::5 (AllSPFRouters) and FF02::6 (AllDRouters).
Same packet types, LSA flooding logic, and DR/BDR election as OSPFv2.

front.text: Show how to summarize inter-area routes on an OSPF ABR and external routes on an ASBR.

back.text:
! Inter-area summarization on an ABR (summarizes routes FROM area 1)
router ospf 1
 area 1 range 10.1.0.0 255.255.0.0

! External (Type 5) summarization on an ASBR
router ospf 1
 summary-address 172.16.0.0 255.255.0.0
area range is used at ABRs for inter-area summarization. summary-address is used at ASBRs for redistributed external routes.

front.text: What does 'passive-interface' do in OSPF, and what is the use case?

items: Stops OSPF from sending hellos out the interface, so no neighbor adjacency is formed there.
The interface's network is still advertised in OSPF (the prefix is still in the LSDB).
Use case: prevent forming adjacencies on user-facing access interfaces while still advertising those subnets.
passive-interface default + no passive-interface <core-link> is the safer best practice.

back.text:
router ospf 1
 passive-interface default
 no passive-interface GigabitEthernet0/0

front.text: Categorize the BGP attributes WEIGHT, LOCAL_PREF, AS_PATH, ORIGIN, MED, NEXT_HOP, and COMMUNITY by attribute type.

rows: Sent to all peers (if not stripped)

front.text: What is the difference between BGP being stuck in Idle vs. Active state?

items: Idle — BGP refuses incoming connections and is not trying to connect. Often caused by missing neighbor configuration, administratively shut down, or no route to peer at all.

Active — BGP is actively trying to establish a TCP session but failing. Most commonly indicates ACL/firewall blocking TCP/179, asymmetric routing, source-IP mismatch, or remote-AS misconfig on the peer.
'Active' is bad — it means the session is NOT established and TCP cannot complete.

front.text: An iBGP-learned route is showing 'inaccessible' in the BGP table even though the eBGP session is up. What is the typical cause and fix?

items: Cause: By default, iBGP does not change the BGP next hop. The original eBGP next hop (the external peer's IP) is unreachable to the receiving iBGP router because the IGP doesn't know about that external subnet.
Fix: On the eBGP-speaking router, configure 'neighbor X.X.X.X next-hop-self' for its iBGP peers, so it advertises iBGP routes with itself as the next hop.
Alternative fix: Redistribute or advertise the external link subnet into the IGP (less common).

back.text:
router bgp 65001
 neighbor 10.0.0.2 remote-as 65001
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.0.0.2 next-hop-self

front.text: What is an EIGRP stub router, and why is it used?

items: An EIGRP stub router announces itself as a stub to neighbors and limits which routes it advertises (default: connected and summary).
Stub routers are NOT queried by hub routers during DUAL — this prevents Stuck-In-Active (SIA) conditions in hub-and-spoke topologies.
Common in DMVPN/branch designs to make spokes stubs.
Configured with: eigrp stub [connected | summary | static | redistributed | receive-only].

back.text:
router eigrp 100
 eigrp stub connected summary

front.text: What is EIGRP named mode and why is it preferred over classic mode?

items: Named mode uses a single 'router eigrp NAME' container with sub-modes for address-family, af-interface, and topology.
Supports both IPv4 and IPv6 in the same EIGRP instance.
Enables newer features: wide metrics (64-bit), per-interface authentication, and easier configuration of stubs.
All EIGRP configuration (interfaces, neighbors, redistribution, authentication) is centralized under one process.

back.text:
router eigrp CORP
 address-family ipv4 unicast autonomous-system 100
  af-interface GigabitEthernet0/0
   authentication mode hmac-sha-256 StrongKey
  exit-af-interface
  network 10.0.0.0
 exit-address-family

front.text: What are the three RSTP link types, and how do they affect rapid transition to forwarding?

rows: Yes — uses proposal/agreement handshake for sub-second convergence
No — falls back to legacy 802.1D timers

back.text: An edge port that receives a BPDU loses its edge status immediately and behaves like a regular RSTP port.

front.text: What problem does UDLD solve, and what is the difference between Normal and Aggressive mode?

items: UDLD (Unidirectional Link Detection) detects when a fiber link is broken in only one direction — a condition that can cause STP loops because BPDUs aren't received on one side.
Normal mode — Detects unidirectional condition and marks the port as undetermined; logs a syslog but does NOT shut the port down.
Aggressive mode — After 8 unsuccessful retries (~7 seconds), the port is err-disabled (preferred for fiber links).
Often paired with Loop Guard for layered protection.

front.text: Compare the VTP modes Server, Client, Transparent, and Off, and explain what version 3 added.

back.text: VTPv3 added: support for extended-range VLANs (1006–4094), MST configuration distribution, primary server concept, and authentication improvements.

front.text: Define the IPv6 prefix types: Global Unicast, Unique Local, Link-Local, and Multicast. Give the prefix range for each.

rows: Globally routable IPv6 (analogous to public IPv4)
Auto-configured per interface; not routable beyond the link

front.text: What does IPv6 Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP) replace from IPv4, and what are the five NDP message types?

items: NDP replaces IPv4 ARP, ICMP Router Discovery, and ICMP Redirect.
Router Solicitation (RS) — Host asks routers to send Router Advertisements.
Router Advertisement (RA) — Router announces its presence, prefixes, MTU, and DHCPv6 flags.
Neighbor Solicitation (NS) — Host asks for the L2 address of a neighbor (replaces ARP request).
Neighbor Advertisement (NA) — Reply to NS with L2 address (replaces ARP reply).
Redirect — Inform a host of a better next hop on the same link.

front.text: How does IPv6 EUI-64 build the interface ID from a 48-bit MAC address?

items: Split the 48-bit MAC into two 24-bit halves (OUI and NIC).
Insert FFFE between them to make a 64-bit identifier.
Flip the 7th bit (Universal/Local bit) of the OUI: if it was 0 (universal), make it 1 (locally administered).
Prepend the /64 prefix to form the full IPv6 address.

front.text: Which 2.4 GHz channels are non-overlapping in North America, and why do we care?

items: Channels 1, 6, and 11 are the only non-overlapping 20 MHz channels in the 2.4 GHz band (US).
2.4 GHz has only ~83.5 MHz of usable spectrum, while a single 802.11 channel is 22 MHz wide; overlap causes co-channel interference and reduced throughput.
5 GHz has many more non-overlapping 20 MHz channels (24+ in the US), which is why 5 GHz is preferred for capacity.
Best practice: deploy a 1/6/11 alternating pattern in 2.4 GHz coverage areas.

front.text: Compare WPA, WPA2, and WPA3, including encryption and key exchange.

rows: SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) replaces PSK 4-way
Forward secrecy, protects against offline dictionary attacks

front.text: What is the difference between intra-controller, inter-controller (Layer 2), and Layer 3 roaming?

items: Intra-controller roaming — Client moves between APs joined to the same WLC. Fast and seamless; same client database.
Inter-controller Layer 2 roaming — Client moves between APs on different WLCs that share the same client VLAN/subnet. Client keeps the same IP; client database is moved between WLCs.
Layer 3 (mobility) roaming — Client moves between WLCs in different VLANs/subnets. The original WLC (anchor) tunnels traffic to the new WLC (foreign) so the client can keep its IP.
WLCs in a mobility group exchange information to enable fast roaming.

front.text: Name the common 802.1X fast roaming methods used on enterprise Wi-Fi.

items: PMK Caching (Sticky Key Caching) — AP/WLC caches the Pairwise Master Key for the client; on return, skips full 802.1X.
Opportunistic Key Caching (OKC) — WLC pre-distributes PMK to other APs so client can roam without re-auth.
CCKM (Cisco Centralized Key Management) — Cisco proprietary; WLC centralizes key distribution for sub-millisecond roaming. Limited client support.
802.11r (Fast BSS Transition / FT) — Standard-based fast roaming; pre-establishes keys with target AP via Over-the-Air or Over-the-DS.
802.11k (Neighbor Reports) — Helps clients identify candidate APs for roaming.
802.11v (BSS Transition Management) — Network suggests a better AP to a client.

front.text: Define BSSID, SSID, and ESSID.

items: BSSID (Basic Service Set ID) — The MAC address of the AP's radio for a specific SSID. Uniquely identifies a single AP/SSID combination.
SSID (Service Set ID) — The human-readable network name (up to 32 characters) clients connect to.
ESSID (Extended Service Set ID) — Same SSID broadcast by multiple APs working together; allows roaming across APs that share the SSID.

class-map match-any VOICE

class-map match-any SIGNALING
 match dscp cs3
class-map match-any BUSINESS
 match dscp af31
!

  priority percent 30
 class SIGNALING
  bandwidth percent 5
 class BUSINESS
  bandwidth percent 25
 class class-default
  fair-queue
  random-detect
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 service-policy output WAN_QOS
priority percent creates the LLQ (with built-in policer). bandwidth percent creates CBWFQ classes with minimum guarantees.

front.text: What is WRED, and why is it preferred over tail drop?

items: WRED (Weighted Random Early Detection) is a congestion-avoidance technique that randomly drops packets BEFORE the queue fills.
Drops are weighted by IP Precedence or DSCP — lower-priority traffic is dropped more aggressively.

Why preferred: Tail drop drops at the end of a full queue and causes 'global TCP synchronization' where many flows back off and ramp up together. WRED smooths this out and increases overall throughput.
DSCP-based WRED can be combined with AF (Assured Forwarding) drop precedences (AFx1/x2/x3).

front.text: What is the difference between IPsec tunnel mode and transport mode?
Compare AH and ESP IPsec protocols and explain why ESP is preferred.

rows: Yes (does not cover outer IP header)
No (NAT changes header → breaks AH ICV)

back.text: ESP is used in nearly all modern IPsec deployments because it provides both confidentiality and integrity AND can traverse NAT via NAT-T.

front.text: What is the difference between DMVPN Phase 1, 2, and 3?

items: Phase 1 — Hub-and-spoke only. All spoke-to-spoke traffic goes through the hub. Spokes use point-to-point GRE tunnels to the hub.
Phase 2 — Spoke-to-spoke direct tunnels are built on demand using NHRP, but each spoke still needs full routing info from the hub (no summarization).
Phase 3 — Spoke-to-spoke with NHRP redirects and shortcuts. Hub can send a summary route, and an NHRP shortcut creates the direct tunnel when traffic hits the spoke. Most scalable design.

front.text: What does BGP EVPN add to VXLAN, and what problem does it solve compared to flood-and-learn VXLAN?

items: MP-BGP EVPN provides a control plane for VXLAN, replacing the flood-and-learn data-plane MAC learning.
VTEPs use BGP to advertise: MAC addresses (Type 2), IP/MAC bindings (Type 2 with IP), and host routes/IRB.
Reduces flooding (no unknown unicast or BUM flooding for known hosts), improves convergence, and supports symmetric/asymmetric IRB for routing.
Standard-based and vendor-interoperable; the foundation for modern data center fabrics (Cisco ACI uses VXLAN with a different control plane; NX-OS VXLAN-EVPN is BGP-based).

front.text: What is the difference between the SD-Access underlay and overlay?

items: Underlay — Physical network providing IP reachability between fabric nodes. Typically a Layer 3 routed access design using IS-IS (DNAC-automated) or OSPF/EIGRP.
Overlay — Logical network built on top of the underlay using VXLAN for the data plane and LISP for the control plane. Carries actual user/endpoint traffic.
Endpoints connect into the overlay via fabric edge nodes; traffic is encapsulated into VXLAN and routed across the underlay.

front.text: What is Cisco Plug and Play (PnP), and how does a new device discover the PnP server?

items: PnP is a zero-touch onboarding mechanism. A factory-default device boots, finds the PnP server (Cisco DNA/Catalyst Center), and downloads its configuration and image automatically.
PnP server discovery methods (in order of preference):
1. Manual config (PnP profile pre-staged via USB or string).
2. DHCP Option 43 — provides PnP server IP/URL.
3. DNS — resolves pnpserver.localdomain.
4. Cisco PnP Connect cloud service — uses device serial number registered to a Smart Account.

front.text: What are the main components of an Ansible playbook?

items: Playbook — A YAML file with one or more plays.
Play — Maps a set of hosts to tasks. Includes hosts, gather_facts, vars, tasks.
Task — A single action that calls a module (e.g., ios_config, ios_command).
Module — Code that does the actual work on the target device.
Inventory — A list of hosts and groups (often hosts.yml or hosts.ini).
Variables — Defined inline, in vars files, or in inventory.

back.text:
- name: Configure routers
  hosts: routers
  gather_facts: no
  tasks:
    - name: Set hostname
      cisco.ios.ios_config:
        lines:
          - hostname R1

front.text: What are the prerequisites and steps to enable SSH on a Cisco IOS device?

Set domain name (ip domain name <name>).

Enable SSH version 2: ip ssh version 2.
Configure local user accounts: username admin secret <pwd>.
Restrict VTY lines to SSH only and use local auth: line vty 0 4 / login local / transport input ssh.

ip domain name example.com
crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048
ip ssh version 2
username admin privilege 15 secret StrongP@ss
line vty 0 4

front.text: What are the four ISE personas and what does each do?

items: Policy Administration Node (PAN) — Provides the GUI and configuration interface; manages all policy and configuration.
Policy Service Node (PSN) — Handles the actual authentication/authorization/policy decisions (RADIUS, TACACS+, profiling).
Monitoring and Troubleshooting Node (MnT) — Aggregates logs and provides reporting.
pxGrid Node — Pub/sub framework that integrates ISE with third-party security/network systems (e.g., firewalls, SIEMs).

front.text: What does Cisco Secure Endpoint (formerly AMP for Endpoints) do, and what does Cisco Umbrella do?

items: Cisco Secure Endpoint (AMP) — Endpoint protection providing AV, EDR, retrospective security (file trajectory), and integration with Talos threat intelligence.
Cisco Umbrella — Cloud-delivered DNS-layer security and Secure Internet Gateway (SIG). Blocks malicious domains, C2 callbacks, and risky destinations before connection is made.
Together: Umbrella stops threats at the DNS/IP layer; Secure Endpoint stops what reaches the device.

front.text: What's the difference between push-based and pull-based configuration management, and which tools use each?

items: Push-based — Control node initiates connections to managed nodes and sends instructions. Examples: Ansible (SSH/NETCONF), SaltStack (master pushes via ZeroMQ).
Pull-based — Managed nodes run an agent that periodically checks in with the master and pulls down their desired state. Examples: Puppet (default 30 min check-in), Chef.
Push: simpler, no agent, fewer firewall changes; but harder to scale to thousands of nodes.
Pull: scales to massive node counts, self-correcting drift; but requires agent installation and certificate management.

front.text: What is anycast addressing, and where is it commonly used?

items: Anycast — A single IP address is advertised from multiple locations; routing delivers packets to the topologically nearest instance.
Built using BGP (Internet) or IGP (intra-AS) advertising the same /32 (IPv4) or /128 (IPv6) from multiple servers.
Common uses: DNS root servers (e.g., 8.8.8.8, 1.1.1.1), CDN edge points, Anycast Gateway in VXLAN/EVPN fabrics (same gateway IP/MAC on all leaf switches).

front.text: What is the difference between SSO, NSF, and GR in Cisco high-availability designs?

items: SSO (Stateful Switchover) — On a dual-supervisor chassis, the standby supervisor maintains synchronized state and takes over instantly on active sup failure. No reboot of line cards.
NSF (Non-Stop Forwarding) — Continues forwarding using the existing FIB while the control plane recovers. Used with SSO so neighbors don't drop adjacencies during a switchover.
GR (Graceful Restart) — Routing protocol extension that lets neighbors keep advertising routes through a peer while it restarts its control plane (NSF helper). Supported by OSPF, BGP, IS-IS, EIGRP.

front.text: Compare StackWise, VSS, and StackWise Virtual.

rows: Multiple physical switches behave as one logical switch using stacking cables; one active sup, one standby.
Two chassis combined into one logical chassis via VSL links; supports MEC (Multichassis EtherChannel).
Modern replacement for VSS using Ethernet links (SVL); two chassis as one logical switch.

back.text: All three eliminate STP loops by presenting one logical control plane and enable cross-chassis EtherChannel for full link utilization.

front.text: What is the difference between a 2-tier (collapsed core) and 3-tier campus design?

items: 3-tier — Access, Distribution, and Core as separate layers. Used in large campuses with multiple distribution blocks; core provides high-speed transit between distribution layers.
2-tier (collapsed core) — Distribution and Core layers are combined into a single layer. Used in small to medium campuses with only one distribution block.
Choose 3-tier when you have multiple buildings or large numbers of access switches; 2-tier is fine when your distribution can handle aggregation and cross-block transit without becoming a bottleneck.

front.text: What does it mean for a network to be 'fabric-based' vs. traditional, and what are the key components of a fabric design?

items: Traditional — STP/L3 hop-by-hop, VLAN-based segmentation, manual config, limited mobility.
Fabric-based — Underlay (L3 routed) carries an overlay (VXLAN, GRE, etc.) with a control plane (LISP, BGP-EVPN). Provides segmentation, mobility, and policy independent of physical topology.
Key fabric components: Edge nodes (where endpoints connect), Border nodes (connect to outside), Control plane nodes (mapping system), and an Underlay providing IP reachability.

front.text: Compare IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS cloud service models. Who manages what?

rows: AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine
Azure App Service, Google App Engine

front.text: How does EtherChannel choose a member link for a given frame, and why might one link in a bundle look overutilized?

items: EtherChannel hashes selected fields (src/dst MAC, src/dst IP, src/dst port) to pick a member link. Same hash → same link.
If only one server talks to one client (single MAC pair or single 5-tuple flow), every frame hashes to the same link — looks like uneven load.
Fix: pick a richer hash algorithm such as src-dst-mixed-ip-port to spread microflows across more links.
Configured globally with: port-channel load-balance src-dst-ip (or src-dst-port, etc.).

back.text: port-channel load-balance src-dst-mixed-ip-port

front.text: What is the native VLAN on a 802.1Q trunk, and why is it a security concern?

items: Native VLAN — The single VLAN whose frames are sent UNtagged on a trunk. Default native VLAN is 1.
Security concern: VLAN hopping via double tagging (an attacker sends a frame with two tags; the first tag matches the native VLAN and is stripped, leaving the inner tag to enter another VLAN).
Mitigations: change native VLAN to an unused, dedicated VLAN; explicitly tag the native VLAN with 'vlan dot1q tag native'; never use VLAN 1 for user traffic.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk native vlan 999
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30
!
vlan dot1q tag native

front.text: Show an HSRP configuration with priority, preempt, and interface tracking that decrements priority on uplink failure.

back.text:
track 1 interface GigabitEthernet0/1 line-protocol
!
interface Vlan10
 ip address 10.1.10.2 255.255.255.0
 standby version 2
 standby 10 ip 10.1.10.1
 standby 10 priority 110
 standby 10 preempt
 standby 10 track 1 decrement 20
 standby 10 authentication md5 key-string MyKey
If Gig0/1 (uplink) goes down, the local priority drops by 20 (110 - 20 = 90). If the peer's priority is higher, preempt allows takeover.

front.text: What is an SNMP MIB, and what is an OID?

items: MIB (Management Information Base) — A hierarchical, tree-structured database that defines all the variables (objects) an SNMP-managed device exposes.
OID (Object Identifier) — A dotted-decimal address (e.g., 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5.0) that uniquely identifies an object in the MIB tree.
Example: 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.5.0 = sysName.0 (the device's hostname).

front.text: How does traceroute work, and what does each hop's reply tell you?

items: Sender sends probe (UDP on Linux, ICMP Echo on Windows) with TTL=1.
First hop router decrements TTL to 0 and sends back ICMP Time Exceeded — revealing its IP.
Sender sends the next probe with TTL=2; second-hop router replies with Time Exceeded, etc.
Process continues until destination replies with ICMP Port Unreachable (UDP) or ICMP Echo Reply (ICMP).
Each * in output means no reply within the timeout — possibly filtered ICMP responses, not necessarily a black hole for data.

front.text: Compare JSON and XML for representing structured data.

rows: Easier for humans and most APIs
More verbose, supports comments and attributes

front.text: What are the key principles of a RESTful API?

items: Client-Server — Clear separation between client and server.
Stateless — Each request contains all information needed; server stores no client session state.
Cacheable — Responses can be cached when appropriate.
Uniform Interface — Standard methods (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) and URIs identify resources.
Layered System — Intermediaries (proxies, load balancers) can be inserted transparently.
Resource-based — Everything is a resource identified by a URI; representations are typically JSON or XML.

front.text: Compare Autonomous, Centralized (WLC), Embedded (Catalyst 9800-CL on switch), and Cloud-managed (Meraki) wireless architectures.

rows: CAPWAP-tunneled to WLC (Local mode) or local (FlexConnect)
Local or tunneled depending on mode

front.text: How does Cisco Catalyst Center use AI/ML for network assurance?

items: Baseline learning — ML continuously builds a baseline of 'normal' for each site, AP, and client.
Anomaly detection — Detects deviations (e.g., unusually low throughput, increased onboarding time) and flags issues automatically.
AI-driven issues — Aggregates correlated symptoms into a single 'issue' with root cause and suggested remediation, instead of dozens of separate alerts.
AI Network Analytics cloud service — Aggregates anonymized telemetry from many customers to compare and benchmark performance.
Predictive insights — Forecast trends like client capacity and AP utilization to drive design changes.

front.text: Compare SLAAC, Stateless DHCPv6, and Stateful DHCPv6 for IPv6 address assignment.

rows: Host generates address from RA prefix + EUI-64 or random
Host SLAAC for address; DHCPv6 only for other options
DHCPv6 server assigns full address (like IPv4 DHCP)

front.text: How does OSPF select its Router ID, and how do you change it without restarting the router?

items: Manually configured router-id under the OSPF process (highest precedence).
Highest IP address on any active loopback interface.
Highest IP address on any active physical interface.

back.text:
router ospf 1
 router-id 1.1.1.1
!
! Apply the new RID without reload
clear ip ospf process
Router ID is selected once at OSPF startup. Use 'clear ip ospf process' to force re-selection — this WILL flap adjacencies.

front.text: When are OSPF virtual links used, and what are the rules for configuring one?

items: Used to connect an area that has no direct adjacency to area 0, or to repair a partitioned backbone.
Configured between two ABRs that share a common non-zero transit area.
The transit area cannot be a stub, totally stubby, NSSA, or totally NSSA area.
Each ABR is configured with the other ABR's Router ID, not interface IP.
Virtual links are considered a temporary fix; redesigning to a proper area 0 is preferred.

back.text:
router ospf 1
 area 1 virtual-link 2.2.2.2

front.text: What problem does iBGP have at scale, and how do route reflectors and confederations solve it?

items: Problem: iBGP requires a full mesh — every iBGP speaker must peer with every other (n*(n-1)/2 sessions). This doesn't scale beyond ~10–20 routers.
Route Reflectors (RRs) — Relax the iBGP no-readvertise rule. Clients peer only with the RR(s); RRs reflect routes between clients, eliminating the full mesh.

Confederations — Split the AS into smaller sub-ASes (member ASes). Within each sub-AS you still need full mesh (or RRs), but between sub-ASes you use eBGP-like sessions while appearing as one AS to outsiders.
RRs are simpler and far more common than confederations.

front.text: What are the BGP route reflector rules for re-advertising prefixes?

items: Routes from a non-client iBGP peer → reflected ONLY to clients.
Routes from a client → reflected to all other clients AND all non-clients.
Routes from an eBGP peer → reflected to all iBGP peers (clients and non-clients), as normal.

back.text: Loop prevention: RRs use the ORIGINATOR_ID and CLUSTER_LIST attributes so an iBGP route is not reflected back into the cluster that originated it.

front.text: What are the four well-known BGP communities, and what does each do?

rows: Do not advertise outside the local AS (kept internal).
Do not advertise to any peer at all.
Do not advertise outside the local sub-AS in a confederation.

front.text: Compare distribute-list, prefix-list, AS-path access-list, and route-map for filtering BGP routes.

rows: Standard or extended ACL on prefixes
Filter by origin AS or path patterns
Combines all of the above + sets attributes
Most flexible — match and modify in one tool

front.text: How does EIGRP support unequal-cost load balancing, and what is the variance command?

items: EIGRP uniquely supports unequal-cost load balancing using the variance command.
Variance is a multiplier applied to the FD of the successor. Any feasible successor with metric ≤ (variance × successor FD) is also installed.
Only feasible successors can be used — the FC (RD < FD) must still be satisfied to prevent loops.
Default variance is 1 (equal-cost only). Maximum variance multiplier is 128.

back.text:
router eigrp 100
 variance 2
 maximum-paths 4

front.text: How many equal-cost paths does OSPF install by default, and how do you change it?

items: Default in modern IOS-XE: up to 4 (or 16 on some platforms) equal-cost paths.
OSPF supports only equal-cost multipath (ECMP), unlike EIGRP which supports unequal-cost via variance.
Change with: maximum-paths <n> under the OSPF process.

back.text:
router ospf 1
 maximum-paths 8

front.text: What are the default STP port costs for 10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, and 100 Gbps under short and long path-cost standards?

back.text: Use 'spanning-tree pathcost method long' on modern networks with 10G+ links so cost differentiation isn't lost (everything ≥1G otherwise has cost 4 or below).

front.text: What is the difference between normal-range and extended-range VLANs?

rows: Cannot be deleted; default native and management VLAN
Token Ring/FDDI legacy, cannot be used or deleted
Required to be in transparent mode (VTP v1/v2); supported by VTP v3

front.text: What are the three Private VLAN port types and three secondary VLAN types?

items: Primary VLAN — Carries traffic from promiscuous ports to all other ports.
Secondary VLAN — Isolated: hosts can only talk to promiscuous ports, never each other.
Secondary VLAN — Community: hosts can talk to each other within the same community VLAN and to promiscuous ports.
Promiscuous port — Can communicate with all hosts in primary, isolated, and community VLANs (typically the default gateway).
Isolated port — Can only talk to promiscuous ports.
Community port — Can talk to other community ports in the same secondary VLAN and to promiscuous ports.

front.text: What are the two STP BPDU types and what does each do?

items: Configuration BPDU — Sent by the root and relayed downstream by other switches every hello interval (default 2s in RSTP). Used for root election, port roles, and topology.
