Topology Change Notification (TCN) BPDU — Sent toward the root when a switch detects a topology change. The root then sends a Configuration BPDU with the TC flag to flush MAC tables.

back.text: BPDUs are sent to the multicast MAC 01:80:C2:00:00:00 and are not forwarded across VLAN/L2 boundaries.

front.text: What is 802.11w (PMF), and what attack does it mitigate?

items: 802.11w / Protected Management Frames (PMF) — Encrypts and integrity-protects management frames (deauth, disassoc, action frames).
Mitigates deauthentication/disassociation flood attacks where an attacker spoofs frames to kick clients off the network.
Required by WPA3.
WLC PMF settings: Disabled, Optional (clients can opt in), or Required (only PMF-capable clients can connect).

front.text: What does Cisco RRM (Radio Resource Management) do, and what are its main components?

items: RRM is a set of WLC algorithms that automatically tune APs' RF parameters across the wireless network.
DCA (Dynamic Channel Assignment) — Selects the best channel for each AP, avoiding interference and overlap.
TPC (Transmit Power Control) — Adjusts AP TX power so coverage cells barely overlap (right-sized cells, less co-channel interference).
Coverage Hole Detection (CHD) — Identifies areas where clients have poor RSSI and increases AP power or alerts admins.
Off-Channel Scanning — APs periodically leave their serving channel to gather RF info, rogue data, and metrics.

front.text: Match these DSCP values to common Cisco QoS classes: EF, AF31, AF41, CS6, CS7.
What are the three steps in the Modular QoS CLI (MQC) configuration model?

items: Class-map — Define what traffic to match (classification).
Policy-map — Define what action to take per class (mark, queue, police, shape).
Service-policy — Apply the policy-map to an interface, tunnel, or VC, in a direction (input/output).

front.text: What problem does NAT-T solve in IPsec, and what UDP port does it use?

items: Problem: ESP (IP protocol 50) doesn't have ports, so PAT/NAT devices can't track multiple ESP sessions. Also, NAT changes IPs that AH integrity covers (breaks AH).
NAT-T (NAT Traversal, RFC 3947) — Detects NAT in the path during IKE Phase 1. If NAT is detected, IKE moves to UDP/4500 and ESP packets are encapsulated inside UDP/4500.
UDP/500 — IKE without NAT-T (or initial detection).
UDP/4500 — IKE + ESP-in-UDP when NAT-T is in use.

front.text: What is Cisco FlexVPN, and how does it differ from DMVPN?

items: FlexVPN is Cisco's unified VPN framework using IKEv2 only.
Supports site-to-site, hub-and-spoke, dynamic spoke-to-spoke (any-to-any), and remote access in a single configuration model.
Uses dynamic Virtual Tunnel Interfaces (dVTIs) and IKEv2 routing for dynamic route distribution between peers.
Compared to DMVPN: DMVPN uses NHRP + GRE + IPsec; FlexVPN uses native IKEv2 + IPsec VTIs (no NHRP/GRE required), with simpler config and standards-based interoperability.

front.text: What is GETVPN, and where is it typically used?

items: Cisco GETVPN (Group Encrypted Transport VPN) — Tunnel-less IPsec encryption that preserves the original IP header.
Uses GDOI (Group Domain of Interpretation) protocol; a Key Server (KS) distributes a single group key to all Group Members (GMs).
Because the original IP header is preserved, multicast and routing protocols natively work across the encrypted overlay.
Best fit: Private MPLS or private IP transports where any-to-any encrypted connectivity is needed without the overhead of point-to-point tunnels.
Not suitable over the Internet because the original (potentially private) IP header is preserved end-to-end.

front.text: Identify these BGP EVPN route types: Type 2, Type 3, Type 5.

rows: Advertises host MAC and (optionally) host IP — primary route type for endpoint reachability.
Used to build the BUM (Broadcast, Unknown unicast, Multicast) replication list per VNI.
Advertises external/aggregate IP prefixes (subnet routes), used for external connectivity and inter-VRF leaking.

front.text: What is an anycast gateway in SD-Access, and what problem does it solve?

items: Every fabric edge node uses the same SVI IP and MAC (anycast) for a given subnet.
Wherever a host roams, its default gateway is always 'one hop away' on the local edge node — no need to update ARP or change gateway.
Eliminates HSRP/VRRP for fabric subnets and supports seamless host mobility.
Enabled automatically by Cisco DNA/Catalyst Center when provisioning fabric VLANs.

front.text: What is OMP in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN, and what types of routes does it carry?

items: OMP (Overlay Management Protocol) — The control-plane protocol between vSmart and edges. Conceptually similar to BGP but runs over DTLS/TLS in the secure control plane.
OMP Route — IP prefixes (LAN-side subnets) learned from edges, advertised to other edges via vSmart.
TLOC (Transport Locator) — Identifies a transport endpoint (system-IP, color, encapsulation). Used to build IPsec tunnels between edges.
Service Route — Advertises services (firewall, IPS, etc.) reachable via specific edges, for service insertion.
vSmart applies policy to OMP routes (e.g., set preference, control which sites can talk to which).

front.text: What is a TLOC color in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN, and why does it matter?

items: Color identifies the type of transport an edge interface uses (e.g., biz-internet, mpls, public-internet, lte).
Colors are tagged 'public' or 'private'. Private colors are used between edges sharing a private transport with no NAT (MPLS); public colors are used over Internet/transports with NAT.
Edges build IPsec tunnels between matching TLOC colors per policy (default is full mesh among compatible TLOCs).
Colors enable application-aware routing decisions (e.g., prefer MPLS for voice, Internet for backup).

front.text: What is the Cisco Catalyst Center Intent API, and how do you authenticate to it?

items: Intent API is the northbound REST API on Catalyst Center under /dna/intent/api/v1/...
Authentication: POST credentials to /dna/system/api/v1/auth/token using HTTP Basic auth (username/password) — receive an X-Auth-Token in the response.
Use that token in the X-Auth-Token header on subsequent requests (token expires, typically after 1 hour).
Common endpoints: /network-device, /site, /template-programmer, /assurance/issues.

back.text:
POST /dna/system/api/v1/auth/token
Authorization: Basic <base64 user:pass>

→ Response: { "Token": "eyJ..." }

GET /dna/intent/api/v1/network-device
X-Auth-Token: eyJ...

front.text: What are the NETCONF datastores: running, candidate, and startup?

rows: Active configuration the device is currently using. Always present.
Working configuration that can be edited atomically and then committed to running. Optional capability.
Configuration loaded at boot. Optional capability; on Cisco devices similar to startup-config.

back.text: With candidate + commit, you can make multiple changes and apply them as one atomic transaction — much safer than direct edits to running.

front.text: What is gNMI, and how does streaming telemetry differ from SNMP polling?

items: gNMI (gRPC Network Management Interface) — A management protocol built on gRPC + Protocol Buffers, modeled with YANG.
Supports get, set, and subscribe operations.
Streaming telemetry — Devices push subscribed data to collectors at high frequency (e.g., every second), rather than the collector polling.
Advantages over SNMP: lower CPU impact at scale, structured data (YANG), much higher data resolution, real-time visibility.

front.text: Compare cadence-based, on-change, and target-defined streaming telemetry subscriptions.

items: Cadence-based (periodic) — Device sends data at a fixed interval (e.g., every 10s). Simplest; useful for counters and metrics.
On-change — Device only sends data when the value changes. Efficient for state that doesn't change often (interface state, BGP neighbor state).
Target-defined — Subscriber requests data and the device chooses the best mode per element (mix of periodic and on-change).

front.text: What is the difference between an SNMP trap and an SNMP inform?

rows: Retransmitted until ACK or retries exhausted
Critical events that must not be lost

front.text: When do you need an IP SLA Responder, and what does it provide?

items: Required for many IP SLA operation types: UDP Jitter, ICMP Jitter, TCP Connect, voice/RTP simulation, etc.
Provides accurate two-way measurement by timestamping packets at ingress and egress on the responder side.
Removes the latency added by software processing at the responder, giving accurate one-way and round-trip metrics.
Configured with: ip sla responder.

back.text:
! Responder side
ip sla responder

! Source side
ip sla 10
 udp-jitter 10.10.10.20 16384 num-packets 100
 frequency 60
ip sla schedule 10 life forever start-time now

front.text: Why do REST APIs use pagination and rate limiting, and how are they typically implemented?

items: Pagination — Limits the size of a single response when the dataset is large. Common patterns: limit/offset, page/size, or cursor/next-token. Often returned via 'next' link in the response or Link header.
Rate limiting — Caps how many API calls a client can make per time window to protect the service. Communicated via headers like X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, Retry-After.
HTTP 429 'Too Many Requests' indicates the client has exceeded the rate limit. Honor the Retry-After header before retrying.

front.text: Compare JSON and YAML, and explain why Ansible uses YAML.

rows: Whitespace/indentation; no quotes for most strings

back.text: YAML is a superset of JSON — any valid JSON is also valid YAML. Ansible chose YAML because playbooks are written and reviewed by humans.

front.text: Show a basic Python script using Netmiko to SSH to a Cisco IOS device and run 'show ip interface brief'.

back.text:
from netmiko import ConnectHandler

device = {
    "device_type": "cisco_ios",
    "host": "10.1.1.1",
    "username": "admin",
    "password": "StrongP@ss",
    "secret": "EnableP@ss",
}

with ConnectHandler(**device) as net_connect:
    net_connect.enable()
    output = net_connect.send_command("show ip interface brief")
    print(output)

front.text: What is VRF route leaking, and what BGP/route-target mechanism enables it in MPLS L3 VPN?

items: Route leaking exchanges routes between VRFs that would normally be isolated.
RD (Route Distinguisher) — 8-byte value prepended to IPv4 prefixes to make them unique across VRFs in MP-BGP. One RD per VRF.
RT (Route Target) — BGP extended community used to control import/export between VRFs. Set RTs on export and match RTs on import to leak routes.
Example: VRF 'shared-services' exports RT 100:100. VRFs that need access import RT 100:100, pulling those prefixes into their RIB.

front.text: What is the difference between centralized and localized policy in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN?

items: Centralized policy — Defined on vManage and applied on vSmart. Manipulates control plane (OMP routes, TLOCs) and data plane (app-aware routing, traffic engineering, service insertion). Affects what edges learn and how traffic is forwarded.
Localized policy — Defined on vManage and pushed to specific edge devices. Implements per-device features like ACLs, QoS, route policies, and BGP/OSPF policies on LAN-side routing.
Rule of thumb: centralized policy = network-wide behavior; localized policy = per-device behavior.

front.text: What is EIRP, and how is it calculated?

items: EIRP (Effective Isotropic Radiated Power) — The total radiated power of a transmitter as if it were radiating equally in all directions.
Formula: EIRP (dBm) = TX power (dBm) − cable/connector loss (dB) + antenna gain (dBi).
Example: 17 dBm radio − 2 dB cable loss + 5 dBi antenna = 20 dBm EIRP.
Regulators (FCC, ETSI) cap maximum EIRP per band/channel; that limit constrains how much power and antenna gain you can combine.

back.text: Rules of thumb: +3 dB ≈ doubles power; +10 dB ≈ 10× power; –3 dB ≈ halves power.

front.text: What are RSSI and SNR in Wi-Fi, and what are good values to target?

items: RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator) — How strong the signal is, in dBm. More negative = weaker. Closer to 0 = stronger.
SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) — Difference between signal and noise floor, in dB. Higher = cleaner signal.
Good targets: RSSI ≥ –67 dBm for voice, ≥ –65 dBm for high-throughput data; SNR ≥ 25 dB for voice, ≥ 20 dB for data.
If RSSI is good but SNR is poor, the issue is interference/noise, not coverage.

front.text: Compare MACsec and IPsec for link encryption.
Compare routed mode, transparent mode, and IDS/IPS modes for a firewall.

items: Routed mode — Firewall is a Layer 3 hop. Each interface is in a different subnet; firewall can perform routing, NAT, VPN.
Transparent mode — Firewall is a 'bump in the wire' Layer 2 device. Same subnet on both sides; firewall does not appear as a hop. Simpler insertion into existing networks.
IDS (passive) — Sniffs traffic via SPAN/TAP and alerts on attacks; cannot block.
IPS (inline) — Sits in the data path and can drop or modify malicious traffic in real time.

front.text: Describe the end-to-end flow of TrustSec: classification, propagation, and enforcement.

items: Classification — Endpoint authenticates (typically via 802.1X to ISE). ISE assigns an SGT based on identity, profile, posture, etc.
Propagation — SGT is carried across the network, either inline (CMD field in frame, hardware support required) or via SXP (TCP-based control-plane protocol mapping IP-to-SGT).
Enforcement — At the egress device (switch, firewall, router), SGACLs match on source SGT + destination SGT (or SG-FW policies on firewalls) to permit/deny traffic.
ISE remains the policy authority; network devices download/refresh the policy matrix from ISE.

front.text: What is Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) in IPsec, and why is it important?

items: PFS forces a fresh Diffie-Hellman key exchange every time a new IPsec SA (Phase 2) is established or rekeyed.
Without PFS, all Phase 2 SAs derive their keys from the same Phase 1 keying material; if that's compromised, all past/future traffic is vulnerable.
With PFS, even if one key is compromised, prior or future sessions remain secure.
Configured per-peer with: set pfs group <14|19|20|21|...>.

front.text: Compare API key, Bearer token (JWT), and OAuth 2.0 for REST API authentication.

rows: Static long-lived key in header or query param
Simple; weak if leaked, hard to revoke individually
Server issues a signed token (e.g., JWT) after auth; client sends it in Authorization: Bearer
Time-bound, signed, can carry claims (user, roles, scopes)
Delegated framework: client gets access token from authorization server on behalf of a user
Best for third-party access; supports refresh tokens, scopes

front.text: Compare the data plane, control plane, and management plane on a network device.

rows: Forwards packets in hardware/ASIC based on local tables
FIB lookup, ACL enforcement, QoS policing
Builds and maintains the tables the data plane uses
Lets admins/automation configure and monitor the device

front.text: What is Segment Routing (SR-MPLS), and how does it differ from traditional MPLS?

items: Segment Routing — Source-routing approach where the ingress router encodes a list of 'segments' (label stack in SR-MPLS, or IPv6 SRH in SRv6) describing the path.
Eliminates the need for LDP and RSVP-TE in many designs — labels are advertised by the IGP (OSPF/IS-IS extensions).
Each node maintains a Segment Routing Global Block (SRGB) and a label per prefix (Prefix-SID) and per adjacency (Adj-SID).
Benefits: simpler control plane, native traffic engineering, fast reroute (TI-LFA), per-flow path control without per-flow state in transit nodes.

front.text: An engineer enabled spanning-tree bpdufilter globally on PortFast-enabled ports. After a switch was added on one of those ports, you have intermittent broadcast storms. What happened and how do you fix it?

items: Root cause: With BPDU filter on PortFast ports, the switch neither sends nor processes BPDUs on those ports. When another switch is plugged in, no BPDUs are exchanged, so no STP loop detection happens — and a loop forms.
Fix: Replace BPDU filter with BPDU Guard on all access PortFast ports. BPDU Guard err-disables the port the moment a BPDU is received, preventing rogue switches and loops.
Best practice: BPDU Guard on user-facing access ports; never use BPDU filter as a default protection mechanism.

back.text:
spanning-tree portfast default
spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default
no spanning-tree portfast bpdufilter default

front.text: Two routers in the same HSRP group are both showing as Active. List the most likely causes.

items: L2 connectivity broken between the two routers — they can't see each other's hellos, so each declares itself Active. Check the SVI/L2 trunk path.
Mismatched HSRP group numbers — they don't actually belong to the same group.
Different virtual IPs configured on each side.
ACL or CoPP blocking HSRP multicast (224.0.0.2 v1, 224.0.0.102 v2).
Authentication mismatch — routers ignore each other's hellos.
Different HSRP versions (v1 vs v2) on each side.

back.text:
show standby brief
show standby Vlan10 10
debug standby errors

front.text: A wired client moves from Building A's edge node to Building B's edge node within the same SD-Access fabric. Walk through how the client keeps its IP address and how the new edge advertises reachability.

items: Client's gateway is the same anycast SVI on every edge, so its default gateway IP/MAC are unchanged after the move.
Building B's edge detects the new endpoint and registers (EID → RLOC mapping) with the Control Plane node (LISP MS).
The MS updates the mapping; old entry from Building A's edge is overwritten or aged out.
Inbound traffic for the client now hits a fabric edge or border, which queries the MR and gets back Building B's edge as the new RLOC.
Traffic is VXLAN-encapsulated to Building B's edge, which decapsulates and delivers to the client locally.
Client's IP, SGT, and policy follow the endpoint with no client-side reconfiguration.

front.text: Voice quality is poor on a WAN link during congestion. RTP is marked EF on the LAN but appears as DSCP 0 on the WAN side. What should you check?

items: Trust boundary not extended to the WAN edge — somewhere along the path, a port is rewriting/clearing DSCP. Check for 'no auto qos' or default DSCP rewrite at switches in the path.
WAN edge QoS policy not classifying on EF — voice ends up in class-default.
Service-policy not applied in the right direction (must be output on the WAN egress interface).
Tunnel/encapsulation stripping inner DSCP — verify QoS pre-classify on tunnels (qos pre-classify).
Service provider rewriting DSCP — confirm SP honors customer markings, or remark to whatever the SP requires (often CoS or MPLS EXP).

front.text: What is the flooding scope of each OSPF LSA type (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7)?

rows: Throughout the OSPF domain (except into/out of stub/totally-stubby boundaries)
Throughout the OSPF domain (except stub-type areas)
Throughout the OSPF domain (NEVER into stub, totally stubby, NSSA, or totally NSSA)
Within the NSSA only; converted to Type 5 by the NSSA ABR on egress

front.text: Define the WLC interface types: Management, AP-Manager, Virtual, Service Port, and Dynamic.

items: Management interface — In-band, used for CAPWAP control traffic with APs and admin access (HTTPS/SSH/RADIUS).
AP-Manager interface — Used for CAPWAP data traffic to/from APs (older AireOS designs; on Catalyst 9800 it's collapsed into the management interface in many deployments).
Virtual interface — Used for client-facing services like web auth, DHCP relay, and mobility. Typically a non-routable IP (e.g., 1.1.1.1).
Service Port — Out-of-band management port (separate physical port) used for system management, independent of the data plane.
Dynamic interface — Per-VLAN interface used to bind a WLAN to a specific client VLAN.

front.text: What is the difference between ASM (Any Source Multicast) and SSM (Source Specific Multicast)?

rows: Internet broadcast/streaming where receivers know the source

front.text: Compare Static RP, Auto-RP, and BSR for distributing the RP-to-group mapping in PIM-SM.

rows: Manually configured on every PIM router
Candidate RPs send announcements to 224.0.1.39; Mapping Agent selects RP and announces to 224.0.1.40
Candidate RPs unicast to BSR; BSR floods RP set in Bootstrap messages to 224.0.0.13

back.text: Auto-RP is Cisco-only and predates BSR. BSR is the standards-based equivalent and is preferred in mixed-vendor environments.

front.text: What is the Cisco Catalyst Center Template Editor used for, and what templating language does it use?

items: Used to push consistent CLI configurations to one or many devices, with variables for site-specific values.
Supports both Velocity and Jinja2 templating syntax for variables, conditionals, and loops.
Templates are attached to Network Profiles or applied directly during Provisioning workflows.
Composite templates can chain multiple templates together for complex deployments (e.g., L3 underlay + management).

back.text:
interface Loopback0
 description $loopback_desc
 ip address $loopback_ip 255.255.255.255

front.text: How does OSPF elect the DR and BDR on a multi-access network, and is the election preemptive?

items: Highest OSPF interface priority wins (default 1; range 0-255). Priority 0 means the router will never become DR/BDR.
If priorities tie, highest Router ID wins.
BDR is elected first, then DR is selected from remaining routers (or BDR is promoted on DR failure).
Election is NOT preemptive — a new router with a higher priority will not displace the existing DR/BDR.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip ospf priority 100

front.text: What are the OSPF Hello and Dead timer defaults on broadcast and non-broadcast network types, and what happens if they don't match?

items: Broadcast / Point-to-Point: Hello 10s, Dead 40s.
Non-Broadcast (NBMA) / Point-to-Multipoint: Hello 30s, Dead 120s.
Both timers must MATCH between neighbors or the adjacency will not form (stuck in Init or Down).
Verify with: show ip ospf interface <int>.

front.text: Show how to configure OSPFv2 MD5 authentication on an interface.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip ospf message-digest-key 1 md5 StrongKey
 ip ospf authentication message-digest
!
! Or globally per area:
router ospf 1
 area 0 authentication message-digest
Both ends of the link must use the same key ID and password. Modern designs prefer SHA via OSPFv3 or key chains for rotation.

front.text: Show how to configure EIGRP MD5 authentication using a key chain.

back.text:
key chain EIGRP_KEYS
 key 1
  key-string SuperSecret
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip authentication mode eigrp 100 md5
 ip authentication key-chain eigrp 100 EIGRP_KEYS
EIGRP named mode supports stronger HMAC-SHA-256 authentication and per-AF interface configuration.

front.text: What does 'clear ip bgp * soft' do, and why prefer it over 'clear ip bgp *'?

items: clear ip bgp * — Hard reset that tears down BGP sessions and re-establishes them. Causes a routing outage during convergence.
clear ip bgp * soft — Soft reset that re-applies inbound/outbound policies without tearing down the session.
soft out — Re-sends routes to the peer with current outbound policy.
soft in — Requires either Route Refresh capability (default on modern IOS) or 'neighbor X soft-reconfiguration inbound' which stores received routes in memory.
Always prefer soft resets in production to avoid traffic loss.

front.text: What does the 'aggregate-address' command do in BGP, and what's the difference between summary-only and as-set?

back.text:
router bgp 65001
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  aggregate-address 10.0.0.0 255.255.0.0 summary-only as-set

items: aggregate-address — Creates a summary prefix in BGP, so the router can advertise one shorter prefix instead of many longer ones.
summary-only — Suppresses the more-specific component routes from being advertised to peers; only the aggregate is sent.
as-set — Includes all component routes' AS_PATH info as an unordered AS_SET in the aggregate. Helps prevent routing loops by preserving AS-path information.
By default, both the aggregate AND the more-specifics are advertised; use summary-only to suppress specifics.

front.text: Why is 'neighbor X update-source Loopback0' commonly used for iBGP peerings, and when is 'ebgp-multihop' also required?

items: iBGP peerings often use loopbacks as endpoints because loopbacks are stable and reachable over multiple paths (resilient to interface failures).
update-source Loopback0 forces BGP to source TCP/179 from the loopback IP so the receiving peer recognizes the connection.
For eBGP peerings between non-directly-connected loopbacks, you ALSO need ebgp-multihop because eBGP has a default TTL of 1.
iBGP doesn't need ebgp-multihop because iBGP uses TTL 255 by default.

back.text:
router bgp 65001
 neighbor 2.2.2.2 remote-as 65002
 neighbor 2.2.2.2 ebgp-multihop 2
 neighbor 2.2.2.2 update-source Loopback0

front.text: Two EIGRP routers won't form an adjacency, and you see %DUAL-5-NBRCHANGE messages with reason 'K-value mismatch'. What does this mean and how do you fix it?

items: Both EIGRP neighbors must agree on the K-values used in the metric formula. If they differ, no adjacency forms.
Default K-values: K1=1, K2=0, K3=1, K4=0, K5=0.
Fix: Make K-values match on both sides with: metric weights 0 K1 K2 K3 K4 K5.
Other reasons adjacencies fail: AS number mismatch, authentication mismatch, mismatched primary subnet, network statement missing.

back.text:
show ip eigrp neighbors
show ip protocols
debug eigrp packets terminate

front.text: What is AS-path prepending, and why use it instead of MED to influence inbound traffic?

items: AS-path prepending adds your own AS number multiple times to the AS_PATH attribute on outbound advertisements, making the path appear longer to upstream BGP speakers.
Used to influence INBOUND traffic on multi-homed designs (e.g., make link A primary, link B backup).
MED is also used for inbound influence but only works between two ASes (one hop) and is non-transitive — many providers ignore MED.
Prepending is universally honored because AS_PATH length is step 4 of the BGP best-path algorithm.
Configured with route-maps: set as-path prepend <my-AS> [<my-AS>...].

back.text:
route-map BACKUP_OUT permit 10
 set as-path prepend 65001 65001 65001
!
router bgp 65001
 neighbor 203.0.113.1 route-map BACKUP_OUT out

front.text: What did UplinkFast and BackboneFast do in legacy PVST+, and why aren't they needed with RSTP?

items: UplinkFast — Cisco proprietary access-layer enhancement: when a switch loses its root port, it immediately puts an alternate port into Forwarding (instead of waiting 30s).
BackboneFast — Speeds up convergence on indirect link failures by detecting inferior BPDUs and skipping the 20-second Max Age timer.
Both are unnecessary in RSTP because RSTP natively does sub-second convergence using port roles (Alternate, Backup) and the proposal/agreement handshake.
Modern designs use Rapid-PVST+ or MST instead of legacy 802.1D + UplinkFast/BackboneFast.

front.text: What does PortFast (RSTP edge port) do, and what is the recommended pairing for security?

items: PortFast — Skips the Listening and Learning states and transitions the port to Forwarding immediately when the link comes up.
Used on access ports connected to end hosts (PCs, printers, IP phones) that should never receive BPDUs.
Recommended pairing: BPDU Guard. If a BPDU is received on a PortFast port, BPDU Guard err-disables the port to prevent a rogue switch from creating a loop.
If a PortFast port receives a BPDU without BPDU Guard, it loses edge status and behaves like a normal RSTP port.

back.text:
spanning-tree portfast default
spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default

front.text: An EtherChannel formed inconsistently and one switch is err-disabled with reason 'channel-misconfig'. What's the cause?

items: Cause: One side has an EtherChannel configured (or one side is using LACP/PAgP) while the other side has individual ports — or there's an inconsistent set of bundled ports.
EtherChannel Misconfig Guard (enabled by default) detects this and err-disables the channel to prevent loops.
Verify both sides have matching: speed/duplex, allowed VLANs, native VLAN, switchport mode, MTU, channel mode, channel-group number.
Recover with: shutdown / no shutdown after fixing the mismatch, or 'errdisable recovery cause channel-misconfig'.

back.text:
show etherchannel summary
show interfaces status err-disabled
show spanning-tree inconsistentports

front.text: What is the VTPv3 'primary server' concept, and how does it prevent the classic VTP database overwrite problem?

items: Classic VTP problem: A switch with a higher revision number could overwrite the entire VLAN database in the domain — even by accident (e.g., adding a switch from a lab).
VTPv3 introduces: Server, Client, Transparent, Off, plus the Primary Server role.
Only a Primary Server can make changes; you must explicitly take the role with: vtp primary [vlan | mst].
Primary status is non-persistent across reload, so an accidental higher-revision switch cannot overwrite the database.
Best practice: keep VTP off (transparent or off mode) unless you have a specific need for it.

front.text: What is the implicit policy at the end of an IPv6 ACL, and what implicit permits exist before it?

items: IPv6 ACLs end with an implicit deny ipv6 any any (just like IPv4).
BUT, before that deny, IPv6 ACLs implicitly permit IPv6 Neighbor Discovery: permit icmp any any nd-na and permit icmp any any nd-ns.
This is critical — without those implicit permits, an IPv6 ACL would break NDP and the network couldn't function.
If you write your own deny ipv6 any any explicitly above the end of the ACL, you may break NDP. Always permit NS/NA explicitly when overriding.

front.text: Compare the IPv6 and IPv4 header. What was simplified or removed?
Show how to configure an IPv6 default route via a link-local next hop on a specific interface.

back.text:
ipv6 unicast-routing
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ipv6 address 2001:db8::1/64
!
! Default route via link-local next hop (must specify exit interface)
ipv6 route ::/0 GigabitEthernet0/0 FE80::1
Link-local next hops require the exit interface because FE80::/10 addresses are not unique outside their link.

front.text: What are the four UNII bands in 5 GHz, and which require DFS?

back.text: DFS (Dynamic Frequency Selection) requires APs to detect radar and vacate the channel within seconds. Wi-Fi 6E adds UNII-5 through UNII-8 in the 6 GHz band.

front.text: What is Cisco CleanAir, and what does it identify that traditional spectrum analysis can't?

items: CleanAir is a Cisco AP feature using a dedicated spectrum-analysis ASIC to detect non-Wi-Fi interferers in real time.
Identifies sources by type: microwave ovens, Bluetooth devices, cordless phones, video bridges, jammers, baby monitors, motion detectors, etc.
Reports interference severity (AQ, Air Quality index) and exact location when integrated with CMX/DNA Spaces.
RRM uses CleanAir data via Event-Driven RRM (ED-RRM) to immediately move APs off severely interfered channels.

front.text: What's the difference between trusting CoS, trusting DSCP, and untrusted with markdown?

items: Trust CoS — Use the 802.1p CoS bits in the 802.1Q tag from the connected device. Used on trunk ports to trusted switches/phones.
Trust DSCP — Use the IP DSCP value already on incoming packets. Used on Layer 3 boundaries between trusted devices.
Untrusted (with markdown) — Don't trust incoming markings; remark all traffic to DSCP 0 (default) or to a class-default value before forwarding. Used on user access ports.
Common pattern: 'mls qos trust device cisco-phone' on access ports — CoS is trusted only when a Cisco phone is detected via CDP.

front.text: An access port hosts a Cisco IP phone with a PC daisy-chained behind it. How do you trust the phone's voice markings while not trusting whatever the PC marks?

items: Use conditional trust with CDP to trust CoS only when a Cisco IP phone is detected.
On the phone, voice traffic gets CoS 5 / DSCP EF; the phone re-marks PC traffic to CoS 0 by default before passing it through.
Place the PC in the data VLAN and the phone in the voice VLAN, both via the same access port.
If CDP doesn't detect a phone, the trust state collapses and incoming markings are zeroed.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10
 switchport voice vlan 110
 auto qos voip cisco-phone

front.text: If a shaper is configured with CIR 10 Mbps and Tc 10 ms, what is the Bc, and how is it calculated?

items: Bc = CIR × (Tc / 1000)
That means each 10 ms interval, up to 100,000 bits (12,500 bytes) can be sent.
Smaller Tc = smoother, less bursty output but more CPU; larger Tc = burstier, less CPU.
Tc = 10 ms is a common 'voice-friendly' value for shaping links carrying real-time traffic.

front.text: What is the difference between IKE Phase 1 and Phase 2, and what is built in each?

rows: Authenticate peers and build a secure management channel
ISAKMP SA used to negotiate Phase 2
Two unidirectional IPsec SAs (one each direction) for protected user traffic

back.text: IKEv2 collapses the lengthy IKEv1 multi-message exchange into a single 4-message exchange (IKE_SA_INIT + IKE_AUTH).

front.text: Compare these Cisco VPN solutions on scale and topology: Site-to-Site IPsec, DMVPN, GETVPN, FlexVPN, Remote Access.

rows: Private MPLS / IP transports needing native multicast

front.text: Show a basic VRF-Lite configuration, including a static route inside the VRF and how to ping inside that VRF.

back.text:
vrf definition CUSTOMER_A
 address-family ipv4
 exit-address-family
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 vrf forwarding CUSTOMER_A
 ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0
!
ip route vrf CUSTOMER_A 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 10.1.1.254
!
! Verification
show vrf
show ip route vrf CUSTOMER_A
ping vrf CUSTOMER_A 10.1.1.254
Without 'vrf forwarding', the interface stays in the global routing table. Assigning the VRF causes any existing IP to be removed — re-add it after the vrf assignment.

front.text: How does LISP support endpoint mobility without renumbering?

items: LISP separates location (RLOC) from identity (EID). The endpoint keeps its EID (IP address) when it moves.
When an EID moves to a new site, the new ETR registers the EID-to-RLOC mapping with the Map Server.
Map Server updates the mapping; cached entries on remote ITRs eventually time out or are signaled to refresh.
Result: endpoints keep their IP addresses across sites — useful for VM mobility, SD-Access roaming, and disaster recovery.

front.text: What is BUM traffic in VXLAN, and how is it replicated in multicast vs ingress replication mode?

items: BUM = Broadcast, Unknown unicast, Multicast traffic that must reach all VTEPs in a VNI.
Multicast underlay — Each VNI maps to an underlay multicast group; VTEPs that have endpoints in the VNI join the group via IGMP/PIM. BUM is sent once and replicated by the underlay.

Ingress (head-end) replication — Source VTEP makes a copy of each BUM frame and unicasts a separate VXLAN packet to every other VTEP that has the VNI. Simpler underlay, but more bandwidth at the source.
EVPN can advertise (Type 3 routes) which VTEPs need a copy, enabling efficient ingress replication.

front.text: What is a Virtual Network (VN) in SD-Access, and how does it relate to VRFs and VLANs?

items: VN (Virtual Network) — Logical macro-segment in the fabric, equivalent to a VRF on the underlying devices.
Each VN provides isolated routing and address space (overlapping IPs allowed across VNs).
Inside a VN, multiple IP pools (subnets/VLANs) can exist; SGTs provide micro-segmentation within and across VNs.
Examples: Corporate VN, Guest VN, IoT VN — each with its own routing table and policy.
DNAC automates VRF, BGP-EVPN, and LISP configuration when you create a VN.

front.text: What is the SD-Access fabric border 'handoff' to a fusion router, and why is it sometimes called the 2-box approach?

items: The fabric border keeps each VN as a separate VRF. To allow inter-VN communication or shared services, you need to leak routes between VRFs.
2-box approach — The border is one device; a separate 'fusion' router/firewall is connected via per-VN sub-interfaces and performs the route leaking using BGP route-targets or VRF route leaking.
1-box approach — The border itself does VRF leaking (less common; reduces security separation).
Fusion router pattern is preferred because it places a security inspection point (firewall) between VNs.

front.text: What is Application-Aware Routing (AAR) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN, and what SLA metrics does it use?

items: AAR is a centralized data policy that selects the best path per application based on real-time SLA measurements across each transport.
SLA metrics: Loss (%), Latency (ms), Jitter (ms).
BFD probes between edges measure these metrics on every IPsec tunnel continuously.
Define an SLA class (e.g., voice = loss <1%, latency <150ms, jitter <30ms) and a list of preferred TLOC colors.
If the preferred path violates the SLA, traffic is rerouted to a transport that meets the SLA — automatically and per-flow.

front.text: What role does BFD play in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN, and how does it differ from typical BFD usage?

items: BFD runs automatically inside every IPsec tunnel between edges (no manual config needed).
Used for both liveness detection AND continuous measurement of loss, latency, and jitter for AAR.
If BFD declares the tunnel down, the edge withdraws the corresponding TLOC and traffic moves to alternate paths.
Default hello timer is around 1 second; multiplier 7 (so ~7 seconds to declare a tunnel down). Tunable for faster failover.

front.text: Show a Cisco IOS SNMPv3 configuration with an authPriv user.

back.text:
snmp-server group MONITOR_GRP v3 priv
snmp-server user MONITOR_USER MONITOR_GRP v3 auth sha StrongAuthPass priv aes 128 StrongPrivPass
snmp-server host 10.10.10.20 version 3 priv MONITOR_USER
snmp-server enable traps
v3 priv = auth + encryption (authPriv). v3 auth = auth only (authNoPriv). v3 noauth = no auth, no encryption (avoid).

front.text: Name five common IP SLA operation types and what each measures.

items: icmp-echo — Basic reachability and round-trip time (does not require responder).
udp-jitter — Round-trip latency, jitter, packet loss for UDP traffic (requires responder).
tcp-connect — TCP three-way handshake completion time to a destination port (requires responder for accuracy).
dns — DNS query response time to a specific server.
http — HTTP GET response time to a URL, including DNS resolution and TCP setup.
icmp-jitter — Like udp-jitter but using ICMP timestamps; useful when UDP probing is blocked.

front.text: Why use conditional debug, and how do you limit a debug to a specific interface or peer?

items: Conditional debug filters debug output to the matched condition only — drastically reducing CPU impact and log noise.
Limits can be by interface, IP address, MAC, VLAN, or other conditions depending on the feature.
Always enable terminal monitor (vty), set the condition, then enable the debug. Remove the condition with 'no debug condition <id>' when done.

back.text:
debug condition interface GigabitEthernet0/0
debug ip ospf adj
!
! Or
debug condition ip 10.1.1.2
debug ip packet

front.text: How do you configure local logging buffer with millisecond timestamps and view the log?

back.text:
service timestamps log datetime msec localtime show-timezone
logging buffered 64000 informational
!
! View the buffer
show logging
!
! Clear the buffer
clear logging
Always enable msec timestamps for accurate troubleshooting. Pair with NTP so events across devices can be correlated.

front.text: Show a curl command that retrieves all network devices from Catalyst Center using a token.

back.text:
# Step 1: Get a token
curl -X POST -k \
  -u 'admin:Cisco123!' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  https://dnac.example.com/dna/system/api/v1/auth/token

# Step 2: Use the token to list devices
curl -X GET -k \
  -H 'X-Auth-Token: eyJ...' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  https://dnac.example.com/dna/intent/api/v1/network-device

front.text: Show a valid JSON payload that creates a new site in a network management API.
Show a Python for loop that iterates a list of dictionaries (interfaces) and prints any whose status is 'down'.
What's the difference between the Ansible modules ios_command, ios_config, and ios_facts?

items: cisco.ios.ios_command — Run arbitrary IOS show/exec commands and return the output for parsing.
cisco.ios.ios_config — Push configuration lines or full config blocks idempotently. Supports parents (interface contexts) and conditional updates.
cisco.ios.ios_facts — Gather structured inventory data (version, interfaces, hardware) into ansible_facts variables.

back.text:
- name: Configure interface
  cisco.ios.ios_config:
    parents: "interface GigabitEthernet0/1"
    lines:
      - "description Uplink"
      - "switchport mode trunk"

front.text: Define these Puppet and Chef terms: manifest, catalog, recipe, cookbook, role.

items: Puppet manifest — A .pp file containing Puppet DSL code declaring desired state for resources.
Puppet catalog — Compiled, node-specific document describing the desired state, sent to the agent for enforcement.
Chef recipe — A Ruby file describing resources and their desired state.
Chef cookbook — Collection of related recipes, templates, files, and metadata.
Chef role — Logical grouping of cookbooks/recipes applied to nodes serving a similar function (e.g., 'webserver').

front.text: What is the difference between Cisco ASA, FTD, and Secure Firewall Threat Defense?

items: Cisco ASA — Classic stateful firewall image with VPN and limited application inspection. Managed by ASDM/CLI/CSM.
Cisco Firepower Threat Defense (FTD) — Unified image combining ASA features and Firepower NGFW/NGIPS. Managed by FMC (Firepower Management Center) or FDM (on-box).
Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense — Current naming for FTD platform; same image, modern branding under the 'Cisco Secure' family.
Choose Secure Firewall Threat Defense for new deployments — ASA software is in maintenance for most use cases.

front.text: What is a Secure Internet Gateway (SIG), and how does Cisco Umbrella implement one?

items: SIG = Cloud-delivered security stack that protects users wherever they are, replacing on-premises web/email gateways.
Umbrella SIG components: DNS-layer security, secure web gateway (SWG), cloud-delivered firewall (CDFW), CASB, and threat intelligence from Talos.
Connection methods: DNS forwarding, IPsec/GRE tunnels from SD-WAN edges, AnyConnect roaming module on endpoints.
Component of SASE (Secure Access Service Edge) — combines networking (SD-WAN) and security in the cloud.

front.text: What is SASE, and what are its main building blocks?

items: SASE (Secure Access Service Edge) — A cloud-delivered architecture combining WAN networking and network security as a service.
Core building blocks: SD-WAN, SWG (Secure Web Gateway), CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker), ZTNA (Zero Trust Network Access), and FWaaS (Firewall-as-a-Service).
Identity-driven and policy-based: same policies apply whether the user is in the office, at home, or on the road.
Cisco's SASE solution combines Catalyst SD-WAN + Umbrella SIG + Duo + Secure Endpoint.

front.text: What is the Zero Trust security model, and what are its three pillars in Cisco's framework?

items: Zero Trust assumes no implicit trust based on network location. Every access request is authenticated, authorized, and continuously verified.
Cisco's three Zero Trust pillars:
1. Workforce — Verify users and their devices (e.g., Duo MFA, posture checks).
2. Workloads — Secure application-to-application communications (e.g., Tetration / Secure Workload).
3. Workplace — Verify any device connecting to the network (e.g., ISE, TrustSec, SD-Access).

front.text: What is Cisco Duo, and what authentication factors does MFA typically combine?

items: Cisco Duo — Cloud-delivered MFA and Zero Trust access service. Provides MFA, device posture, and adaptive access policies.
MFA combines two or more factors:
Something you know — password, PIN.
Something you have — phone (push/OTP), hardware token, smart card.
Something you are — biometrics (fingerprint, face).
Strong MFA combines factors from different categories — e.g., password (know) + Duo Push (have).

front.text: What are the two MACsec key derivation modes, and when is each used?

items: PSK-based (manual) — Static Pre-Shared Key configured locally on both ends. MKA derives session keys from the PSK. Used on switch-to-switch trunks where 802.1X isn't practical.

802.1X-EAP-TLS based — Switch authenticates the endpoint via 802.1X EAP-TLS; the MACsec session key (CAK) is derived as part of the authentication. Used on access ports where users/devices authenticate (e.g., Cisco AnyConnect with NAM, or supplicants supporting MACsec).

front.text: What is a TrustSec policy matrix, and where is it stored and enforced?

items: Policy matrix is a 2-dimensional grid: source SGTs (rows) × destination SGTs (columns). Each cell contains a permit/deny SGACL.
Stored on Cisco ISE; downloaded to network devices that need to enforce traffic between specific SGT pairs.
Enforcement happens at the egress device (the destination switch/firewall/router) by matching the source SGT in the frame and the destination SGT of the egress port.
Allows policy without IPs, ACLs, or VLANs — making changes easy as topology shifts.

front.text: What telemetry sources can Cisco Secure Network Analytics ingest, and what kinds of detections does it produce?

items: Telemetry: NetFlow v5/v9, IPFIX, sFlow, NSEL (ASA), proxy logs, AnyConnect endpoint telemetry, and ETA-enhanced flows.
Detections: Behavioral anomalies (port scans, data exfiltration, beaconing), policy violations, encrypted traffic threats (via ETA/Cognitive), insider threat indicators.
Integrates with ISE for user-context (username, device, location) and with pxGrid/SecureX for orchestrated response.
Provides forensic flow query for after-the-fact investigation.

front.text: Which legacy/unused services should be disabled on Cisco IOS devices for hardening?

items: no ip http server / no ip http secure-server (use NETCONF/RESTCONF or SSH instead).
no service config (don't auto-load config from network).
no ip source-route (prevent source-routed packets).
no ip directed-broadcast (per interface; prevents Smurf attacks).
no service finger / no ip finger.
no cdp run on Internet-facing interfaces (or globally if not needed).
no service pad.
Disable unused VTY transports: transport input ssh.

front.text: What's the difference between banner motd, banner login, and banner exec, and why are they important?

items: banner motd — Message of the day; shown before login prompt to all connections.
banner login — Shown after motd but before login prompt.
banner exec — Shown after successful login when the EXEC session starts.
Importance: Legal notices warning unauthorized users are often required for prosecution in many jurisdictions. Avoid using 'Welcome' — use 'Authorized access only' style language.

back.text:
banner motd ^
WARNING: This system is for authorized users only.
All activity is monitored and logged.
Unauthorized access is prohibited.
^

front.text: What is the OSPF LSA refresh interval and MaxAge, and what happens when an LSA reaches MaxAge?

items: LSA Refresh interval — Originating router refreshes its LSA every 1800 seconds (30 minutes), even if nothing changed.
LSA MaxAge — 3600 seconds (1 hour). If an LSA isn't refreshed and reaches MaxAge, it's flushed from the LSDB.
When the originating router flushes an LSA, it sends one last update with age = MaxAge to tell other routers to remove it.
Helps OSPF clean up stale LSAs from routers that have left the area.

front.text: What is OSPF SPF throttling, and what are the three timers used?

items: SPF throttling delays SPF recomputation when topology changes occur, preventing CPU thrash during flapping events.
spf-start (initial wait) — Time to wait before running the first SPF after a change.
spf-hold (minimum hold time) — Minimum time between two consecutive SPF runs; doubles on each event up to spf-max.
spf-max (maximum wait time) — Cap on the hold timer.
If the network is stable for long enough, the hold timer resets back to the initial wait time.

back.text:
router ospf 1
 timers throttle spf 50 200 5000

front.text: How does EIGRP perform manual summarization, and what is special about how the summary is announced?

items: Manual summarization is configured per-interface — only routes advertised out that interface are summarized.
EIGRP automatically installs a Null0 route for the summary on the local router to prevent loops.
By default, automatic summarization at classful boundaries is DISABLED in modern IOS — manual summarization is required.
Summary metric is the lowest metric of the component routes.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip summary-address eigrp 100 10.1.0.0 255.255.0.0
!
! EIGRP named mode equivalent
router eigrp CORP
 address-family ipv4 unicast autonomous-system 100
  af-interface GigabitEthernet0/0
   summary-address 10.1.0.0 255.255.0.0

front.text: What are the three EIGRP tables, and what does each contain?

rows: Adjacent EIGRP neighbors, their hold timers, queue counts
All routes learned from neighbors, including successors and feasible successors with metrics
Best routes (successors) installed by EIGRP into the RIB

front.text: How does BGP prevent routing loops in eBGP vs iBGP?

items: eBGP — Loop prevention via AS_PATH. If a router sees its own AS in a received AS_PATH, the route is rejected.
iBGP — Loop prevention via the split-horizon rule: a route learned from one iBGP peer is NOT re-advertised to another iBGP peer. This is what forces a full mesh (or RR/confederations).
Route Reflector loop prevention — Uses ORIGINATOR_ID and CLUSTER_LIST attributes; if a route reflector sees its own cluster ID in CLUSTER_LIST, it drops the route.

front.text: What ranges are reserved as BGP private AS numbers, and what does 'remove-private-as' do?

items: 16-bit private AS range: 64512–65534.
32-bit (4-byte) private AS range: 4200000000–4294967294.
remove-private-as — On eBGP outbound, strips private AS numbers from the AS_PATH before advertising to public peers.
Common in service-provider edge designs where customers use private AS numbers but the SP must hide them from the global Internet.

back.text:
router bgp 65001
 neighbor 203.0.113.1 remote-as 100
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 203.0.113.1 remove-private-as

front.text: What triggers an STP Topology Change (TC) event, and what's the impact on MAC address tables?

items: Trigger: A non-edge port transitions to or from Forwarding (e.g., link comes up/down on a switch-to-switch port).
Edge ports (PortFast) coming up/down do NOT generate TCs — that's a key reason for PortFast on access ports.
When a TC occurs, switches reduce the MAC aging timer to the forward delay (15s) so stale MACs flush quickly.
Excessive TCs cause MAC table thrashing → unicast flooding → traffic spikes. Hunt for the source switch that's flapping interfaces.
Verify with: show spanning-tree detail (look for 'topology change count' and 'last change').

front.text: How is the STP bridge ID structured, and what priority does 'root primary' set?

items: Bridge ID = 4-bit priority (in increments of 4096) + 12-bit extended system ID (the VLAN number) + 6-byte MAC.
Default priority: 32768 + VLAN.
spanning-tree vlan X root primary → priority 24576 (or 4096 lower than current root).
spanning-tree vlan X root secondary → priority 28672.
Manually: spanning-tree vlan X priority <0–61440 in 4096 increments>.

front.text: What is the LACP system priority used for, and how does the LACP port priority affect bundling?

items: LACP system priority + system MAC = LACP system ID. Lower system priority wins; this device's port priorities then govern which links bundle.
Default LACP system priority is 32768.
LACP port priority — Determines which ports are active vs hot-standby when more ports are configured than the bundle limit (default limit 8 active, hot-standby up to 8 more).
Lower port priority = more preferred to be active. Default port priority is 32768.

back.text:
lacp system-priority 100
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 lacp port-priority 100
 channel-group 1 mode active

front.text: What is VLAN pruning on a trunk, and what's the difference between manual pruning and VTP pruning?

items: VLAN pruning prevents broadcast/unknown-unicast for a VLAN from being sent across trunks where that VLAN has no active ports.
Manual pruning — Use 'switchport trunk allowed vlan' to explicitly list VLANs that are allowed on the trunk. Most common modern method.
VTP pruning — Dynamic, automatic pruning negotiated by VTP. Globally enabled on a VTP server.
Manual pruning is preferred for explicit, predictable behavior; VTP pruning is rarely used in modern designs.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30,110
!
! VTP-based
vtp pruning

front.text: What does the 'ip helper-address' command do, and which UDP services does it forward by default?

items: ip helper-address converts subnet broadcasts on the configured interface into unicast packets sent to the helper IP. Most commonly used to relay DHCP requests across subnets.
By default it forwards 8 UDP services: TFTP (69), DNS (53), Time (37), TACACS (49), NetBIOS Name Server (137), NetBIOS Datagram Server (138), BootP/DHCP server (67), BootP/DHCP client (68).
Add or remove forwarded ports with: 'ip forward-protocol udp <port>' / 'no ip forward-protocol udp <port>'.

back.text:
interface Vlan10
 ip address 10.1.10.1 255.255.255.0
 ip helper-address 10.10.10.50

front.text: What does DHCP Snooping do, and what's the difference between a trusted and an untrusted port?

items: DHCP Snooping is an L2 security feature that filters DHCP messages and builds a binding table (MAC, IP, VLAN, port, lease) used by other features (DAI, IP Source Guard).
Trusted port — DHCP server-side traffic (DHCPOFFER/DHCPACK) is allowed. Configure on uplinks toward the legitimate DHCP server.
Untrusted port — DHCP server messages from these ports are dropped. Default state for access ports — protects against rogue DHCP servers.
Also rate-limits DHCP messages and validates DHCP option-82 information.

back.text:
ip dhcp snooping
ip dhcp snooping vlan 10,20,30
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/24
 description Uplink to DHCP server
 ip dhcp snooping trust

front.text: What is Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI), and what feature must be enabled first?

items: DAI inspects ARP packets in a VLAN, validating each ARP against the DHCP Snooping binding table. Invalid ARPs (spoofed sender IP/MAC) are dropped.
Stops ARP poisoning / ARP spoofing attacks (e.g., man-in-the-middle attempts).
Requires DHCP Snooping to be enabled first so the binding table exists.
ARP ACLs can supplement the binding table for static hosts.
Trusted ports (uplinks, server links) bypass DAI checks.

back.text:
ip arp inspection vlan 10,20,30
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/24
 ip arp inspection trust

front.text: What is IP Source Guard, and what does it protect against?

items: IP Source Guard restricts traffic on an access port to packets with a source IP/MAC that matches the DHCP Snooping binding table for that port.
Prevents IP/MAC spoofing — a host can only send traffic using the IP it actually leased via DHCP.
Implemented as a per-port PACL that's automatically updated when DHCP Snooping bindings change.
Requires DHCP Snooping to be enabled in the same VLAN.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip verify source port-security

front.text: What are the three port security violation modes, and how does each react to a violation?

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 switchport mode access
 switchport port-security
 switchport port-security maximum 2
 switchport port-security violation restrict
 switchport port-security mac-address sticky

front.text: What does storm control do, and what types of traffic can it limit?

items: Storm control rate-limits broadcast, multicast, and unknown-unicast traffic on a port to prevent broadcast storms from overwhelming the network.
Configurable as a percentage of bandwidth, packets per second, or bits per second.
Can take action when threshold is exceeded: drop traffic above the rate, shutdown the port, or send an SNMP trap.
Useful on access ports where a misbehaving NIC or loop downstream would otherwise saturate uplinks.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 storm-control broadcast level 1.00
 storm-control multicast level 1.00
 storm-control action shutdown

front.text: What IPv6 First-Hop Security features mitigate rogue Router Advertisements and DHCPv6 servers?

items: IPv6 RA Guard — Drops Router Advertisements received on access ports (only allow on uplink/server ports). IPv6 equivalent of preventing rogue routers.
DHCPv6 Guard — Drops DHCPv6 server messages on access ports.
ND Inspection / IPv6 Snooping — Builds an IPv6 binding table (similar to DHCP snooping), used by other FHS features.
IPv6 Source Guard — Filters traffic against the binding table (analogous to IP Source Guard).
IPv6 Destination Guard — Drops traffic destined for unknown addresses, preventing NDP cache exhaustion attacks.

front.text: What is Bridge Assurance, and how is it different from Loop Guard?

items: Bridge Assurance sends BPDUs on ALL operational STP ports (including designated forwarding ports), not just the standard direction. If BPDUs aren't received on a Bridge Assurance-enabled port, the port is moved to BA-inconsistent (blocked).
Detects unidirectional links and software/CPU failures even on designated ports — catches more failure modes than Loop Guard.
Loop Guard only protects non-designated (root/alternate) ports against missing BPDUs.
Both ends of the link must support and have Bridge Assurance enabled.
Available on RPVST+/MST.

front.text: What are the four DHCP message types in the DORA process?

items: Discover — Client broadcasts looking for any DHCP server.
Offer — Server responds with an IP address offer (typically unicast, but may be broadcast).
Request — Client broadcasts a request for the offered IP (so all servers see which offer was accepted).
Acknowledge — Selected server confirms the lease; other servers retract their offers.

back.text: Renewal: at T1 (50% of lease), client unicasts a Request to its server. At T2 (87.5%), it broadcasts a Request to any server.

front.text: Show how to configure a Cisco IOS device as a DHCP server with an excluded address range.

back.text:
ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.10.1 10.1.10.10
!
ip dhcp pool VLAN10
 network 10.1.10.0 /24
 default-router 10.1.10.1
 dns-server 8.8.8.8 1.1.1.1
 domain-name example.com
 lease 7
ip dhcp excluded-address removes addresses from the pool — typically the gateway, server addresses, and any static IPs in the subnet.

front.text: Compare TCP and UDP, and give example protocols that use each.

rows: DNS, DHCP, NTP, SNMP, RTP, syslog

front.text: What is Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD), and what breaks it?

items: PMTUD lets a sender discover the smallest MTU on the path. The sender sets the DF (Don't Fragment) bit; if a router along the path can't forward without fragmenting, it returns ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (Fragmentation Needed) with the next-hop MTU.
Sender then reduces TCP MSS (or its packet size) to fit.
Common breakage: firewalls or ACLs that drop ICMP Type 3 messages, leading to PMTUD black holes — connections hang on large transfers but small packets work fine.
Workaround: 'ip tcp adjust-mss <bytes>' on tunnel/WAN interfaces forces the TCP MSS during the 3-way handshake (typical 1360 over GRE+IPsec).

front.text: On a GRE-over-IPsec tunnel, why is 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' commonly applied, and where?

items: GRE adds 24 bytes; IPsec ESP+AES adds another ~50–60 bytes; that drops the effective IP MTU below 1500 bytes.
Without adjustment, TCP endpoints negotiate MSS based on Ethernet MTU (1460), causing fragmentation or PMTUD issues over the tunnel.
ip tcp adjust-mss tells the router to rewrite the TCP MSS option in passing TCP SYN packets, capping it at the configured value.
Apply on the tunnel interface in both directions of traffic flow.
Common safe value: 1360 for GRE+IPsec; can go higher if the actual MTU allows.

back.text:
interface Tunnel0
 ip mtu 1400
 ip tcp adjust-mss 1360

front.text: Show how to configure NTP authentication on a client to a specific server.

back.text:
ntp authentication-key 1 md5 NtpKey
ntp authenticate
ntp trusted-key 1
ntp server 10.1.1.1 key 1
!
clock timezone EST -5 0
clock summer-time EDT recurring
!
! Verify
show ntp associations
show ntp status

front.text: Compare these NTP modes: client/server, peer, broadcast/multicast.

rows: Provides time but does not adjust its own clock from clients (e.g., your master NTP source).
Synchronizes to the configured server. Standard configuration on access devices.
Mutual relationship — each can adjust the other. Used between two equally authoritative time sources.
Server periodically sends time updates to a subnet/group; clients listen passively. Less secure, rarely used.

front.text: Show how to enable multicast routing globally and PIM on an interface, and verify.

back.text:
ip multicast-routing distributed
ip pim rp-address 10.10.10.1
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip pim sparse-mode
!
! Verification
show ip mroute
show ip pim neighbor
show ip pim rp mapping
show ip igmp groups

front.text: What is MSDP, and where is it used?

items: MSDP (Multicast Source Discovery Protocol) — Connects RPs in different PIM-SM domains so each RP learns about active sources in other domains.
Used between independent multicast domains (e.g., separate enterprises, or inter-AS multicast).
Also used inside one organization with redundant Anycast RPs — MSDP is run between the Anycast RPs so they share active sources (Anycast RP with MSDP).
Sources are advertised via SA (Source-Active) messages between MSDP peers; receivers' RPs can then send (S, G) joins toward the source's RP.
Built on top of TCP/639.

front.text: What is Anycast RP, and what problem does it solve?

items: Multiple RPs share the same IP address (anycast). Routers reach the topologically closest RP via the IGP.
Provides RP redundancy and load sharing — if one RP fails, IGP routes to another.
All Anycast RPs must learn about each other's sources so receivers behind any RP can find any source. This is done via:
• MSDP between Anycast RPs, OR
• PIM Anycast RP (RFC 4610) using PIM Register messages without MSDP.
Common in service-provider and large enterprise multicast designs.

front.text: What is the RPF check in multicast forwarding, and what happens if it fails?

items: RPF (Reverse Path Forwarding) check: the router verifies that a multicast packet arrives on the interface used to reach the source (or RP for shared trees).
Done by performing a unicast RIB lookup against the source IP; the resulting outgoing interface is the expected RPF interface.
If the packet arrives on the RPF interface — accept and forward down the tree.
If it arrives on any other interface — drop. This is how multicast prevents loops without TTL-based loop detection.
RPF failures are the most common multicast troubleshooting issue; verify with: show ip rpf <source>.

front.text: How does IPv6 multicast work, and what replaces IGMP for IPv6?

items: IPv6 multicast addresses use the FF00::/8 prefix.
MLD (Multicast Listener Discovery) replaces IGMP for IPv6 host-to-router signaling. MLDv1 ≈ IGMPv2; MLDv2 ≈ IGMPv3 (for SSM).
MLD runs over ICMPv6.
PIM (PIM6) operates the same in IPv6 as in IPv4 — sparse mode, SSM, etc.
Solicited-node multicast (FF02::1:FFXX:XXXX) replaces ARP and is used in NDP.

front.text: What is bufferbloat, and how does AQM (e.g., CoDel, FQ-CoDel, RED) help?

items: Bufferbloat — Excessive buffering in network devices causes high latency and jitter under load even when there's no packet loss; harmful to interactive apps like voice and video.
AQM (Active Queue Management) intervenes before queues fully fill, dropping or marking packets early to signal congestion.
RED/WRED — Random Early Detection drops based on average queue depth and (W)RED weights.
CoDel — Drops based on packet sojourn time (how long a packet sits in the queue), not queue depth.
FQ-CoDel — Combines fair queuing with CoDel; isolates flows so a single bulk flow can't cause latency for interactive flows.

front.text: Show a policer that conforms to drop-eligible markings on exceed instead of dropping (a markdown policer).

back.text:
policy-map MARK_DOWN
 class BUSINESS_DATA
  police cir 10000000
   conform-action transmit
   exceed-action set-dscp-transmit af11
   violate-action drop
Conforming traffic is sent as-is; exceeding traffic is remarked to AF11 (high drop precedence) and forwarded; violating traffic is dropped. This is preferred over hard drops for non-critical apps.

front.text: What commands verify MQC class-maps, policy-maps, and per-interface counters?

back.text:
show class-map
show policy-map
show policy-map interface GigabitEthernet0/1
show policy-map interface GigabitEthernet0/1 output
show policy-map interface gives per-class match counts, drops, and policer/queue stats — the most useful QoS troubleshooting command.

front.text: What is Wi-Fi 'band select', and why is it used?

items: Band select encourages dual-band clients to associate to 5 GHz instead of 2.4 GHz, where capacity is greater and interference is lower.
AP suppresses (delays) probe responses on 2.4 GHz so the client receives 5 GHz probe responses first.
After the client steers, normal operation resumes.
Tunable per WLAN; typically combined with 2.4 GHz client load-balancing settings.

front.text: What does 'AAA Override' do on a WLAN, and what kinds of attributes can RADIUS push back?

items: AAA Override allows RADIUS-returned attributes to override WLAN-level settings on a per-client basis.
Common overrides: Tunnel-Private-Group-ID (VLAN assignment), QoS profile, ACL, VRF, SGT, session timeout.
Example: ISE matches a user's role and returns 'Airespace-ACL-Name = STAFF_ACL', so each role gets a tailored policy on the same SSID.
Reduces SSID sprawl by collapsing many SSIDs into a single SSID with role-based policy via RADIUS.

front.text: Why are MTU and fragmentation common issues over IPsec/GRE tunnels, and how do you mitigate them?

items: Each encapsulation layer adds overhead: GRE 24 bytes, ESP+AES typically 50–60 bytes, plus a new outer IP header.
Effective tunnel MTU is often around 1400 bytes vs. 1500 on Ethernet, causing fragmentation or PMTUD blackholes.
Mitigations:
• ip mtu 1400 on the tunnel interface so the router fragments large packets before encryption.
• ip tcp adjust-mss 1360 so TCP endpoints negotiate a smaller MSS during the SYN exchange.
• Allow ICMP Type 3 Code 4 end-to-end to keep PMTUD working.
• Enable 'crypto ipsec df-bit clear' to allow fragmentation of inner traffic when DF was set.

front.text: Show how to configure SSH public-key authentication for a user on Cisco IOS.

back.text:
ip ssh pubkey-chain
 username admin
  key-string
   AAAAB3NzaC1yc2EAAAA...truncated...user@laptop
  exit
 exit
exit
!
ip ssh server algorithm authentication publickey password
line vty 0 4
 login local
 transport input ssh
Public-key auth is stronger than passwords and is the basis for automation tools (Ansible, Netmiko) connecting securely without storing passwords.

front.text: Compare 'service password-encryption', 'enable secret', 'username X secret', and Type 8/9 password storage.

rows: Type 5 (MD5) by default; Type 8 (PBKDF2-SHA-256) or Type 9 (Scrypt) on modern IOS

back.text:
username admin algorithm-type sha256 secret StrongPassword
enable algorithm-type scrypt secret StrongEnable

front.text: What are the methods to classify and assign an SGT to a host?

items: Dynamic — Through 802.1X/MAB/web auth via ISE; ISE returns the SGT in the RADIUS Access-Accept (cisco-av-pair).
IP-to-SGT — Static mapping configured on a switch or distributed by ISE.
Subnet-to-SGT — Map an entire subnet to an SGT.
VLAN-to-SGT — Map an entire VLAN to an SGT (useful for legacy devices that don't support 802.1X).
Port-to-SGT — Static mapping on the access port itself (cts manual).
L3 IF-to-SGT — Apply an SGT to all traffic ingress on a routed interface.

front.text: What is Cisco Secure Access, and what categories of capabilities does it deliver?

items: Cisco Secure Access is Cisco's converged SSE/SASE service combining cloud-delivered networking and security.
Capabilities: ZTNA (Zero Trust Network Access), VPN-as-a-Service, SWG (Secure Web Gateway), CASB, FWaaS, DNS-layer security, RBI (remote browser isolation), DLP.
Identity-driven, applied per user/device regardless of location.
Replaces the need for separate point products (VPN concentrator, web proxy, URL filtering) with a unified cloud service.

cards.subtopic: Host Groups in Secure Network Analytics

front.text: What are host groups in Cisco Secure Network Analytics, and why do they matter for detection?

items: Host groups are logical groupings of IP addresses by role (DNS servers, domain controllers, printers, web servers, BYOD, etc.).
Detections and alarms can be tuned per host group — e.g., 'high concern index for an Active Directory server' has a different meaning than for a workstation.
Behavioral baselines are computed per host group, improving anomaly detection accuracy.
Critical to populate accurately during deployment; otherwise behavioral detections miss context and produce noise.

front.text: What is Cisco ThousandEyes, and what visibility does it provide that traditional monitoring lacks?

items: ThousandEyes is a SaaS-based digital experience monitoring platform.
Provides end-to-end visibility across networks the organization doesn't own — Internet, ISPs, cloud providers, SaaS apps.
Tests include: HTTP transactions, page load, BGP route monitoring, network path visualization (hop-by-hop with loss/latency).
Enterprise agents (in-network), endpoint agents (on user devices), and cloud agents (global vantage points) provide 360° insight.
Embedded in Catalyst 9300/9400 switches and IOS-XE routers — no separate appliance needed.

front.text: Compare gNMI and NETCONF for programmable network management.

rows: gRPC over HTTP/2 (TLS, typically TCP/9339 or 50051)

front.text: Which HTTP methods are idempotent, and why does it matter for automation?

rows: Replaces resource; same call produces same final state.
Repeating a DELETE leaves the resource deleted.
Creates a new resource each time it's called.
Depends on implementation; merging changes can be idempotent.

back.text: Why it matters: in automation, a script that retries on timeout is safe with idempotent methods. POST retries can create duplicate resources.

front.text: What is a Python virtual environment, and why use one for network automation projects?

items: A virtual environment is an isolated Python installation with its own packages, separate from the system Python.
Prevents version conflicts: project A can use Netmiko 4.0 while project B uses Netmiko 3.4 without affecting each other.
Standard tools: venv (built-in), virtualenv, pyenv, poetry, uv.
Activate: source venv/bin/activate (Linux/Mac) or venv\Scripts\activate (Windows).
Best practice: keep a requirements.txt or pyproject.toml committed to source control so others can reproduce the environment.

back.text:
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install netmiko ansible requests
pip freeze > requirements.txt

front.text: What is an Ansible role, and what is the standard directory layout?

items: A role is a reusable, self-contained bundle of tasks, variables, files, templates, and handlers focused on a specific function (e.g., 'cisco_baseline').
Standard directory structure under roles/<rolename>/:
• tasks/ — main task list (tasks/main.yml).
• vars/ — role-scoped variables.
• defaults/ — default variables (lowest precedence).
• templates/ — Jinja2 templates.
• files/ — static files to copy.
• handlers/ — handlers triggered by 'notify'.
• meta/ — dependencies on other roles.
Roles encourage modular, reusable, and testable automation code.

front.text: You want to send VoIP traffic out the MPLS WAN by default, but fall back to the Internet VPN if MPLS becomes unhealthy. How would you implement this with PBR + IP SLA?

items: Define an IP SLA probe to a target reachable only over MPLS (e.g., the MPLS WAN edge or a remote DC IP).
Track the SLA reachability with a track object.
In the PBR route-map, set the next hop to the MPLS WAN with 'set ip next-hop verify-availability <next-hop> 1 track 1'.
Add a second 'set ip next-hop' as the fallback (Internet VPN tunnel).
Apply the route-map to the LAN ingress interface with 'ip policy route-map'.

back.text:
ip sla 1
 icmp-echo 10.99.99.1
 frequency 5
ip sla schedule 1 life forever start-time now
track 1 ip sla 1 reachability
!
route-map VOIP_PBR permit 10
 match ip address VOIP_ACL
 set ip next-hop verify-availability 10.1.0.1 1 track 1
 set ip next-hop 172.16.0.1
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip policy route-map VOIP_PBR

front.text: How do you create a custom privilege level on Cisco IOS for a user who can only run 'show' commands?

back.text:
privilege exec level 7 show
privilege exec level 7 show running-config
!
username readonly privilege 7 secret StrongPass
!
! Verify by logging in as readonly
! show ? — only 'show' commands available
Modern best practice: use AAA with command-set authorization (TACACS+ via ISE) instead of local privilege levels — far more flexible and centrally managed.

front.text: Show how to enable TACACS+ command authorization for level 15 commands.

back.text:
aaa new-model
tacacs server ISE-1
 address ipv4 10.10.10.50
 key SuperSecretKey
!
aaa group server tacacs+ ISE_GROUP
 server name ISE-1
!
aaa authentication login default group ISE_GROUP local
aaa authorization config-commands
aaa authorization commands 15 default group ISE_GROUP local
aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group ISE_GROUP
!
line vty 0 4
 authorization commands 15 default
 login authentication default
ISE evaluates each command against per-user/group command sets and returns permit or deny. This is far more granular than IOS privilege levels and provides full audit trails.

front.text: What is an Infrastructure ACL (iACL), and where is it applied?

items: An iACL is a large ACL applied at the network edge to deny traffic destined to the routing/management infrastructure address ranges (loopbacks, link networks).
Allows transit traffic but blocks attempts to reach internal device IPs from outside the AS.
Combined with CoPP, iACLs reduce the attack surface against control- and management-plane resources.
Applied inbound on edge interfaces facing untrusted networks (Internet, peering, customer edges).

front.text: Compare RACL, VACL, and PACL ordering and direction.

rows: Layer 2 + Layer 3/4 traffic on the port
All traffic in the VLAN (no direction)
All bridged or routed traffic in that VLAN

back.text: Order on ingress: PACL → VACL → RACL. On egress: RACL → VACL (no PACL on egress).

front.text: What is Cisco Fastlane (Apple), and what does it provide for Wi-Fi traffic?

items: A joint Apple/Cisco initiative that prioritizes specific business-critical apps on iOS/macOS over Wi-Fi.
iOS uses an MDM-pushed list to mark certain apps' traffic with high QoS markings (DSCP 46/EF for voice, AF41 for video).
Cisco WLCs/APs honor those markings end-to-end on the wired and wireless segments, including 802.11e WMM AC mapping.
Result: business apps (e.g., Webex, MS Teams) get prioritized, while non-business apps don't hog the air time.

front.text: What is the difference between OSPF E1 and E2 external routes, and which is the default?

rows: Metric stays at the seed value across the OSPF domain — internal cost is NOT added.
Metric = seed metric + cumulative internal OSPF cost to the ASBR. Reflects true total cost.

back.text: Use E1 when you want each router to choose the closest ASBR for the redistributed prefix. Use E2 when you want a stable, predictable metric across the domain.

front.text: You're doing two-way redistribution between OSPF and EIGRP at two routers. How can route tags prevent routing loops?

items: When redistributing OSPF → EIGRP, set a tag (e.g., tag 100) on the routes leaving OSPF.
On the OTHER redistribution router, when redistributing EIGRP → OSPF, deny any route carrying tag 100 (those originated in OSPF and shouldn't loop back).
Mirror the same logic in the other direction with a different tag (e.g., tag 200 for EIGRP-originated routes).
Implemented with route-maps using 'set tag <value>' on outbound redistribution and 'match tag <value>' with deny on inbound redistribution.

back.text:
route-map OSPF_TO_EIGRP permit 10
 set tag 100
!
route-map EIGRP_TO_OSPF deny 10
 match tag 100
route-map EIGRP_TO_OSPF permit 20
 set tag 200

front.text: Show how to configure an OSPF totally stubby area on the ABR and an internal router.

back.text:
! On the ABR
router ospf 1
 area 10 stub no-summary
!
! On internal routers in area 10
router ospf 1
 area 10 stub
The 'no-summary' keyword on the ABR makes it Totally Stubby (also blocks Type 3 LSAs except a default). All routers in the area must agree on the stub flag or no adjacency forms.

front.text: How do you originate a default route into OSPF, and what's the difference between 'always' and conditional behavior?

back.text:
! Conditional: only advertises 0.0.0.0 if router has a default route
router ospf 1
 default-information originate

! Unconditional: always advertises 0.0.0.0
router ospf 1
 default-information originate always

items: default-information originate — Router advertises a default route to OSPF only if it has a default in its RIB (e.g., from BGP or static).
default-information originate always — Advertises 0.0.0.0/0 unconditionally, even if the router itself has no default. Use carefully: black-holes traffic if upstream connectivity is lost.
Stub/totally-stubby areas automatically get a default from the ABR — no explicit command needed there.

front.text: What is BFD, and how does enabling it with OSPF/EIGRP/BGP improve convergence?

items: BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection) is a lightweight L3 hello protocol designed for sub-second link failure detection.
Routing protocols subscribe to BFD; instead of waiting for protocol-level dead timers (40s OSPF, 15s EIGRP, 180s BGP), they react when BFD declares the path down.
Typical BFD timers: 50–300 ms intervals with multiplier 3 → sub-second detection.
Enabled per interface for the data plane and per protocol for the control plane subscription.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 bfd interval 100 min_rx 100 multiplier 3
!
router ospf 1
 bfd all-interfaces
!
router bgp 65001
 neighbor 10.0.0.2 fall-over bfd

front.text: Compare PVST+, Rapid-PVST+, and MST.

rows: Low (groups of VLANs map to instances)

front.text: What happens at an MST region boundary, and what is the IST?

items: All switches with the same MST configuration name, revision, and VLAN-to-instance mapping form a region.
At the region boundary, MST presents itself as a single virtual bridge to the outside world (which may be running PVST+/Rapid-PVST+).
IST (Internal Spanning Tree, MSTI 0) — Special instance always present; it interacts with the outside STP world.
All other MSTIs (1, 2, …) operate only inside the region; outside the region, only IST BPDUs are visible.
