PVST simulation lets MST regions interoperate with surrounding PVST+ domains by mapping per-VLAN BPDUs onto the IST.

cards.subtopic: LACP Min Links and Max Bundle

front.text: What do LACP 'min-links' and 'max-bundle' do?

items: min-links — Minimum number of bundled member links required to keep the port-channel UP. If active members fall below this, the entire bundle is brought down. Used to prevent the channel from operating at degraded capacity.
max-bundle — Maximum number of active member links in the bundle. Excess physical members configured beyond max-bundle become hot-standby (selected by LACP port priority).
Cisco IOS-XE allows up to 8 active + 8 hot-standby members per LACP port-channel.

back.text:
interface Port-channel10
 port-channel min-links 2
 lacp max-bundle 4

front.text: What's the difference between VRRPv2 and VRRPv3?

rows: Removed (rely on infrastructure security like CoPP/ACL)

front.text: Define the GLBP roles AVG, AVF, and what makes GLBP different from HSRP/VRRP.

items: AVG (Active Virtual Gateway) — One per group; assigns virtual MAC addresses to AVFs and replies to ARPs for the virtual IP, distributing load by handing out different virtual MACs.
AVF (Active Virtual Forwarder) — Up to 4 per group; each owns a unique virtual MAC and forwards traffic destined to that MAC.
Different from HSRP/VRRP: GLBP load-balances natively at L2 — the AVG steers different clients to different AVFs by handing out different virtual MACs in ARP responses.
Load-balancing methods: round-robin (default), weighted (based on configured weight), host-dependent (sticky per client MAC).

front.text: Which FHRPs support IPv6, and what address is used for the virtual gateway?

items: HSRP for IPv6 — Supported. Virtual gateway uses a link-local IPv6 (auto-generated or configured).
VRRPv3 — Supports both IPv4 and IPv6 (RFC 5798).
GLBP for IPv6 — Supported on Cisco IOS.
Hosts use the virtual link-local address as their default gateway, learned via RA (the active FHRP router sends RAs).
IPv6 also has its own native redundancy via NDP and multiple routers in RAs, but FHRPs provide deterministic single-active behavior.

front.text: Compare static NAT, dynamic NAT, and PAT (NAT overload).

rows: 1:1 fixed mapping between inside-local and inside-global IPs
Servers that need to be reachable from outside on a specific public IP
Many-to-many; inside hosts get a public IP from a pool on demand
Many-to-one (or many-to-few); multiple inside hosts share one public IP using unique source ports
Most common; conserves public IPs for many internal hosts

front.text: Show how to configure static port forwarding so that connections to <public IP>:443 are forwarded to an internal web server at 10.1.1.10:443.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 description Outside
 ip address 203.0.113.1 255.255.255.0
 ip nat outside
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 description Inside
 ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0
 ip nat inside
!
! Port forward to internal web server
ip nat inside source static tcp 10.1.1.10 443 interface GigabitEthernet0/1 443

front.text: An inside host can't reach the Internet through PAT. The translation is configured but no entry shows in 'show ip nat translations'. What are common causes?

items: Wrong direction labels — verify that the inside interface has 'ip nat inside' and the outside interface has 'ip nat outside'.
ACL doesn't match the source — verify the ACL referenced by 'ip nat inside source list' actually includes the host's source IP.
No route back — the router has no route to the destination on the outside interface, so traffic never enters the NAT engine.
Asymmetric path — the return traffic comes back via a different router that doesn't have the NAT translation.
Use 'debug ip nat' (carefully — high CPU) and 'show ip nat statistics' to inspect Hits/Misses.

back.text:
show ip nat translations
show ip nat statistics
debug ip nat detailed

front.text: An IPsec site-to-site VPN won't establish. What show and debug commands help isolate Phase 1 vs Phase 2 issues?

back.text:
show crypto isakmp sa
show crypto ipsec sa
show crypto session detail
!
debug crypto isakmp
debug crypto ipsec

items: Phase 1 stuck — Check ISAKMP policy match (encryption, hash, DH group, authentication, lifetime).
Phase 1 fails authentication — Mismatched PSK or wrong identity (peer IP).
Phase 2 fails — Mismatched transform set, mismatched proxy ACLs (interesting traffic), or mismatched PFS group.
Tunnel up but no traffic — ACL/route issue, NAT translating tunnel traffic, or asymmetric routing.

front.text: Why do many management features need to be 'VRF-aware', and what's an example?

items: VRFs isolate routing tables — by default, traffic generated by the device (router-originated) uses the global table. If the management network lives in a VRF, those packets won't take the right path without VRF-awareness.
Examples of VRF-aware commands:
• ping vrf MGMT 10.10.10.10
• ssh -vrf MGMT 10.10.10.20
• ip name-server vrf MGMT 8.8.8.8
• ntp server vrf MGMT 10.10.10.5
• snmp-server host 10.10.10.20 vrf MGMT version 3 priv MONITOR_USER
Many platforms now use a dedicated 'Mgmt-vrf' so out-of-band management and in-band data are isolated.

front.text: What ports/protocols does LISP use for its control and data planes?

back.text: Both planes ride on UDP, which simplifies firewall traversal compared to GRE-based overlays.

front.text: How does VXLAN flood-and-learn (no EVPN) work, and what are its drawbacks?

items: VTEPs flood broadcast/unknown-unicast/multicast (BUM) traffic to all other VTEPs in the VNI (via multicast underlay or ingress replication).
Each VTEP learns remote MAC addresses by inspecting source MACs of decapsulated frames — pure data-plane learning.
Drawbacks: lots of unnecessary BUM flooding, slow convergence on host moves, no advertisement of host IPs (just MACs).
Improvement: BGP EVPN replaces flood-and-learn with a proper control plane that advertises MACs/IPs and prunes BUM.

front.text: What functions does a fabric edge node perform in SD-Access?

items: Acts as the access switch where wired endpoints connect.
Provides the anycast gateway (default gateway) for endpoints in fabric VNs/VLANs.
Performs LISP EID registration with the control plane node when an endpoint is detected.
VXLAN-encapsulates traffic from endpoints toward remote edges or borders (data plane).
Enforces SGT-based policy on egress when configured (distributed enforcement).

front.text: How is multicast delivered in SD-Access fabrics?

items: Native multicast — Underlay carries multicast natively (PIM-SSM/PIM-SM on the underlay). Most efficient — single copy traverses the underlay and replicates as needed.
Head-end replication — Source edge ingress-replicates multicast as separate VXLAN unicast packets to each VTEP that needs the traffic. Simpler underlay (no PIM) but more BW at the source.
Catalyst Center automates either model based on design selection.
Anycast RP often implemented via MSDP between border RPs for redundancy.

front.text: Walk through the high-level onboarding sequence for a new Cisco SD-WAN edge using ZTP/PnP.

items: Edge boots and uses DHCP to get an IP and DNS.
Edge resolves the ZTP/PnP server via DNS and contacts the cloud (ztp.viptela.com or pnpserver).
ZTP/PnP returns the organization name and the vBond hostname/IP.
Edge connects to vBond, which authenticates the device using its certificate and serial number.
vBond returns the IPs of vManage and vSmart controllers.
Edge establishes secure DTLS/TLS control connections to vManage and vSmart.
vManage pushes the device's configuration template; vSmart sends OMP routes/policies.
Edge builds IPsec data-plane tunnels (TLOCs) to other edges per policy.

front.text: What is a 'VPN' in Cisco SD-WAN, and what are the special VPN 0 and VPN 512?

items: In SD-WAN, a VPN is essentially a VRF — a separate routing/forwarding instance.
VPN 0 — Transport VPN. Contains the WAN-facing transport interfaces (TLOCs); used to reach controllers and other edges over IPsec.
VPN 512 — Management VPN. Out-of-band management (e.g., dedicated mgmt port). Isolated from data and transport.
VPNs 1–511 — User/service VPNs (VRFs) carrying customer traffic; mapped to LAN-side networks.
OMP advertises routes per VPN, and policies can manipulate which VPNs see which routes.

front.text: What is the SNMPv3 Engine ID, and why does it matter for users and informs?

items: Engine ID uniquely identifies an SNMP entity. Used in key derivation for SNMPv3 authentication and privacy.
Each agent has a local Engine ID; managers use a remote Engine ID when localizing keys to a specific agent.
When you replace a device, the new Engine ID will not match user keys derived for the old one — users must be recreated for the new agent.
For SNMPv3 informs (which the manager must acknowledge), the agent needs to know the manager's Engine ID — configured via 'snmp-server engineID remote ...'.

front.text: Show how to restrict an SNMPv3 group to a read-only view that only includes the system MIB.

back.text:
snmp-server view SYS_ONLY system included
snmp-server group READONLY_GRP v3 priv read SYS_ONLY
snmp-server user MONITOR_USER READONLY_GRP v3 auth sha StrongAuth priv aes 128 StrongPriv
Views allow fine-grained control over which OIDs/subtrees a user can read or write — preferred over giving full access.

front.text: In a Flexible NetFlow record, what's the difference between 'match' and 'collect' fields?

items: match — Key fields. Define how flows are uniquely identified. Two packets with identical match field values belong to the same flow.
collect — Non-key fields. Additional info exported per flow (counters, interface info, TCP flags, byte/packet counts).
Best practice: keep match fields minimal (typical 5-tuple: src IP, dst IP, src port, dst port, protocol) and add useful collect fields (byte count, TCP flags, application, etc.).

back.text:
flow record FNF_REC
 match ipv4 source address
 match ipv4 destination address
 match transport source-port
 match transport destination-port
 match ipv4 protocol
 collect counter bytes
 collect counter packets
 collect transport tcp flags

front.text: Show how to configure local SPAN and RSPAN.

back.text:
! Local SPAN: copy Gig0/1 (source) traffic to Gig0/24 (destination)
monitor session 1 source interface GigabitEthernet0/1 both
monitor session 1 destination interface GigabitEthernet0/24

! RSPAN source switch
vlan 999
 remote-span
!
monitor session 2 source interface GigabitEthernet0/1 both
monitor session 2 destination remote vlan 999

! RSPAN destination switch
vlan 999
 remote-span
!
monitor session 2 source remote vlan 999
monitor session 2 destination interface GigabitEthernet0/24

front.text: Show a basic ERSPAN source session configuration.

back.text:
monitor session 1 type erspan-source
 source interface GigabitEthernet0/1 both
 no shutdown
 destination
  erspan-id 100
  ip address 10.10.10.50
  origin ip address 10.1.1.1
ERSPAN encapsulates mirrored traffic in GRE and sends it to a remote IP destination — useful when the analyzer is across the Layer 3 network.

front.text: Show a basic IOS-XE streaming telemetry subscription that pushes interface counters every 30 seconds via gRPC dial-out.

back.text:
telemetry ietf subscription 100
 encoding encode-kvgpb
 filter xpath /interfaces-ios-xe-oper:interfaces/interface
 stream yang-push
 update-policy periodic 3000
 receiver ip address 10.10.10.50 57500 protocol grpc-tcp
update-policy periodic 3000 = every 3000 centiseconds (30 seconds). Use 'on-change' for state changes only. KVGPB = Key-Value Google Protocol Buffers encoding.

front.text: Name common tools used to test REST APIs and their typical use case.

items: Postman / Insomnia / Bruno — GUI clients to build requests, save collections, and chain calls. Great for exploration and team sharing.
curl — Command-line HTTP client; ubiquitous in scripts and CI pipelines.
HTTPie — User-friendly CLI HTTP client with JSON-aware syntax.
Python requests — Programmatic HTTP from Python scripts.
Swagger UI / OpenAPI explorer — Auto-generated API documentation that lets you 'try it out' against the API.

front.text: What is OpenAPI (formerly Swagger), and how does it help when working with a new REST API?

items: OpenAPI is a specification (YAML or JSON) that describes a REST API: endpoints, methods, parameters, request/response schemas, authentication.
Auto-generates interactive documentation (Swagger UI) where you can read the contract and execute calls in the browser.
Enables code generation: SDKs in Python, Go, Java, etc., can be auto-generated from the spec, eliminating boilerplate.
Most modern Cisco APIs (DNAC/Catalyst Center, Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, ISE 3.x) publish OpenAPI specs.

front.text: Name the common tools for working with YANG models.

items: pyang — Python tool for validating YANG modules and generating tree views, sample XML, etc.
yangsuite — Cisco web-based tool for browsing models, building NETCONF/RESTCONF/gNMI calls, and testing.
ncclient — Python library for NETCONF client operations.
ansible-netcommon / cisco.ios collections — Use YANG models under the hood to manage devices.
Visual Studio Code with YANG extensions — Useful for editing/viewing modules.

front.text: Compare Netmiko, NAPALM, and Cisco's pyATS for network automation in Python.

rows: Multi-vendor, simple, great for sending commands and reading raw output
Provides a unified API across vendors (get_facts, get_interfaces); structured data
Powerful parsers (Genie), comparators, and topology modeling for test automation
Pure-Python alternative to Ansible with concurrency

front.text: What is an Ansible inventory, and what is the difference between static and dynamic inventory?

items: Inventory is the list of managed hosts and how Ansible reaches them; can include groups, host vars, and group vars.
Static inventory — Plain INI/YAML files that list hosts and groups. Easy and deterministic.
Dynamic inventory — A script or plugin that pulls hosts from a source-of-truth at runtime (e.g., NetBox, AWS, vManage, Catalyst Center).
Recommended pattern: keep a source-of-truth (NetBox, etc.) and use dynamic inventory so playbooks always run against current data.

front.text: What is a feature branch workflow in Git, and why is it useful for network automation code?

items: Create a branch from main: git checkout -b feature/add-snmpv3.
Make changes, test locally, and commit them to the feature branch.
Push the branch and open a Pull/Merge Request for peer review.
Reviewers check the diff, run CI tests (lint, unit tests, dry-run), and approve.
Merge into main; main stays deployable. Optionally delete the feature branch.

back.text: Benefits: parallel work, peer review, clear history, easier rollbacks, and CI/CD gating before changes hit production.

front.text: What does a CI/CD pipeline for network automation typically include?

items: Lint stage — yamllint, ansible-lint, pylint to catch syntax errors.
Static checks — Validate Jinja templates, schema-validate intent files.
Unit tests — Run pyATS/Genie tests against mock outputs.
Dry-run / 'check mode' — Use Ansible --check or NSO dry-run to preview changes.
Lab/CML deployment — Deploy to a Cisco Modeling Labs (CML) sandbox and run integration tests.
Approval gate — Manual approval before production deployment.
Production deployment — Apply changes via Ansible/NSO/Catalyst Center.
Post-deployment validation — Run pyATS or assurance checks; rollback on failure.

front.text: What is Model-Driven Programmability (MDP) on IOS-XE, and what are its three pillars?

items: MDP describes Cisco IOS-XE's structured-data programmability foundation: configuration and state are defined in YANG models, accessed via standard protocols.
Three pillars:
1. YANG models (data) — IETF, OpenConfig, and native IOS-XE models.
2. Protocols — NETCONF, RESTCONF, and gNMI to read/modify model data.
3. Encodings — XML (NETCONF), JSON/XML (RESTCONF), Protobuf/JSON (gNMI).
Replaces screen-scraping CLI with a deterministic, machine-friendly interface.

front.text: Show a sample NETCONF <edit-config> RPC payload that sets the description on an interface using IOS-XE native YANG.

back.text:
<rpc message-id="101" xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0">
  <edit-config>
    <target>
      <running/>
    </target>
    <config>
      <native xmlns="http://cisco.com/ns/yang/Cisco-IOS-XE-native">
        <interface>
          <GigabitEthernet>
            <name>0/0</name>
            <description>Uplink to Distribution</description>
          </GigabitEthernet>
        </interface>
      </native>
    </config>
  </edit-config>
</rpc>

front.text: Show a curl example that uses RESTCONF to retrieve the running config of all interfaces from an IOS-XE device.

back.text:
curl -k -u admin:cisco \
  -H 'Accept: application/yang-data+json' \
  https://10.1.1.1/restconf/data/Cisco-IOS-XE-native:native/interface
RESTCONF endpoints follow the YANG model paths. /restconf/data is the configuration tree; /restconf/operations are RPCs.

front.text: Compare Syslog, SNMP polling, SNMP traps, and streaming telemetry as monitoring approaches.

rows: Logs and free-form events; not structured for metrics
Counters and stats; high overhead at scale

front.text: What is Flexible Radio Assignment (FRA) on Cisco APs?

items: FRA is an RRM feature on dual-radio APs (especially XOR/dual-5GHz APs) that automatically reassigns radios between roles based on network conditions.
Possible roles include serving 5 GHz clients, serving 2.4 GHz clients, or operating as a dedicated monitor radio for security/scanning.
RRM evaluates RF conditions (density, interference, coverage) and shifts radios to the role that best serves users.
Key for tri-radio Wi-Fi 6/6E APs that can serve 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz simultaneously.

front.text: What are the FlexConnect AP modes (Connected vs Standalone), and what happens to existing clients during the transition?

items: Connected mode — AP has CAPWAP control connection to the WLC. Authentication and policies applied normally; data may be locally switched or centrally switched per WLAN.
Standalone mode — Lost the WLC connection. AP continues to serve cached SSIDs locally if 'Local Authentication' is enabled.
Existing authenticated clients keep working during transition for locally switched WLANs; centrally switched WLANs drop because the data path goes through the WLC.
When the WLC connection is restored, AP transitions back to Connected mode automatically.
Use case: branch offices that need Wi-Fi to keep working over a temporary WAN outage.

front.text: Show a basic 'cts manual' configuration that enables inline SGT propagation on a switch trunk with a static SAP PMK.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
 switchport mode trunk
 cts manual
  policy static sgt 100 trusted
  sap pmk AAAA1234 mode-list gcm-encrypt
cts manual is used between two TrustSec-capable switches without the full ISE 802.1X+TrustSec automation. SAP (Security Association Protocol) negotiates MACsec keys for link encryption.

front.text: What is IP Device Tracking (IPDT) / Switch Integrated Security Features (SISF) Device Tracking, and what features depend on it?

items: IPDT/SISF Device Tracking maintains a per-port binding table of MAC, IP, and VLAN for each connected endpoint.
It uses ARP, DHCP, and ND snooping to populate and validate entries.
Required by features that need an authoritative IP-to-port mapping: 802.1X with SGT assignment, dot1x guest VLANs, web auth, IPv6 First-Hop Security, IP Source Guard.
Older IPDT implementations sent ARP probes that could disrupt static IP devices; modern SISF resolves this with smarter tracking and per-VLAN policies.

front.text: What is out-of-band (OOB) management, and why is it considered best practice?

items: OOB management uses a separate physical network or dedicated mgmt port that's isolated from the production data plane.
Allows admins to reach devices even when the production network is broken — e.g., during a routing storm, ACL misconfig, or major outage.
Often combined with terminal/console servers (e.g., Cisco IR/Avocent) for last-resort access.
Cisco devices typically expose Mgmt0/0 in a dedicated 'Mgmt-vrf' so it's isolated from data forwarding.

front.text: What's the difference between macro-segmentation and micro-segmentation in modern network security?

items: Macro-segmentation — Coarse separation between large groups (e.g., guest vs corporate vs IoT). Implemented by VRFs/VNs/VLANs. Each macro segment has its own routing table or broadcast domain.
Micro-segmentation — Fine-grained policy WITHIN a macro segment, between specific user/application groups. Implemented by SGTs/SGACLs (TrustSec) or by application-aware firewall policies.
Together they implement defense-in-depth: macro keeps unrelated populations apart; micro restricts lateral movement inside a population.
SD-Access uses macro (VNs) + micro (SGTs) together to deliver this model.

front.text: What three ETA-specific data elements does Encrypted Traffic Analytics export, and what is each used for?

items: Initial Data Packet (IDP) — First non-zero-payload packet of the flow. For TLS, includes the ClientHello — used to inspect cipher suites, extensions, server name (SNI), and TLS version.
Sequence of Packet Lengths and Times (SPLT) — Distribution of packet sizes and inter-arrival times. Distinct malware C2 patterns differ from normal application traffic.
Byte distribution / TLS metadata — Statistical features of the encrypted payload bytes, plus TLS handshake metadata.
Stealthwatch + Cognitive correlate these features against ML models trained on known malware behaviors to detect threats without decryption.

front.text: An API returns 1000 results per page and includes a 'next' link in its response body. Outline how a script should iterate through all pages.

items: Issue the initial GET request with the desired filters and page size.
Process the items in the 'response' (or equivalent) array.
Check the response body (or Link header) for a 'next' URL.
If 'next' exists, issue a GET to that URL using the same auth headers.
Repeat until 'next' is null/missing.
Always honor rate-limit headers (e.g., X-RateLimit-Remaining, Retry-After).

back.text:
url = base_url
while url:
    resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers).json()
    process(resp["items"])
    url = resp.get("next")

front.text: What is a Network Source of Truth (NSoT), and why is it important for automation?

items: NSoT is a single authoritative database of intended network state — devices, sites, IP addresses, VLANs, circuits, cabling, prefixes, etc.
Examples: NetBox, Nautobot, Infoblox, custom CMDB.
Automation tools (Ansible, Terraform, Catalyst Center) use the NSoT as their input. Devices are then continuously reconciled to match.
Eliminates drift between spreadsheets, Visio diagrams, and the actual network. Becomes the basis for self-service portals, audit, and CI/CD pipelines.

front.text: What is Cisco Modeling Labs (CML), and how is it commonly used by network engineers?

items: CML is Cisco's network simulation/virtualization platform that runs IOS-XE, IOS-XR, NX-OS, ASAv, FTDv, IOSv, and other images as VMs.
Used for hands-on practice (CCNP/CCIE study), proofs of concept, and pre-deployment testing of automation playbooks.
Topologies are saved as YAML and can be spun up on-demand — fits CI/CD pipelines as a 'lab' deployment target.
Replaces older simulators like VIRL and works alongside DevNet sandboxes.

front.text: Compare these IPv6 transition mechanisms: Dual Stack, NAT64/DNS64, and 6to4/Tunneling.

rows: Devices run IPv4 and IPv6 simultaneously
Translates IPv6 packets to IPv4 (and synthesizes AAAA records from A records)
Tunnels IPv6 across an IPv4-only core

back.text: IPv6 Tunneling (6to4) is largely deprecated; modern best practice is Dual Stack with NAT64/DNS64 for the long tail of IPv4-only services.

front.text: Compare classic OpenFlow-style SDN with controller-based SDN solutions like Cisco SD-Access and ACI.

rows: Fully centralized — controller programs flow tables on switches
Distributed control plane (LISP, BGP-EVPN) with centralized policy/management
Standard L2/L3 switches running protocols, programmed by Catalyst Center / APIC
Loss of controller = no new flows can be programmed
Loss of controller = existing forwarding continues; new policy changes paused

front.text: Match these management protocols to their default ports/transport: SSH, Telnet, HTTPS, NETCONF, RESTCONF, SNMP, TACACS+, RADIUS auth, RADIUS acct.
Show how to change the OSPF network type on an Ethernet interface to point-to-point and verify it.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip ospf network point-to-point
!
! Verify
show ip ospf interface GigabitEthernet0/0 | include Network Type|Hello|Dead
Both ends must use the same network type, or the adjacency will not form.

front.text: What does 'max-metric router-lsa on-startup' do in OSPF, and why is it useful?

items: Causes the router to advertise its router-LSA with the maximum metric (effectively making it a 'last resort' transit) for a configured time after startup or for a manual graceful shutdown.
Other routers see the high cost and prefer alternate paths, allowing the new router to fully boot, build its tables (BGP, etc.), and warm up before carrying transit traffic.
Prevents traffic black-holing during BGP convergence on a freshly booted edge.
Common with: 'max-metric router-lsa on-startup wait-for-bgp'.

back.text:
router ospf 1
 max-metric router-lsa on-startup wait-for-bgp

front.text: How can you influence EIGRP path selection without changing interface bandwidth/delay, and which method is preferred?

items: Change interface 'delay' (preferred) — Adjusts the metric without affecting QoS, queuing, or other features that key off interface bandwidth.
Change interface 'bandwidth' (NOT preferred for path selection) — It also influences QoS and other protocols; side effects.
offset-list — Adds a fixed offset to the EIGRP composite metric for routes matched by an ACL (in or out, on a specific interface).
Best practice: use 'delay' on a per-interface basis for clean, surgical path manipulation.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 delay 1000
!
! Or with an offset list
router eigrp 100
 offset-list 10 in 20 GigabitEthernet0/1
access-list 10 permit 10.10.0.0 0.0.255.255

front.text: What's the difference between BGP peer groups and peer templates, and why use either?

items: Both reduce configuration repetition for groups of BGP neighbors that share policy.
Peer group — Older method; all members must share inbound/outbound policy. Outbound updates are generated once and replicated.
Peer template — Newer, more flexible. Two types: peer-session (timers, auth, AS) and peer-policy (route maps, prefix lists). A neighbor can inherit multiple templates.
Operational benefit: outbound update replication scales better and reduces CPU on the BGP speaker.
Best practice on modern IOS: use peer templates.

front.text: Show two ways to advertise a default route in BGP: 'default-originate' to a neighbor and 'network 0.0.0.0' with a static.

back.text:
! Method 1: per-neighbor default-originate
router bgp 65001
 neighbor 203.0.113.1 default-originate

! Method 2: network statement requires a matching route in the RIB
ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 Null0
router bgp 65001
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  network 0.0.0.0 mask 0.0.0.0
default-originate is per-neighbor and doesn't require the route to exist locally. The 'network' statement requires the prefix in the RIB (commonly via a static to Null0).

front.text: What is MP-BGP, and what address families does it commonly carry beyond IPv4 unicast?

items: MP-BGP (Multiprotocol BGP) extends BGP to carry routing information for multiple address families inside a single BGP session.
Common AFs: IPv4 unicast, IPv6 unicast, VPNv4 (L3VPN), VPNv6, EVPN (Ethernet VPN for VXLAN/MPLS L2VPN), IPv4 multicast, link-state.
Each address family is negotiated using BGP capabilities at session establishment.
Required for: MPLS L3VPN, BGP-EVPN, Cisco SD-Access fabric route exchange.
Configured under 'address-family' sub-mode in router bgp.

front.text: What is 6PE (and 6vPE) in service-provider MPLS networks?

items: 6PE — IPv6 Provider Edge. Carries IPv6 routes between PEs across an IPv4 MPLS core using MP-BGP labeled-unicast (afi 2 / safi 4).
Core (P) routers do NOT need to be IPv6-aware — IPv6 traffic is label-switched between PEs.
6vPE — Same idea, but per-VRF (IPv6 L3VPN). Carries IPv6 customer prefixes inside a VRF using MP-BGP VPNv6 across an IPv4 MPLS core.
Lets SPs add IPv6 services without upgrading the entire core to IPv6.

front.text: Why is 'passive-interface default' considered a best practice, and how do you enable adjacencies on selected interfaces?

back.text:
router ospf 1
 passive-interface default
 no passive-interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 no passive-interface GigabitEthernet0/1

items: Default-passive prevents accidentally forming routing adjacencies on user-facing or new interfaces.
Reduces risk of a rogue device speaking your IGP on an access port.
You explicitly opt-in core-facing interfaces with 'no passive-interface' — much safer than the opposite default.

front.text: You connect a new switch to a distribution switch port. The new switch unintentionally has a lower bridge priority and tries to become root. How do you prevent this without changing priorities?

items: Apply Root Guard on the distribution switch port facing the downstream switch.
If a superior BPDU is received on that port, the switch puts the port in root-inconsistent state (effectively blocking) until the superior BPDUs stop.
The distribution switch's role and the existing root remain undisturbed.
Root Guard does NOT apply to access ports facing endpoints (no BPDUs expected there) — that's BPDU Guard's job.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/24
 spanning-tree guard root

front.text: On a fiber link between two switches, BPDUs stop being received in one direction (unidirectional). What does Loop Guard do, and how is it different from UDLD's role here?

items: Loop Guard — On a non-designated port (root or alternate), if expected BPDUs stop arriving, the port is moved to loop-inconsistent (blocking) instead of transitioning to designated forwarding. Prevents the loop that would otherwise form when both sides think they're the designated port.
UDLD — Detects unidirectional links by exchanging hello packets at Layer 2 and validating the neighbor sees us. In aggressive mode, UDLD err-disables the port.
Recommended pairing on inter-switch fiber links: enable BOTH UDLD aggressive AND Loop Guard for layered protection.

back.text:
spanning-tree loopguard default
udld aggressive

front.text: Show how to configure a Layer 3 EtherChannel between two routers/Layer 3 switches.

back.text:
interface Port-channel10
 no switchport
 ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.252
!
interface range GigabitEthernet0/1 - 2
 no switchport
 channel-group 10 mode active
Use 'no switchport' on the Port-channel interface to make it routed. Member interfaces must also be 'no switchport' before joining the L3 channel.

front.text: What's the difference between 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 10', 'add 10', 'remove 10', 'except 10', and 'all'?

rows: Replaces the allowed VLAN list with just VLAN 10.
Adds VLAN 10 to the existing list.
Removes VLAN 10 from the existing list.
Allows all VLANs (default if no command).

back.text: A common outage: typing 'switchport trunk allowed vlan 50' on an existing trunk that was carrying many VLANs — instantly drops every other VLAN. Use 'add' to be safe.

front.text: Two switches in the same VTP design aren't sharing VLANs. What are the most common causes?

items: VTP domain name mismatch — Domain names are case-sensitive and must match exactly.
VTP version mismatch — A v3 server won't sync VLANs to a v1/v2 device unless v3 explicitly enables backward compatibility.
Authentication mismatch — VTP password mismatch silently breaks DB sync.
Trunk down or wrong allowed VLAN list — VTP advertisements ride on VLAN 1 by default; VLAN 1 must be in the allowed list.
Mode 'transparent' or 'off' on a path device — VTPv1/v2 transparent forwards advertisements; VTPv3 off does not. VTP off blocks everything.

back.text:
show vtp status
show interfaces trunk
show vlan brief

front.text: What is PVST simulation in MST, and what error message do you get when there's a mismatch?

items: PVST simulation lets an MST region interoperate with surrounding PVST+/Rapid-PVST+ switches at region boundaries.
MST presents only the IST (instance 0) externally; PVST simulation maps per-VLAN BPDUs from PVST+ neighbors onto the IST so STP topology stays consistent.
Mismatch error: 'PVST simulation inconsistency' — happens when MST sees a PVST+ neighbor on a port that should be running MST, or when there's a topology that breaks the simulation rules.
Resolution: ensure boundary links are clean MST↔PVST+ (or both MST), and that the MST region root is the root for ALL VLANs the boundary sees from PVST+.

front.text: What kinds of objects can HSRP/VRRP/GLBP track besides interface line protocol?

items: Interface line-protocol — Up/down state of an interface.
Interface IP routing — Whether the interface is participating in IP routing.
IP route reachability — Whether a specific prefix is in the RIB and reachable.
IP SLA — Health of an IP SLA probe (e.g., reachability, jitter SLA).
List (boolean) — Combine multiple track objects with AND/OR logic.
Stub objects — External feedback from EEM or scripts.

back.text:
track 1 ip sla 100 reachability
track 2 interface GigabitEthernet0/1 line-protocol
track 3 list boolean and
 object 1
 object 2
!
interface Vlan10
 standby 10 track 3 decrement 30

front.text: How do NAT64 and DNS64 work together to let IPv6-only clients reach IPv4-only servers?

items: Client (IPv6-only) sends a DNS AAAA query for an IPv4-only server.
DNS64 server has no AAAA record; it queries A and synthesizes a AAAA record by embedding the IPv4 address inside an IPv6 prefix (typically 64:ff9b::/96).
Client connects to the synthesized IPv6 address.
Traffic reaches the NAT64 router, which strips the IPv6 prefix to recover the IPv4 destination, then performs IPv6→IPv4 translation (similar to NAT44).
Return traffic is translated back IPv4→IPv6 by NAT64.

back.text: NAT64 only works for client-initiated connections from IPv6 to IPv4. The reverse (legacy IPv4 client to IPv6-only server) requires NAT46 or proxies and is rarely used.

front.text: Why are public DNS resolvers (8.8.8.8, 1.1.1.1) deployed as anycast, and what does this provide for users?

items: Multiple DNS servers around the world advertise the SAME IP via BGP. Routing automatically delivers each query to the nearest instance.
Reduces latency — users hit the closest physical instance.
Provides natural high availability — if one instance fails, BGP withdraws and the next-closest instance takes over without client reconfiguration.
Provides natural DDoS distribution — attack traffic is split across many instances rather than concentrated on one box.

front.text: What does IGMP Snooping do on a Layer 2 switch, and what happens if it's disabled?

items: IGMP Snooping — The switch listens to IGMP join/leave messages between hosts and the multicast router. It builds per-VLAN multicast forwarding tables so multicast frames are sent only out ports where receivers actually exist.
Without IGMP Snooping, the switch treats multicast like broadcast and floods it out every port in the VLAN — wastes bandwidth and CPU on uninterested hosts.
Querier role — A multicast router (or designated switch) periodically sends IGMP queries; the switch needs to see these to keep the snooping state alive.
Enabled by default on most Cisco switches but verify in production.

front.text: What is hierarchical QoS (HQoS), and what is a typical use case?

items: HQoS nests one policy-map inside another (parent + child).
Parent — Typically a shaper that constrains the total rate to the SP-enforced sub-rate (e.g., 50 Mbps on a Gig physical interface).
Child — Usual class-based queueing/policing applied to traffic AFTER the parent shapes it down to the contract rate.
Use case: physical interface is 1 Gbps but the SP only allows 50 Mbps; without shaping, packets get dropped at the SP without QoS prioritization. HQoS makes our QoS effective at the contract rate.

back.text:
policy-map CHILD
 class VOICE
  priority percent 30
 class class-default
  fair-queue
!
policy-map PARENT
 class class-default
  shape average 50000000
  service-policy CHILD
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 service-policy output PARENT

front.text: What is RSVP, and where is it used today?

items: RSVP (Resource Reservation Protocol) is a signaling protocol used by the IntServ QoS model. Applications request bandwidth/latency guarantees per flow; routers reserve resources along the path.
Doesn't scale to the public Internet (per-flow state on every router), so it's largely unused there.

Still relevant in two areas: 1) MPLS-TE, where RSVP-TE signals traffic-engineered LSPs and reserves bandwidth on each link. 2) Classic Cisco IOS Voice/Video CAC (Call Admission Control) for legacy enterprise voice.
DiffServ replaced IntServ for general QoS marking/scheduling in modern designs.

front.text: Voice marked DSCP EF traverses a GRE-over-IPsec tunnel. After encryption, all packets appear as DSCP 0 to the QoS policy on the WAN egress. What's the fix?

items: Cause: When IPsec/GRE encapsulates the original IP, the outer header gets a default DSCP. Service-policy on the physical interface only sees the outer header.
Fix 1: 'qos pre-classify' on the tunnel/crypto interface — QoS classification is performed BEFORE encapsulation, so it can match the inner DSCP/ACL.
Fix 2: Configure IPsec/GRE to copy the inner DSCP to the outer header (default for many tunnel types). Then QoS on the physical interface can match outer DSCP normally.
Verify with: 'show policy-map interface' for match counts and check both inner and outer DSCP using 'debug crypto ipsec' or packet captures.

back.text:
interface Tunnel0
 qos pre-classify

front.text: Why does naive 802.1X re-auth on every roam break voice quality, and how do PMK Caching, OKC, and 802.11r solve it?

items: Plain 802.1X re-auth requires a full EAP exchange + 4-way handshake for each new AP — easily 700ms+ — which causes audible voice cutouts on roams.
PMK Caching — AP caches the PMK so a returning client skips the 802.1X exchange.
OKC (Opportunistic Key Caching) — WLC pre-shares the PMK with neighboring APs so the first roam to a new AP can also skip 802.1X.
802.11r (Fast BSS Transition) — Standardized; pre-establishes keys via Over-the-Air or Over-the-DS so the 4-way handshake is collapsed into the reassociation request, achieving sub-50ms roams.
802.11k/v help clients select better roam candidates earlier.

front.text: What is an anchor controller in Cisco wireless, and what's a typical use case?

items: Anchor controller — A WLC that 'owns' a particular SSID's traffic, regardless of where the user actually associates.
Foreign WLCs tunnel that SSID's user traffic via a CAPWAP mobility tunnel back to the anchor.

Typical use: Guest Wi-Fi. Users connect at any branch (foreign WLC), but their traffic is tunneled to a guest anchor in the DMZ for centralized policy and Internet egress — internal corporate networks are never exposed.
Configured under WLAN → Mobility Anchors on each foreign WLC, pointing to the anchor WLC.

front.text: When and why is IPv6 link-local-only routing acceptable on a router-to-router link, and what features still work?

items: Routing protocols (OSPFv3, EIGRPv6, BGP next-hop) form adjacencies using link-local addresses — so the link itself doesn't need a global IPv6 prefix.
Saves global address space and reduces attack surface (no globally routable IP on the link).
Still works: hop-by-hop forwarding, NDP, traceroute (using link-local responses).
Doesn't work: pinging the link from off-link (the link itself isn't reachable globally), or NAT/IPv6 services that need a globally addressable transit IP.
Practical pattern: link-local for inter-router links + loopback global IPs for management and BGP peering.

front.text: How does IPv6 handle multiple addresses per interface, and why doesn't it use 'secondary' like IPv4?

items: IPv6 natively allows MULTIPLE global addresses per interface — they're all first-class addresses, not 'secondary'.
Common scenario: an interface has a link-local (auto), one or more global unicast addresses, and possibly a ULA — all simultaneously.
Source-address selection follows RFC 6724 rules (longest match, scope, preferred state, etc.).
Configure with multiple 'ipv6 address' lines; no 'secondary' keyword needed.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ipv6 address 2001:db8:1::1/64
 ipv6 address 2001:db8:cafe::1/64

front.text: When troubleshooting IPsec, how do you tell whether Phase 1, Phase 2, or interesting traffic is the problem?

items: show crypto isakmp sa — If no entry exists, Phase 1 isn't establishing (suspect mismatched policy, PSK, identity, or routing/firewall blocking UDP/500 or UDP/4500).

show crypto ipsec sa — Look for 'pkts encaps/encrypt > 0'. If you see encaps but no decaps, the tunnel is up but the remote isn't sending — Phase 2 might be one-way; check ACLs and routing on both ends.
Show interface counters on the tunnel/source — verify interesting traffic is matching the crypto ACL.
If Phase 1 is QM_IDLE and Phase 2 has SAs but no traffic, suspect: NAT translating tunnel traffic, ACL on either end mismatch (proxy IDs), or routing not pointing through the tunnel.

front.text: When does a GRE tunnel interface come up/down, and what's the 'tunnel keepalive' for?

items: By default, a GRE tunnel interface goes up the moment the tunnel source has a valid IP — it doesn't actually verify reachability to the destination.
This means the tunnel can be 'up/up' even when the destination is unreachable, black-holing traffic.
tunnel keepalive — Sends periodic GRE keepalive packets to the destination. If they aren't echoed back, the tunnel transitions to down/down, allowing routing protocols to reconverge to a backup path.
Default keepalive timer: 10 seconds, retries 3.
Important on hub-and-spoke and dual-tunnel WAN designs to avoid black-holing during partial failures.

back.text:
interface Tunnel0
 tunnel keepalive 5 3

front.text: How do you ping, traceroute, and run show route inside a specific VRF on Cisco IOS?

back.text:
ping vrf MGMT 10.10.10.10 source Loopback0
traceroute vrf MGMT 10.10.10.10
show ip route vrf MGMT
show ip arp vrf MGMT
show ip cef vrf MGMT 10.10.10.10
telnet 10.10.10.10 /vrf MGMT
Default behavior of these commands is the global RIB. Without specifying the VRF, you'll get confusing 'destination unreachable' results when the prefix only exists in the VRF.

front.text: Describe the LISP data-plane encapsulation: what does the encapsulated packet look like?

items: Outer IP header — Source = ITR's RLOC; Destination = ETR's RLOC. This is what the underlay routes on.
Outer UDP header — Destination port 4341.
LISP header — 8 bytes; carries flags and the Instance ID (IID) used to support virtualization (multi-tenant separation).
Inner IP header — Original source/destination EID addresses (the original packet).

back.text: The Instance ID effectively makes LISP VRF-aware in the data plane — it's how SD-Access carries multiple Virtual Networks across the same fabric.

front.text: In a BGP-EVPN VXLAN data center, what role do the spine and leaf switches play?

items: Leaf — Acts as the VTEP. Connects to endpoints and gateways; encapsulates/decapsulates VXLAN traffic, advertises EVPN routes via BGP.
Spine — Underlay backbone. Provides any-to-any IP reachability between leaves and acts as the BGP route reflector for EVPN routes (so leaves don't have to full-mesh).
Underlay typically runs OSPF or BGP; overlay runs MP-BGP EVPN (afi L2VPN, safi EVPN).
ECMP across multiple spines provides high bandwidth and redundancy.
Anycast gateway: every leaf shares the same default-gateway IP/MAC for each VLAN, so endpoints can move freely between leaves.

front.text: What is Direct Internet Access (DIA) in SD-WAN, and how is it commonly secured?

items: DIA — Branch traffic for SaaS apps, web browsing, etc., breaks out to the Internet locally instead of being backhauled to a DC.
Reduces WAN load, improves SaaS performance (no hairpin), and lowers MPLS bandwidth costs.
Security options: NAT + ACLs + zone-based firewall on the edge, AppQoS for QoS, integration with Cisco Umbrella / Cisco Secure Access for cloud-delivered web/DNS security.
vManage data policy can steer specific applications (e.g., Office 365) directly to the Internet while sending business-critical apps over MPLS.

front.text: What does Cisco Cloud OnRamp do, and what are its three flavors?

items: Cloud OnRamp extends SD-WAN policy and visibility into cloud and SaaS environments.
Cloud OnRamp for SaaS — Continuously measures performance to top SaaS apps (M365, Salesforce, etc.) over each transport and steers users to the best path.
Cloud OnRamp for IaaS — Automates the deployment of cloud-hosted vEdges (in AWS/Azure/GCP) so cloud workloads join the SD-WAN fabric.
Cloud OnRamp for Multicloud / Colocation — Extends SD-WAN to colos and multicloud landing zones for direct branch-to-cloud connectivity.

front.text: Compare these dual-homed WAN designs: Active/Standby, Active/Active equal-cost, and Active/Active per-application.

rows: Primary link is preferred; backup link only used on failure (e.g., floating static, BGP local-pref).
Simple; backup BW is often wasted.
Both links advertised equally; routing uses ECMP load-sharing.
Uses both links; harder to maintain symmetric flows for stateful services like firewalls.
Different apps prefer different transports (e.g., voice over MPLS, web over Internet).
Best link utilization and UX; most common with SD-WAN.

front.text: What are the GLBP load-balancing modes, and when would you use each?

rows: AVG hands out vMACs in rotation per ARP request.
AVFs are assigned weights; AVG distributes vMACs proportionally.
AVFs have asymmetric capacity (different sized routers/links).
Each client's MAC always gets the same vMAC.
Stateful flows (firewall, IPsec) where the same client must consistently use the same forwarder.

front.text: What's the difference between the console line, aux line, and vty lines on a Cisco IOS device?

items: Console (line con 0) — Local serial RJ-45 / USB console for direct CLI access. Always present, used for OOB and recovery.
Aux (line aux 0) — Auxiliary port, historically used for modem dial-in. Often disabled or removed on modern devices for security.
VTY (line vty 0 N) — Virtual TTY lines for remote SSH (and historically Telnet). 5 lines (0–4) is the legacy default; modern IOS-XE typically supports more (0–15).

back.text: Best practice: secure console with login + exec-timeout, disable aux line entirely, and limit vty to SSH only with an access-class to a trusted subnet.

front.text: An AAA method list is configured as 'group ISE_GROUP local'. The TACACS+ server is unreachable. What happens?

items: AAA tries the first method (group ISE_GROUP) first. If the TACACS+ server is unreachable (network failure or no response), it moves on to the next method.
If the server actively REJECTS the user, AAA does NOT fall through to local — fallback only happens for 'no response', not for explicit rejection.
Local fallback then uses the local username database. This is why every device must have at least one well-known emergency local account.
Best practice: define the local fallback explicitly and audit it; avoid 'group ... none' patterns that allow no-auth as a last resort.
Always test AAA outage behavior before pushing to production!

front.text: What are SNMP hardening best practices on Cisco IOS?

items: Use SNMPv3 with authPriv (auth + encryption) — never v1/v2c in production if avoidable.
Restrict access with an ACL bound to the SNMP community/user (only NMS subnets).
Use views to expose only the OIDs you need (read-only by default).
Disable any unused community strings and remove default 'public' / 'private'.
Apply CoPP to limit SNMP rate to control plane.
Use a dedicated management VRF and source SNMP traffic from a stable loopback.

front.text: Why use 'logging rate-limit', and how do you configure it without losing critical messages?

items: logging rate-limit caps the number of log messages per second the router processes — prevents a flapping interface or chatty event from consuming CPU.
Use 'except severity X' to ensure higher-severity messages (errors, alerts, emergencies) are never rate-limited.
Default behavior is no rate limit, which is risky in production.

back.text: logging rate-limit 100 except 4

front.text: How do you view IP SLA results, recent statistics, and historical buckets?

back.text:
show ip sla configuration
show ip sla statistics
show ip sla statistics 10 details
show ip sla history 10 full
IP SLA can also export results via SNMP (CISCO-RTTMON-MIB) and to syslog or EEM, useful for proactive alerting on SLA breaches.

front.text: Outline the right way to handle errors and retries in a Python REST API client.

items: Always check the HTTP status code; treat 2xx as success, 4xx as client error (don't retry blindly), 5xx as transient (retry with backoff).
Honor Retry-After on 429/503 responses.
Implement exponential backoff with jitter for 5xx and timeouts.
Limit max retries to avoid infinite loops.
Validate the JSON body with try/except — never assume a successful 200 always has the expected schema.
Log enough context (URL, method, status, response body excerpt) without leaking secrets like tokens.

front.text: Show a Python pattern that calls a REST API with proper exception handling using requests.
Name three common YAML pitfalls that cause Ansible playbooks or config files to misbehave.

items: Tabs vs spaces — YAML requires spaces only. A single tab character anywhere usually breaks the parser.
Boolean coercion — Strings like 'yes', 'no', 'on', 'off', 'true' become booleans automatically. Quote them ('yes') if you really mean strings.
Indentation drift — Mixed indentation levels can silently change the structure (an item being under the wrong key) without throwing an error.
Octal/numeric coercion — Leading zeros (e.g., 0123) can be parsed as octal or stripped — quote IDs and version numbers.
Multi-doc files — '---' separates YAML documents; missing it can merge what you intended as separate docs.

front.text: What's the difference between 'git merge' and 'git rebase', and when would you use each?

items: git merge — Combines two branches by creating a new merge commit. Preserves the actual chronological history but can produce a messy graph.
git rebase — Replays your commits on top of another branch as if they were authored after the latest changes. Produces a clean, linear history.
Use merge for: integrating long-lived branches, public/shared branches where rewriting history would break collaborators.
Use rebase for: keeping a feature branch up to date with main BEFORE merging, or cleaning up local commit history.
Golden rule: never rebase commits that have already been pushed and shared with others.

front.text: What are Ansible handlers, and why are they useful for network playbooks?

items: Handlers are special tasks that only run when 'notified' by another task that reported a change.
Each handler is run only once at the end of the play, even if notified by many tasks.
Use case: tasks that modify configs (loopbacks, ACLs, routing) all notify a 'save config' handler that runs once at the end of the play.
Avoids repeated 'wr mem' / 'commit' operations during a long playbook.

back.text:
tasks:
  - name: Configure interface
    cisco.ios.ios_config:
      lines:
        - description Uplink
      parents: interface GigabitEthernet0/1
    notify: save config

handlers:
  - name: save config
    cisco.ios.ios_command:
      commands:
        - write memory

front.text: What is Ansible Vault, and what kinds of data should you put in it?

items: Ansible Vault encrypts files (or specific values) at rest using AES-256 with a password or a vault key file.
Used to safely commit secrets to source control: device passwords, RADIUS keys, API tokens, certificate keys.
Common patterns: encrypt entire group_vars/all/secrets.yml, or use single-line vault strings inside a regular YAML file.
Run playbooks with --ask-vault-pass or use a vault password file in CI/CD.

back.text:
ansible-vault encrypt group_vars/all/secrets.yml
ansible-vault edit group_vars/all/secrets.yml
ansible-playbook site.yml --ask-vault-pass

front.text: What is the Cisco Secure Network Analytics 'Data Store' architecture, and why was it introduced?

items: Data Store — Distributed storage cluster that decouples flow storage from the Manager and Flow Collector.
Allows much higher flow ingestion rates and longer flow retention than legacy 'flat' deployments.
Lets organizations scale by adding Data Nodes rather than adding more standalone Flow Collectors.
Improves search/query performance for forensic investigations.
Recommended for any large-scale Stealthwatch deployment today.

front.text: What is the typical highly-available ISE deployment, and how do PAN, MnT, and PSN nodes scale?

items: Two-node minimum: Primary Admin/MnT + Secondary Admin/MnT. Standard for small deployments; PAN/MnT/PSN can be combined.
Distributed: Separate Primary and Secondary PAN, two MnTs (active/standby), and multiple PSNs for scale.
PAN — Active/Standby. Only one is the configuration master at any time.
MnT — Active/Standby for log/report aggregation.
PSN — Active/Active. Add more PSNs to scale RADIUS/TACACS auth volume; load-balance via a load balancer or by configuring multiple servers on the network device.
pxGrid — Pub/sub integration; deployed on dedicated nodes in larger deployments.

front.text: Compare common 802.1X EAP methods: EAP-TLS, PEAP-MSCHAPv2, EAP-FAST, EAP-TTLS.

rows: Strongest; mutual cert auth. Best for managed devices with PKI.
Tunnel built with server cert; user authenticates with username/password (AD-friendly).
Cisco-developed; uses Protected Access Credentials (PACs) to bootstrap trust without PKI everywhere.
Similar to PEAP; supports legacy inner methods like PAP. Less common in Cisco shops.

front.text: What are MAB and WebAuth, and how do they relate to 802.1X?

items: MAB (MAC Authentication Bypass) — Used for endpoints that can't run an 802.1X supplicant (printers, IoT). The switch sends the device's MAC address as the credential to the AAA server.
WebAuth — Captive portal authentication. After bypass auth (or quasi-open access), the user opens a browser and is redirected to a login page; credentials are checked via RADIUS.
Common combined flow: 802.1X first, fall back to MAB on timeout, fall back to WebAuth as last resort. Each fallback gets a different VLAN/SGT and different policy.
Cisco ISE orchestrates all three methods on a single port via a 'flexible authentication' policy.

front.text: What is the 'class-default' class in MQC, and what default actions apply to it on an output policy?

items: class-default catches all traffic that doesn't match any user-defined class.
By default, class-default uses FIFO and gets whatever bandwidth is left after all other classes' guarantees are satisfied.
Common practice on output policies: enable 'fair-queue' (WFQ) inside class-default for fairness among non-priority flows, plus 'random-detect' (WRED) to avoid tail drop.
If class-default has no shape/police, in some platforms it inherits the parent shape rate.

back.text:
policy-map WAN_OUT
 class VOICE
  priority percent 30
 class class-default
  fair-queue
  random-detect

front.text: What is SAE in WPA3, and what attack does it mitigate?

items: SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) replaces the WPA2 4-way handshake's PSK exchange.
Uses a Dragonfly key-exchange (mutual challenge-response). Each session derives a fresh PMK with forward secrecy.
Mitigates offline dictionary attacks: capturing the handshake doesn't help an attacker since they can't brute-force the password offline.
Provides forward secrecy — even if the password is later compromised, past sessions can't be decrypted.
WPA3-Personal uses SAE; WPA3-Enterprise uses 802.1X with stronger ciphers (192-bit GCMP-256 mode).

front.text: Why must OSPF stub-area flags match between neighbors, and which bit in the Hello packet conveys this?

items: OSPF Hello packets include an Options field. The 'E' bit (External) indicates whether external (Type 5) LSAs are allowed in the area.
Stub areas have E=0; standard areas have E=1.
If two neighbors disagree on the E bit (one configured 'area X stub', the other isn't), the adjacency stuck at INIT — no FULL state.
Other bits include 'N' (NSSA), 'DC' (demand circuit), and 'O' (opaque LSA). Mismatches in N/E commonly break adjacency.

front.text: OSPF cost is set to 100 on R1's interface but 10 on R2's interface for the same point-to-point link. Why does this matter, and what does it cause?

items: OSPF cost is per interface and per direction. Each router uses its own outbound interface cost when calculating SPF.
Asymmetric costs cause asymmetric routing — traffic from R1→R2 takes a different path than R2→R1 if other paths exist.
This can break stateful devices in the path (firewalls, NATs, IPS) that need to see both directions of a flow.
Fix: ensure both ends of the link use the same cost, OR ensure asymmetric routing is acceptable (no stateful devices).

front.text: What is an EIGRP Stuck-In-Active (SIA) condition, and what mechanisms help prevent it?

items: When DUAL goes Active for a route (no FS available), the router sends Queries and waits for Replies. If a neighbor doesn't reply within the SIA timer, the route becomes Stuck-In-Active.
SIA causes the originating router to reset the neighbor relationship — potentially cascading and destabilizing the network.
Prevention: use stub routers on spokes (stubs are not queried), summarize aggressively at hubs (limits query scope), and ensure timely SIA Reply mechanism is enabled (default in modern IOS).
Show with: show ip eigrp topology active to identify routes currently in Active state.

front.text: List the final BGP best-path tiebreakers after IGP metric to next hop (steps 9–13).

items: 9. Prefer the path that has been in BGP longest (oldest path) — provides stability.
10. Prefer the path with the lowest BGP Router ID.
11. Prefer the path with the shortest CLUSTER_LIST length (route reflector hop count).
12. Prefer the path with the lowest neighbor IP address (the BGP peer that sent it).
13. Multipath consideration — if equal, install both/all (when 'maximum-paths' is configured).

back.text: Steps 1–8 covered earlier: Weight, LOCAL_PREF, Originated, AS_PATH, Origin, MED, eBGP/iBGP, IGP metric.

front.text: What's the difference between 'ebgp-multihop' and 'disable-connected-check' in eBGP?

items: ebgp-multihop — Increases the TTL of eBGP packets above 1, allowing peers that aren't directly connected (e.g., loopback-to-loopback through a router).
disable-connected-check — Disables BGP's safety check that the neighbor IP is on a directly connected subnet. Useful when peering loopback-to-loopback between directly connected routers.
If both peers are directly connected and you peer loopback-to-loopback over a single hop: 'disable-connected-check' is sufficient (no need for multihop).
If peers are multiple IP hops apart: 'ebgp-multihop' is required.

front.text: When would you use 'neighbor X allowas-in' on a BGP session, and what risk does it introduce?

items: By default, BGP rejects routes whose AS_PATH already contains the local AS (loop prevention).
allowas-in tells BGP to accept up to N occurrences of the local AS in the path.
Use case: hub-and-spoke MPLS L3VPN where all sites share the same customer AS — without allowas-in, the hub would drop spoke routes that have already passed through the AS.
Risk: relaxes loop prevention. Combine with proper route filtering and tags (SoO — Site of Origin) to prevent actual loops.

back.text:
router bgp 65001
 address-family ipv4 vrf CUSTOMER_A
  neighbor 10.1.1.2 allowas-in 1

front.text: What does the BGP Route Refresh capability provide, and why is it preferred over 'soft-reconfiguration inbound'?

items: Route Refresh — Negotiated capability that lets a router request a peer to resend all its routes. No memory cost on the local router.
soft-reconfiguration inbound — Stores all received routes in memory before applying inbound policy, so re-applying policy doesn't require asking the peer again. Costs significant memory.
Route Refresh is automatically enabled and used on modern Cisco IOS. Verify capabilities with: show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors X.X.X.X.
Best practice: rely on Route Refresh; avoid soft-reconfiguration unless specifically needed for legacy peers.

front.text: What is BPDU skew detection, and what does it warn about?

items: BPDU skew = the relayed BPDU is delayed beyond the expected timing. Suggests CPU congestion on a switch in the STP path.
Cisco logs a syslog message when skew is detected — gives early warning of overloaded switches.
Doesn't take corrective action; it's purely diagnostic.
Triggered by: software-switched STP, CPU saturation due to broadcast/multicast storms, attacks, or undersized control planes.
Enabled with: spanning-tree bpdu skew-detection.

front.text: Why and how do you change to STP path-cost method 'long', and what's the impact?

back.text: spanning-tree pathcost method long

items: Default 'short' method assigns 4 to 1 Gbps and 2 to 10 Gbps — collapses 1G/10G/40G/100G into too few values.
Long method (32-bit costs) gives 20,000 / 2,000 / 200 / 20 / 2 for 1/10/40/100/400 Gbps — meaningful differentiation.
Important: must enable on ALL switches in the L2 domain or you get inconsistent path selection.
Recommended on any modern campus with 10G+ uplinks.

front.text: Show a basic Private VLAN configuration with one isolated VLAN and one promiscuous port.

back.text:
vlan 100
 private-vlan primary
 private-vlan association 101
!
vlan 101
 private-vlan isolated
!
! Promiscuous port (e.g., default gateway)
interface GigabitEthernet0/24
 switchport mode private-vlan promiscuous
 switchport private-vlan mapping 100 101
!
! Host (isolated) port
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 switchport mode private-vlan host
 switchport private-vlan host-association 100 101

front.text: Show a MACsec configuration using MKA with a static pre-shared CKN/CAK.

back.text:
key chain MKA_KEYCHAIN macsec
 key 1234ABCD
  cryptographic-algorithm aes-256-cmac
  key-string 0123456789ABCDEF0123456789ABCDEF0123456789ABCDEF0123456789ABCDEF
!
mka policy MACSEC_POLICY
 key-server priority 1
 macsec-cipher-suite gcm-aes-256
!
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
 macsec network-link
 mka policy MACSEC_POLICY
 mka pre-shared-key key-chain MKA_KEYCHAIN
Both ends must agree on CKN, CAK, cipher suite, and confidentiality offset. Verify with: show mka sessions, show macsec interface.

front.text: Outline a step-by-step approach to troubleshoot an IPsec site-to-site tunnel that won't pass traffic.

items: Verify IP reachability between peers — ping the peer's tunnel endpoint IP. Look for routing/NAT/firewall blocking UDP/500 and UDP/4500.
Verify Phase 1 with 'show crypto isakmp sa'. Should be 'QM_IDLE'. If not, check ISAKMP policy and PSK match.
Verify Phase 2 with 'show crypto ipsec sa'. Look for SAs in both directions.
Check encaps/decaps counters — are they incrementing? If only encaps, return path is broken.
Verify interesting traffic ACL (proxy IDs) matches identically on both sides.
Check that real traffic actually matches the crypto map ACL — debug with 'show access-list' counters.
Verify NAT exemption — site-to-site traffic should NOT be translated (use a route-map or NAT exemption ACL).
If using VTI: confirm tunnel interface is up/up and routing is forwarding traffic into Tunnel0.

front.text: Compare crypto-map-based IPsec and VTI-based IPsec, and explain why VTI is preferred today.

rows: No tunnel interface; can't run dynamic routing protocols natively
Dynamic routing (OSPF, BGP, EIGRP) and multicast supported
More complex; ACL + crypto map + apply to outside int
Simpler; configure tunnel interface + IPsec profile

front.text: What is the difference between sVTI (Static VTI) and dVTI (Dynamic VTI)?

items: sVTI — Pre-configured Tunnel interface for a specific peer. One Tunnel interface per peer. Used for fixed site-to-site VPNs.
dVTI — Virtual-Template-based; a single template spawns Virtual-Access tunnel interfaces dynamically when peers connect. Used on hubs serving many remote spokes (e.g., FlexVPN servers, RA hubs).
FlexVPN hubs typically use a single dVTI to serve thousands of clients/spokes.
Routing per-spoke is typically handled by IKEv2 routing, not by static routes per Virtual-Access interface.

front.text: A static route inside a VRF points to a next-hop IP that's reachable only in the global table. What happens, and how do you make it work?

items: By default, recursive lookup for a static route happens IN THE SAME VRF. If the next-hop isn't in that VRF's RIB, the route is invalid.
Fix: specify 'global' on the static route to tell the router to do the recursive next-hop lookup in the global table.
Common pattern: Internet edge with a public WAN in global, and customer traffic in a VRF — VRF default route points to a global next-hop (e.g., the ISP gateway).
Verify: show ip route vrf X — the route should be installed and resolved.

back.text: ip route vrf CUSTOMER_A 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1 global

front.text: What is a LISP Instance ID (IID), and how does SD-Access use it?

items: IID — A 24-bit identifier carried in the LISP data-plane header that supports virtualization (multi-tenant separation).
Different VRFs (Virtual Networks) map to different IIDs — provides EID address overlap isolation.
SD-Access uses one IID per VN, equivalent to one VRF's worth of EID-to-RLOC state.
Mapping registrations are stored per-IID in the Map Server, so two different VNs can have the same EID without collision.

front.text: How much extra overhead does VXLAN add per packet, and what does that imply for the underlay MTU?

items: VXLAN overhead per packet:
• 8 bytes VXLAN header
• 8 bytes UDP header
• 20 bytes outer IPv4 header
• 14 bytes outer Ethernet header
• Total ≈ 50 bytes (without options)
If endpoints expect a 1500-byte L3 MTU, the underlay must support ~1550 bytes — best practice is to set underlay MTU to 9216 (jumbo) so VXLAN overhead and any QinQ/MPLS labels fit safely.
Mismatched underlay MTU is a top VXLAN troubleshooting issue.

front.text: How does SD-Access integrate wireless, and what's different about 'fabric-enabled wireless' vs centralized CAPWAP?

items: Centralized CAPWAP — All wireless data tunneled to the WLC, hairpinning through it.

Fabric-enabled wireless — APs (joined to the fabric WLC) tunnel only CAPWAP CONTROL traffic to the WLC. CLIENT DATA traffic is VXLAN-encapsulated by the AP/edge into the fabric directly, just like wired traffic.
Result: distributed forwarding (no WLC bottleneck), unified policy/segmentation across wired and wireless (same VN/SGT model), better scale.
Requires fabric-capable APs (Wi-Fi 5 Wave 2 / Wi-Fi 6) and a fabric-capable WLC (9800 series).

front.text: What is an SD-Access 'extended node', and how is it different from a regular fabric edge?

items: Extended node — A switch (often Catalyst Industrial / Catalyst 9200) connected to a fabric edge but NOT a fabric edge itself. It extends fabric services (VLANs, SGTs) to additional ports without running LISP/VXLAN.
Useful for IoT, OT/industrial, or low-port-density closets where deploying a full fabric edge isn't justified.
Configured via 802.1Q trunking and policy enforcement at the upstream fabric edge.
Policy Extended Node — Variant that supports SGT inline tagging end-to-end into the extended node, enabling micro-segmentation closer to IoT endpoints.

front.text: How does Zone-Based Firewall (ZBF) work in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN, and what zones are typical?

items: ZBF on cEdge devices provides L3-L7 inspection between zones with stateful firewall policies.
Typical zones: User VPN(s), Guest VPN, DMZ, Transport (VPN 0), and Self (the device itself).
Policies are configured centrally on vManage as security policy and pushed to edges.
Combined with App-aware routing, SIG (Umbrella), URL filtering, and IPS, edges can act as a unified security service in the branch.

front.text: What is a TLOC Extension in Cisco SD-WAN, and when do you use it?

items: TLOC Extension lets two edges in the same site share each other's WAN transports.

Use case: Edge1 is plugged into the MPLS circuit, Edge2 is plugged into the Internet circuit. Each edge can borrow the other's transport via a TLOC extension link, providing redundancy without dual-circuiting each device.
Configured by extending the transport interface to the peer over a dedicated L2/L3 link with a special TLOC-extension config.
Also used to provide an active/active edge pair where one of the transports fails to a single edge.

front.text: Decode this syslog line: '*Mar 15 10:00:23.456: %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet0/1, changed state to up'.

items: Timestamp: *Mar 15 10:00:23.456 — date and time (the asterisk means the clock isn't yet synchronized to NTP).
Facility: %LINK — the IOS subsystem generating the message (link/interface).
Severity: 3 — Error (using the 0–7 syslog severity scale).
Mnemonic: UPDOWN — short identifier for this specific event type.
Message text: 'Interface GigabitEthernet0/1, changed state to up'.

front.text: After replacing a switch with a new device of the same model, the NMS can no longer poll SNMPv3 even though the same user/passwords are configured. Why?

items: Cause: SNMPv3 keys are localized to the device's Engine ID, which is derived from the device's identity and changes with the new chassis.
Even if the username/passwords are identical in the config, the localized keys differ from what the NMS computed for the old device.
Fix 1: Recreate the SNMPv3 user on the new device after deletion to regenerate localized keys, then re-register the device on the NMS so it learns the new Engine ID.
Fix 2: Manually configure the same Engine ID on the new device to match the old (advanced/uncommon).
Verify with: show snmp engineID.

front.text: Show an IOS-XE configuration for an on-change telemetry subscription that streams interface oper-status changes.

back.text:
telemetry ietf subscription 200
 encoding encode-kvgpb
 filter xpath /interfaces-ios-xe-oper:interfaces/interface/oper-status
 stream yang-push
 update-policy on-change
 receiver ip address 10.10.10.50 57500 protocol grpc-tcp
On-change subscriptions only emit data when the watched value changes — far more efficient than polling for state-style data.

front.text: Show how to configure an IP SLA TCP Connect probe to a web server on TCP/443 every minute.

back.text:
ip sla 50
 tcp-connect 10.20.30.40 443 source-ip 10.10.10.1
 frequency 60
ip sla schedule 50 life forever start-time now
!
track 50 ip sla 50 reachability
Useful for proactively detecting application-layer reachability issues that ICMP echo wouldn't catch (e.g., a web server is up but TCP/443 is dropping).

front.text: What are the three parts of a JWT, and how is the token structured?

items: Format: header.payload.signature — three Base64URL-encoded parts joined by dots.
Header — JSON describing the type (JWT) and signing algorithm (e.g., HS256, RS256).
Payload — JSON of 'claims' such as 'sub' (subject), 'exp' (expiration), 'iat' (issued at), plus any custom data (roles, tenant).
Signature — HMAC or RSA signature over Base64URL(header) + '.' + Base64URL(payload), using the issuer's secret/key.
Important: payload is BASE64-encoded, not encrypted. Don't put secrets in JWT claims; use HTTPS in transit.

front.text: What are the main sections of an OpenAPI specification document?

items: info — Title, version, description, contact info.
servers — Base URLs (production, sandbox, etc.).
paths — Endpoints, methods, parameters, request bodies, responses.
components.schemas — Reusable JSON object definitions referenced from paths.
components.securitySchemes — Defines authentication (Bearer, Basic, OAuth flows).
tags — Logical groupings of endpoints for documentation.

front.text: Match these YANG node types: container, list, leaf, leaf-list, choice.

rows: Groups related nodes; like a JSON object. No data of its own.
Ordered/keyed collection (e.g., a list of interfaces); like a JSON array of objects.
A single data value of a specific type (string, int, boolean, identityref, etc.).
A list of values of the same type (e.g., a list of DNS servers).
Mutually exclusive set of cases — only one branch may have data at a time.

front.text: What is XPath used for in NETCONF/YANG, and give an example?

items: XPath is a query language used to address specific nodes inside a YANG/XML tree.
Used in NETCONF filter selection, YANG must/when constraints, and telemetry subscriptions.
Example: /interfaces/interface[name='GigabitEthernet0/0']/oper-status — selects oper-status of a specific interface.
Subscriptions use XPath to define exactly what subtree is being watched (e.g., counters, state).

front.text: After 'git pull' you see a merge conflict. Outline how to resolve it.

items: Run 'git status' to see which files have conflicts.
Open each conflicted file. Conflicts are marked with '<<<<<<<', '=======', '>>>>>>>'.
Decide which side wins, or combine both — manually edit the file to the desired final state.
Remove the conflict markers entirely.
Run 'git add <file>' to mark each conflict resolved.
Continue: 'git commit' (for merge) or 'git rebase --continue' (for rebase).

front.text: Show an Ansible task that loops over a list of VLANs and uses 'when' to skip if a VLAN is below 10.
Show a Jinja2 template that renders a Cisco interface block from a list of interface dictionaries.

back.text: Jinja2 powers Ansible templates and Catalyst Center config templates. Use {% %} for control flow and {{ }} for variable substitution.

front.text: Compare TrustSec and MACsec — what does each provide, and how do they complement each other?

items: TrustSec — Identity-based segmentation using SGTs and SGACLs. Operates as a policy framework on top of L2/L3 forwarding.
MACsec — L2 hop-by-hop link encryption (802.1AE). Provides confidentiality, integrity, and origin authentication on a single link.
They solve different problems: TrustSec controls what flows are allowed; MACsec encrypts the wire so even if someone taps the cable, the data is unreadable.
Both are managed by ISE in a typical Cisco deployment, often deployed together for defense in depth.

front.text: What is ISE Profiling, and what data sources does it use?

items: Profiling fingerprints endpoints by their behavior on the network and matches them to a profile (Apple iPad, Cisco IP Phone, Windows 10, etc.).
Used to apply role-based policy automatically — no manual asset list required.

Common probes/data sources: DHCP fingerprints (Option 55), RADIUS attributes (Calling-Station-ID, OUI), HTTP User-Agent (via web auth), CDP/LLDP, SNMP query of access switch, NMAP scans, Active Directory.
Combined with 802.1X/MAB to enforce policy: e.g., printers go to Printer SGT, IoT cameras get a different VLAN.

front.text: What are ISE policy sets, and how do they differ from older monolithic policy?

items: Policy Sets group authentication and authorization rules into modular blocks; each block is gated by a top-level condition (e.g., 'TACACS+', 'Wired 802.1X', 'Wireless Guest').
Modular structure scales better than a single flat list — easier to read, maintain, and troubleshoot.
Each policy set can have its own identity sources, authentication rules, authorization profiles, and exception rules.
Default policy set acts as the catch-all if no specific set matches.
Best practice: separate policy sets per network access type (wired 802.1X, wired MAB, wireless corp, wireless guest, TACACS+ device admin).

front.text: How do you configure an AP to prefer a primary WLC, with secondary and tertiary fallback?

items: Each AP can be configured (via WLC GUI/CLI or DHCP/PnP) with up to three WLCs: primary, secondary, tertiary.
If the AP loses its current WLC, it tries Primary → Secondary → Tertiary in order before falling back to dynamic discovery.
Combined with WLC SSO (high availability) for sub-second failover within an HA pair, plus inter-WLC fallback for full-WLC failure.
Configured per AP from the WLC: ap name <AP> primary-base <WLC-name> <WLC-mgmt-IP>.

back.text:
ap name AP-1 primary-base WLC-A 10.1.1.10
ap name AP-1 secondary-base WLC-B 10.1.1.11
ap name AP-1 tertiary-base WLC-C 10.1.1.12

front.text: Show the high-level configuration intent for a WPA3-Enterprise SSID on a Catalyst 9800 WLC.

back.text:
wlan CORP_WLAN 1 CORP
 no security wpa wpa2
 security wpa wpa3
 security wpa psk set-key ascii 0 ! (no PSK on enterprise)
 security wpa akm dot1x-sha256
 security pmf mandatory
 security dot1x authentication-list ISE
 no shutdown
WPA3-Enterprise mandates 802.11w (PMF) and uses AES-CCMP-128 or stronger ciphers. Pair with EAP-TLS for the strongest security.

front.text: List Cisco's QoS best practices for an enterprise network using DiffServ.

items: Classify and mark traffic as close to the source as possible (access edge), so internal devices can trust DSCP.
Use a consistent DSCP scheme network-wide (e.g., RFC 4594-aligned: EF=voice, AF41=video, AF31=multimedia streaming, CS6=routing).
Apply LLQ for real-time traffic (voice, video) with appropriate policer (LLQ has built-in policing).
Use CBWFQ for non-real-time classes; class-default with WFQ + WRED.
Enable QoS pre-classify on tunnel interfaces.
On WAN links, shape to the contracted rate (HQoS) before the SP enforces it.
Keep policies simple — fewer classes are easier to operate and troubleshoot.

front.text: Why does tail drop cause TCP global synchronization, and how does WRED prevent it?

items: Tail drop — When the queue is full, every new packet is dropped. Many TCP flows experience drops at the same time, all back off simultaneously, then ramp up together (synchronization). Causes large utilization swings and poor link utilization.
WRED — Drops packets randomly as queue depth approaches full, hitting different flows at different times. Spreads out TCP backoffs so flows don't synchronize.
Result: smoother queue depth, better average utilization, less jitter for sensitive traffic.
DSCP-based WRED is preferred — different DSCP classes have different drop profiles.

front.text: What PoE standards are needed to fully power Cisco Wi-Fi 6 / Wi-Fi 6E APs, and what happens with insufficient power?

rows: Modern Wi-Fi 6E APs at full performance
Tri-radio + USB + IoT APs at full performance

back.text: Insufficient PoE causes APs to disable radios, drop tx power, or run only one radio — major performance impact. Verify with: show power inline (switch) and AP power status on WLC.

front.text: Why is IPv6 Routing Header Type 0 (RH0) deprecated, and what should you do about it?

items: RH0 allowed source-routing in IPv6 — a sender could specify a list of intermediate hops the packet had to traverse.
Attackers could use RH0 to bypass ACLs and amplify traffic between two hosts (ping-pong amplification).
RFC 5095 deprecated RH0 in 2007; modern OSes ignore it.
Best practice: explicitly drop IPv6 packets with RH0 at the network edge using ACLs (deny ipv6 any any routing-type 0 log).

front.text: Show how to configure a SISF device-tracking policy and apply it to a VLAN.

back.text:
device-tracking policy DT_POLICY
 tracking enable
!
! Apply on the VLAN
