vlan configuration 10,20,30
 device-tracking attach-policy DT_POLICY
Modern IOS-XE has replaced legacy IPDT with SISF (Switch Integrated Security Features). Many features (DAI, IPv6 FHS, 802.1X) require an attached device-tracking policy to function.

front.text: Show a basic IOS Zone-Based Firewall (ZBF) configuration that allows TCP/UDP/ICMP traffic from inside to outside.

back.text:
zone security INSIDE
zone security OUTSIDE
!
class-map type inspect match-any IN_TO_OUT_CM
 match protocol tcp
 match protocol udp
 match protocol icmp
!
policy-map type inspect IN_TO_OUT_PM
 class type inspect IN_TO_OUT_CM
  inspect
 class class-default
  drop
!
zone-pair security INSIDE_OUTSIDE source INSIDE destination OUTSIDE
 service-policy type inspect IN_TO_OUT_PM
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 zone-member security INSIDE
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 zone-member security OUTSIDE

front.text: What are the current recommended IPsec/IKEv2 cipher choices for new deployments?

items: Encryption: AES-GCM-256 (preferred) or AES-CBC-256 with SHA-256 HMAC.
Hash/PRF: SHA-256 or higher; avoid SHA-1 and MD5.
DH groups: 14 (2048-bit MODP) minimum; prefer ECDH groups 19 (P-256), 20 (P-384), or 21 (P-521).
Authentication: RSA certificates (2048+ bits) or ECDSA; PSK only if certs aren't feasible.
Avoid: DES, 3DES, MD5, SHA-1, DH groups 1/2/5, IKEv1 main mode where IKEv2 is supported.

front.text: What are the typical IKEv2 SA and IPsec SA lifetimes, and why are they different?

items: IKEv2 SA (control SA, Phase 1) — Long lifetime; default ~24 hours (86400 s). Used for negotiating child SAs.
IPsec SA (data SA, Phase 2) — Shorter lifetime; default ~1 hour (3600 s) and/or volume-based (e.g., 4.6 GB).
Rekey limits the amount of data protected by a single key, reducing exposure if a key is ever compromised.
Lifetimes can be time-based, volume-based, or both — whichever expires first triggers rekey.
PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy) at rekey ensures each new IPsec SA gets fresh DH keying material.

front.text: What HTTP headers should a hardened REST API set on responses?

items: Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) — Forces HTTPS for future requests.
Content-Security-Policy (CSP) — Restricts what the browser can load (defense in depth for any HTML responses).
X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff — Prevents MIME-type sniffing.
X-Frame-Options: DENY — Prevents clickjacking via iframes.
Cache-Control: no-store (for sensitive data) — Prevents caching of secrets.
Expose only minimal data in error responses — avoid leaking stack traces and internal paths.

front.text: What is an Ansible Collection, and how do you install and reference one?

items: A Collection is a versioned bundle of modules, plugins, roles, and docs published on Ansible Galaxy or a private repo.
Replaces the older monolithic 'ansible' install where every module shipped together.
Install: 'ansible-galaxy collection install cisco.ios'.
Reference modules using their FQCN (Fully Qualified Collection Name): cisco.ios.ios_config, cisco.dnac.network_device_info, cisco.iosxr.iosxr_facts.
Pin versions in requirements.yml for reproducible CI builds.

front.text: What is a Python context manager, and why use 'with ConnectHandler(...) as conn:' instead of just calling ConnectHandler?

items: A context manager guarantees cleanup (file close, connection disconnect) even if exceptions are raised mid-block.
'with' invokes __enter__ on entry and __exit__ on exit (including exception cases).
Netmiko's ConnectHandler supports the context manager protocol — exiting the 'with' block disconnects the SSH session cleanly.
Without 'with', forgetting conn.disconnect() can leak SSH sessions on the device, eventually exhausting VTY lines.

back.text:
with ConnectHandler(**device) as conn:
    output = conn.send_command("show ip interface brief")
    print(output)
# Connection auto-closed here, even on exception

front.text: Name three common ways REST APIs are versioned, and which is most common at Cisco.

items: URI versioning — Version is in the path: /api/v1/devices, /api/v2/devices. Most common; used by Catalyst Center (/dna/intent/api/v1/...) and many Cisco products.
Header versioning — Custom request header (e.g., 'Accept-Version: 2') or content negotiation via Accept (e.g., 'application/vnd.cisco.dnac.v2+json').
Query parameter — ?version=2. Less common; can be cached incorrectly by intermediaries.
Best practice: pick one strategy per product, document deprecation timelines, and provide overlap windows so clients can migrate.

front.text: What does 'Network as Code' (Infrastructure as Code) mean, and what are its core principles?

items: Network state is expressed declaratively in version-controlled text files (YAML, JSON, Terraform).
Source of truth is a Git repo, not running configurations on devices.
Changes are reviewed via Pull Requests, gated by automated tests and approvals.
Deployment is automated via pipelines — no manual CLI typing in production.
Drift detection routinely compares device state to the source of truth and alerts/remediates differences.
Benefits: traceable change history, peer review, reproducible environments, faster recovery, fewer human errors.

front.text: Why is model-driven configuration via YANG/NETCONF preferable to CLI screen-scraping for automation?

rows: High — output formatting changes break parsers
No transactions — partial config can be applied
Errors only show after sending each command
Schema-validated before send; errors caught early

front.text: What is AIOps in the context of network operations, and what kinds of problems does it solve?

items: AIOps = AI for IT Operations. Applies machine learning and analytics to operational data to improve detection and response.
Common problems addressed: alert noise reduction, root cause inference across many correlated signals, anomaly detection on metrics, capacity prediction.
Cisco AIOps capabilities live in Catalyst Center (Assurance), Meraki Insight, ThousandEyes, and AppDynamics.
Ultimate goal: reduce MTTR by surfacing the few real issues out of huge telemetry volumes and recommending remediation.
